title: Rigorous Analysis of the 3D Ising Model Phase Transition

statement.canonical: The three-dimensional Ising model on the cubic lattice Z^3 with nearest-neighbor ferromagnetic coupling J > 0 and Hamiltonian H = -J sum_{<i,j>} sigma_i sigma_j (where sigma_i in {-1, +1}) is known to exhibit a phase transition at some critical inverse temperature beta_c in (0, infinity). While the existence of a phase transition (spontaneous magnetization for beta > beta_c) has been rigorously established, the following fundamental problems remain open: (1) prove that the critical exponents exist and take the values predicted by the renormalization group and conformal bootstrap (e.g., beta ~ 0.3265, gamma ~ 1.2372, nu ~ 0.6301); (2) prove conformal invariance of the scaling limit at criticality; (3) determine whether an exact closed-form solution exists analogous to Onsager's 2D solution.
statement.informal: The Ising model describes magnetic spins on a lattice that prefer to align with their neighbors. In two dimensions, Onsager found an exact solution in 1944. In three dimensions, we know a phase transition occurs (the material becomes magnetic below a critical temperature), but we cannot prove the precise mathematical nature of this transition. Can we rigorously compute the critical exponents, prove the scaling limit is conformally invariant, or find an exact solution?

why_it_matters: The 3D Ising model is the paradigmatic model of phase transitions and critical phenomena. Its critical exponents are among the most precisely measured quantities in physics (via Monte Carlo and conformal bootstrap), yet rigorous mathematical proofs remain elusive. A solution would represent a major breakthrough in mathematical physics and probability theory, with implications for understanding universality and renormalization group theory.

canonical.title: Crystal Statistics. I. A Two-Dimensional Model with an Order-Disorder Transition
Solving the 3d Ising Model with the Conformal Bootstrap II. c-Minimization and Precise Critical Exponents

status_evidence.title: 100 years of the (critical) Ising model on the hypercubic lattice

partial_results.summary: Onsager (1944) exactly solved the 2D Ising model, computing the free energy and proving a phase transition.
Peierls (1936) proved the existence of a phase transition in the Ising model in dimensions d >= 2.
Aizenman and Fernandez rigorously proved that the critical exponents of the Ising model in d >= 5 take their mean-field values.
The conformal bootstrap program numerically determined 3D Ising critical exponents to extraordinary precision: Delta_sigma = 0.5181489(10), Delta_epsilon = 1.412625(10).
Chelkak, Hongler, and Izyurov proved conformal invariance of the 2D Ising model at criticality.

machine_generated.summary: This problem asks for a rigorous mathematical proof of the critical exponents and conformal invariance of the 3D Ising model phase transition, extending Onsager's celebrated 2D solution to three dimensions.
decomposition: Rigorously establish the renormalization group fixed point and compute critical exponents in 3D
Prove conformal invariance of the scaling limit using conformal bootstrap or probabilistic methods

title: Additivity Conjecture for Quantum Channel Capacity
aliases: Additivity of Minimum Output Entropy Conjecture

statement.canonical: The additivity conjecture for quantum channel capacity, in its strongest form, asserted that the minimum output entropy S_min(Phi) = min_{rho} S(Phi(rho)) of a quantum channel Phi is additive under tensor products: S_min(Phi_1 tensor Phi_2) = S_min(Phi_1) + S_min(Phi_2) for all channels Phi_1, Phi_2. Hastings (2009) disproved this conjecture by showing the existence of counterexamples. However, the fundamental open problem remains: determine the classical capacity C(Phi) = lim_{n -> infinity} (1/n) chi(Phi^{tensor n}) of a general quantum channel Phi in a single-letter (computable) formula, i.e., whether the regularization in the HSW theorem can be removed for specific classes of channels, and characterize for which channels additivity of the Holevo capacity chi(Phi_1 tensor Phi_2) = chi(Phi_1) + chi(Phi_2) holds.
statement.informal: When we send classical information through a quantum channel, can we always compute the maximum transmission rate (classical capacity) from a single use of the channel, or do we need to consider entangled inputs across many channel uses? While the general additivity conjecture was disproved, determining the classical capacity of a general quantum channel and identifying which channels have additive capacity remains wide open.

why_it_matters: Understanding quantum channel capacity is central to quantum information theory and quantum communication. The resolution of the additivity question determines whether the classical capacity of a quantum channel is computable. Hastings' counterexample showed the problem is fundamentally harder than hoped, but a complete characterization of channel capacities remains one of the deepest open problems in quantum Shannon theory.

canonical.title: The capacity of the quantum channel with general signal states
Equivalence of Additivity Questions in Quantum Information Theory

status_evidence.title: Superadditivity of communication capacity using entangled inputs
Quantum Information Theory (2nd ed.)

partial_results.summary: King proved additivity for unital qubit channels and entanglement-breaking channels.

Shor showed equivalence of several additivity conjectures: additivity of Holevo capacity, additivity of minimum output entropy, strong superadditivity of entanglement of formation, and additivity of entanglement of formation.
Hastings disproved the general additivity conjecture by constructing random counterexamples for minimum output entropy.

machine_generated.summary: The additivity conjecture problem concerns whether the classical capacity of a quantum channel can be determined from a single use, or requires considering entangled inputs across multiple uses.
decomposition: Characterize the class of quantum channels for which Holevo capacity is additive
Develop computable bounds or single-letter formulas for the classical capacity of specific channel families

title: AGT Correspondence Proof

statement.canonical: Prove the AGT (Alday-Gaiotto-Tachikawa) correspondence: the Nekrasov instanton partition function of a 4D N=2 superconformal gauge theory of class S (obtained by compactifying the 6D (2,0) theory on a punctured Riemann surface C) equals the conformal block of 2D Toda conformal field theory on C. Specifically, prove that for SU(2) gauge theories, the Nekrasov partition function equals the Virasoro conformal block, and for SU(N) gauge theories, it equals the W_N algebra conformal block. Establish this at the mathematical level of rigor for all genera and numbers of punctures.
statement.informal: The AGT correspondence is a surprising duality between 4D gauge theory partition functions and 2D conformal field theory correlation functions. Despite overwhelming evidence, a mathematical proof is missing.

why_it_matters: The AGT correspondence is one of the most profound dualities in mathematical physics, connecting gauge theory, conformal field theory, integrable systems, and geometric representation theory. A proof would illuminate deep structures in all these areas.

canonical.title: Liouville Correlation Functions from Four-dimensional Gauge Theories

status_evidence.title: A brief review of the 2d/4d correspondences

partial_results.summary: The AGT correspondence has been verified in many cases using combinatorial identities and representation theory.
Schiffmann and Vasserot proved a mathematical version for the pure gauge theory case using geometric methods.
Maulik and Okounkov developed a mathematical framework using stable envelopes connecting to AGT.

machine_generated.summary: Proving the AGT correspondence between 4D gauge theory partition functions and 2D conformal blocks.
decomposition: Prove the correspondence for all class S theories
Establish the mathematical framework connecting instanton moduli spaces and conformal blocks

title: Anderson Metal-Insulator Transition

statement.canonical: Prove the existence of the Anderson metal-insulator transition for the Anderson model (tight-binding Hamiltonian with random on-site potential) on Z^d for d >= 3. Specifically, prove that there exists a critical disorder strength W_c such that for W < W_c the spectrum contains absolutely continuous component (extended states, metallic phase) and for W > W_c the spectrum is pure point with exponentially localized eigenstates (insulating phase). In d=3, determine the critical exponent nu for the divergence of the localization length xi ~ |W - W_c|^{-nu}.
statement.informal: In a crystal with random impurities, electrons can either spread freely (metal) or get trapped (insulator). Proving that this metal-insulator transition exists in 3D is one of the most important open problems in mathematical physics.

why_it_matters: Anderson localization is a fundamental phenomenon in condensed matter physics. Proving the existence of a metal-insulator transition in 3D would be a landmark result connecting probability theory, spectral theory, and physics.

canonical.title: Absence of Diffusion in Certain Random Lattices

status_evidence.title: Random Operators: Disorder Effects on Quantum Spectra and Dynamics

partial_results.summary: Froehlich and Spencer proved Anderson localization at large disorder (insulating phase) using multiscale analysis.
Aizenman and Molchanov proved localization using fractional moment methods.
The existence of absolutely continuous spectrum (extended states) at weak disorder in d >= 3 remains unproven.

machine_generated.summary: Proving the existence of the Anderson metal-insulator transition in three-dimensional disordered systems.
decomposition: Prove existence of absolutely continuous spectrum at weak disorder in d >= 3
Determine the critical disorder strength and critical exponent

title: Muon Anomalous Magnetic Moment Theory Prediction

statement.canonical: Determine the precise Standard Model prediction for the muon anomalous magnetic moment a_mu = (g-2)/2 to sufficient accuracy to compare with the experimental measurement. The dominant theoretical uncertainty comes from the hadronic vacuum polarization (HVP) contribution. Resolve the tension between the data-driven dispersive approach (using e+e- -> hadrons cross-section data) and lattice QCD calculations of the HVP contribution, and determine whether a_mu^{SM} agrees with the experimental value measured by Fermilab and BNL.
statement.informal: The muon's magnetic moment has been measured to exquisite precision and may disagree with the Standard Model prediction, hinting at new physics. But the theory prediction itself is uncertain because of difficulties computing the strong-force contributions. Resolving this is one of the most pressing problems in particle physics.

why_it_matters: The muon g-2 anomaly is one of the strongest hints of physics beyond the Standard Model. Resolving the theoretical prediction is essential to determine whether this discrepancy is real or an artifact of incomplete calculations.

canonical.title: Measurement of the Positive Muon Anomalous Magnetic Moment to 0.46 ppm

status_evidence.title: The anomalous magnetic moment of the muon in the Standard Model

partial_results.summary: The Muon g-2 Theory Initiative published a consensus Standard Model prediction showing ~4.2 sigma tension with experiment.
BMW lattice QCD collaboration computed HVP with a result closer to experiment, creating tension with data-driven methods.
CMD-3 experiment published new e+e- -> pi+pi- data that shifted the data-driven prediction toward the lattice result.

machine_generated.summary: Resolving the theoretical prediction for the muon anomalous magnetic moment, particularly the hadronic vacuum polarization contribution.
decomposition: Resolve the tension between dispersive and lattice QCD approaches to HVP
Improve hadronic light-by-light scattering calculations

title: BKL Conjecture on Singularity Structure

statement.canonical: Prove the BKL conjecture: generic spacelike singularities in solutions of the Einstein equations are oscillatory (Mixmaster-like), locally spatially homogeneous, and the dynamics at each spatial point near the singularity is well-approximated by a sequence of Kasner epochs connected by BKL maps (Bianchi type IX or type VIII dynamics). Specifically, prove that as the singularity is approached, spatial derivatives become negligible compared to time derivatives ("asymptotically velocity term dominated" or AVTD behavior is replaced by BKL oscillations for generic data).
statement.informal: What happens at the singularity inside a black hole or at the Big Bang? The BKL conjecture predicts a wild, chaotic oscillation of the geometry where each point in space evolves independently. Proving this is one of the hardest problems in GR.

why_it_matters: The BKL conjecture describes the generic structure of spacetime singularities in general relativity. Proving it would complete our understanding of singularity formation and has implications for quantum gravity, where knowledge of classical singularity structure is essential.

canonical.title: Oscillatory approach to a singular point in the relativistic cosmology

status_evidence.title: Numerical Approaches to Spacetime Singularities

partial_results.summary: Ringstrom proved the BKL conjecture for Bianchi type IX cosmologies (spatially homogeneous case).
Numerical simulations by Garfinkle and others strongly support BKL behavior for inhomogeneous cosmologies.
Damour, Henneaux, and Nicolai connected BKL dynamics to hyperbolic Kac-Moody algebras.

machine_generated.summary: Proving the BKL conjecture that generic spacetime singularities are oscillatory and locally homogeneous.
decomposition: Extend Ringstrom's proof from homogeneous to inhomogeneous spacetimes
Prove that spatial derivatives are asymptotically negligible near generic singularities

title: Classification of Topological Phases of Matter in 3D

statement.canonical: Provide a complete mathematical classification of gapped quantum phases of matter in three spatial dimensions. Specifically: (1) For symmetry-protected topological (SPT) phases in 3D with a given symmetry group G, prove that the classification is given by the generalized cohomology theory (e.g., group cohomology H^{d+1}(BG, U(1)) for bosonic SPT phases, or appropriate cobordism groups Omega^{d+1}_{Spin}(BG) for fermionic SPT phases), and determine whether these classifications are complete. (2) For intrinsic topological order in 3D (phases without symmetry protection), provide a complete classification: in 2D, topological orders are classified by modular tensor categories, but the 3D analog requires higher-categorical structures (e.g., modular 2-categories or fusion 2-categories). Prove that a specific mathematical framework (such as fully extended 3+1D topological quantum field theories, or equivalently 4-categories with appropriate duality structure) provides a complete classification. (3) Establish the bulk-boundary correspondence rigorously: prove that every 3D topological phase has a well-defined 2D boundary theory and that the boundary theory uniquely determines the bulk phase.
statement.informal: Matter can exist in exotic "topological phases" where the properties are determined not by symmetry breaking (like ordinary phases) but by global quantum entanglement patterns. In two dimensions, we have a fairly complete mathematical classification of these phases. The challenge is to achieve the same in three dimensions: what are all the possible topological phases of matter in 3D, and what mathematical structures describe them?

why_it_matters: The classification of topological phases is one of the central problems in modern condensed matter physics and mathematical physics, with direct applications to quantum computing (topological quantum computation), materials science (topological insulators and superconductors), and pure mathematics (higher category theory, TQFT). The 2016 Nobel Prize in Physics was awarded for theoretical discoveries of topological phase transitions. A complete 3D classification would have profound consequences for both physics and mathematics.

canonical.title: Symmetry protected topological orders and the group cohomology of their symmetry group
Anyons in an exactly solved model and beyond

status_evidence.title: Reflection positivity and invertible topological phases

partial_results.summary: The ten-fold way classification of free-fermion topological insulators and superconductors was completed by Kitaev, Schnyder, Ryu, Furusaki, and Ludwig using K-theory and random matrix theory.
Chen, Gu, Liu, and Wen proposed group cohomology classification of bosonic SPT phases, but it was shown to be incomplete: beyond-cohomology phases exist in 3D.
Freed and Hopkins classified invertible topological phases using Anderson-dual bordism, providing a complete classification for invertible (short-range entangled) phases.
Lan, Kong, and Wen proposed a classification of 3+1D topological orders based on fusion 2-categories, but completeness and mathematical rigor remain to be fully established.
Johnson-Freyd and collaborators established significant mathematical foundations using higher categories for the classification of topological phases.

machine_generated.summary: This problem seeks a complete mathematical classification of gapped topological phases of matter in three spatial dimensions, using higher-categorical structures and topological quantum field theory.
decomposition: Prove completeness of fusion 2-category or modular 2-category frameworks for classifying 3D topological orders
Rigorously establish the bulk-boundary correspondence and determine which 3D phases have beyond-cohomology descriptions

title: Confinement in Quantum Chromodynamics

statement.canonical: Prove that quantum chromodynamics (QCD), the SU(3) Yang-Mills gauge theory coupled to quarks, exhibits confinement: isolated quarks and gluons cannot exist as asymptotic particle states, and all physical states are color singlets (hadrons). Rigorously, prove that the Wilson loop expectation value satisfies an area law <W(C)> ~ exp(-sigma * Area(C)) for large loops C, where sigma > 0 is the string tension, or establish confinement through an equivalent criterion such as the existence of a linear quark-antiquark potential at large distances.
statement.informal: Why can't we ever see a single quark? QCD predicts that the force between quarks grows with distance, permanently confining them inside protons and neutrons. But no one has proven this mathematically from the fundamental theory.

why_it_matters: Confinement is one of the most important phenomena in fundamental physics. It explains why matter is made of hadrons rather than free quarks. A proof would be a major breakthrough in both mathematical physics and our understanding of the strong nuclear force.

canonical.title: Confinement of quarks

status_evidence.title: An Introduction to the Confinement Problem

partial_results.summary: Wilson formulated the lattice gauge theory framework and the area law criterion for confinement.
Lattice QCD simulations provide overwhelming numerical evidence for confinement with string tension consistent with experiments.
't Hooft and Mandelstam proposed the dual superconductor mechanism for confinement.

machine_generated.summary: Proving that QCD exhibits confinement, meaning isolated quarks and gluons cannot exist as free particles.
decomposition: Prove the area law for Wilson loops in SU(3) Yang-Mills theory
Establish the dual superconductor mechanism or an alternative confinement mechanism rigorously

title: Conformal Invariance at Criticality (General)

statement.canonical: Prove that critical lattice models in d=2 are conformally invariant in the scaling limit, for a general class of models beyond the Ising model. Specifically, prove conformal invariance for the critical q-state Potts model for general q (including non-integer q via the Fortuin-Kasteleyn representation), the O(n) loop model for general n, and the self-avoiding walk (n=0 case). In d >= 3, determine whether critical systems possess conformal invariance and characterize the conformal field theories that describe them.
statement.informal: At a phase transition, lattice systems become scale-invariant. A stronger property, conformal invariance, is widely assumed but proven only for the 2D Ising model and a few other cases. Proving it in general is a major challenge.

why_it_matters: Conformal invariance at criticality is the foundational assumption of conformal field theory, which has been spectacularly successful in both physics and mathematics. Proving it rigorously would justify this entire edifice from first principles.

canonical.title: Conformal symmetry of critical fluctuations

status_evidence.title: Conformal invariance in random cluster models

partial_results.summary: Smirnov proved conformal invariance of critical site percolation on the triangular lattice (Fields Medal 2010).
Chelkak, Hongler, and Izyurov proved conformal invariance of the 2D critical Ising model correlations.
Conformal invariance for the q-state Potts model with q != 2 remains open even in d=2.

machine_generated.summary: Proving conformal invariance at criticality for general lattice models in 2D and determining its role in d >= 3.
decomposition: Prove conformal invariance for the q-state Potts model at criticality
Determine whether conformal invariance holds at 3D critical points

title: Existence of a Consistent Theory of Quantum Gravity
aliases: Unification of General Relativity and Quantum Mechanics

statement.canonical: Does there exist a mathematically consistent, background-independent quantum theory that reduces to general relativity in the classical limit and to quantum field theory in the weak-gravity limit? More precisely, construct a quantum theory satisfying the following: (1) the theory possesses a well-defined Hilbert space (or suitable generalization) of states; (2) in the semiclassical limit (hbar -> 0 or G_N -> 0), the theory reproduces Einstein's field equations R_{mu nu} - (1/2) R g_{mu nu} + Lambda g_{mu nu} = (8 pi G_N / c^4) T_{mu nu}; (3) the theory is perturbatively or non-perturbatively finite (free of unrenormalizable divergences that plague naive quantization of general relativity); (4) the theory is unitary and consistent with the principles of quantum mechanics; (5) the theory resolves or circumvents the singularities of classical general relativity (black hole singularities, Big Bang singularity); and (6) the theory provides a consistent resolution of the black hole information paradox.
statement.informal: General relativity describes gravity as the curvature of spacetime, and quantum mechanics describes the behavior of particles at the smallest scales. Both theories are extraordinarily successful, but they are fundamentally incompatible: attempts to combine them using standard techniques produce nonsensical infinities. Does a consistent mathematical theory exist that unifies gravity with quantum mechanics?

why_it_matters: The construction of a consistent theory of quantum gravity is widely regarded as the most fundamental open problem in theoretical physics. It is essential for understanding the very early universe (Big Bang), the interior of black holes, and the ultimate structure of spacetime at the Planck scale (~10^{-35} meters). Candidate approaches include string theory, loop quantum gravity, causal set theory, and asymptotic safety, but none has achieved a complete, experimentally verified formulation.

canonical.title: Quantum Theory of Gravity. I. The Canonical Theory
String Theory, Volume 1: An Introduction to the Bosonic String

status_evidence.title: Quantum Gravity: a Progress Report

partial_results.summary: String theory provides a perturbatively finite framework for quantum gravity in 10 dimensions, with five consistent superstring theories unified under M-theory, but a complete non-perturbative formulation remains elusive.

The AdS/CFT correspondence (Maldacena, 1997) provides a non-perturbative definition of quantum gravity in anti-de Sitter space via a dual conformal field theory, but extension to de Sitter or flat space is not established.

Loop quantum gravity provides a background-independent quantization of general relativity with discrete area and volume spectra, but its semiclassical limit and connection to low-energy physics remain unproven.
Weinberg's asymptotic safety scenario suggests gravity may be non-perturbatively renormalizable at a UV fixed point; functional renormalization group studies provide evidence but no rigorous proof.

machine_generated.summary: This problem asks whether a mathematically consistent quantum theory of gravity exists that unifies general relativity and quantum mechanics while resolving singularities and the black hole information paradox.
decomposition: Establish a complete non-perturbative formulation of string theory or an alternative framework
Prove that a candidate theory reproduces general relativity in the classical limit and is free of divergences

title: Constructive Quantum Field Theory in 4D

statement.canonical: Construct a non-trivial (interacting) quantum field theory in four spacetime dimensions that satisfies the Wightman axioms or the Osterwalder-Schrader axioms. The theory should have a well-defined Hilbert space, field operators as operator-valued distributions, a unitary representation of the Poincare group, and satisfy locality, spectral condition, and clustering. No such construction exists for any interacting theory in d=4, though several exist in d=2 and d=3.
statement.informal: Despite the enormous success of quantum field theory in physics, no one has ever rigorously constructed a single interacting quantum field theory in the physical four-dimensional spacetime. Can this be done for any model?

why_it_matters: This is one of the most fundamental open problems in mathematical physics. The absence of any rigorous interacting QFT in 4D means we lack mathematical proof that the theoretical framework underlying the Standard Model of particle physics is self-consistent.

canonical.title: Quantum Physics: A Functional Integral Point of View

status_evidence.title: Constructive Field Theory and Applications

partial_results.summary: Glimm and Jaffe constructed phi^4_2 (interacting scalar field in 2D) rigorously.
Feldman, Magnen, Rivasseau, and Seneor constructed phi^4_3 using renormalization group methods.
Gubinelli, Imkeller, and Perkowski gave an alternative construction of phi^4_3 using paracontrolled distributions.

machine_generated.summary: Constructing a rigorous interacting quantum field theory in four spacetime dimensions satisfying the Wightman or Osterwalder-Schrader axioms.
decomposition: Extend constructive methods from d=3 to d=4
Control ultraviolet divergences non-perturbatively in 4D

title: Cosmic Censorship Conjecture

statement.canonical: The cosmic censorship conjecture, proposed by Roger Penrose in 1969, comes in two forms. The Weak Cosmic Censorship Conjecture (WCCC) states that for generic asymptotically flat initial data for the Einstein equations satisfying appropriate energy conditions, the maximal Cauchy development possesses a complete future null infinity (scri+); equivalently, singularities forming from gravitational collapse of generic initial data are hidden behind event horizons and cannot be seen by distant observers. The Strong Cosmic Censorship Conjecture (SCCC) states that for generic compact or asymptotically flat initial data, the maximal globally hyperbolic development is inextendible as a suitably regular Lorentzian manifold; equivalently, the solution to Einstein's equations is uniquely determined by initial data, and Cauchy horizons (beyond which determinism breaks down) are non-generic. Formally, for the vacuum Einstein equations Ric(g) = 0 with generic initial data (Sigma, h, K) satisfying the constraint equations, the maximal globally hyperbolic development (M, g) is inextendible as a C^0 Lorentzian manifold (Christodoulou's formulation).
statement.informal: When a massive star collapses under its own gravity, general relativity predicts that a singularity (a point of infinite density) forms. The cosmic censorship conjecture says that such singularities are always hidden inside black holes and can never be "naked" (visible to distant observers). The strong version additionally claims that the laws of physics remain deterministic everywhere outside singularities: the future is uniquely determined by initial conditions.

why_it_matters: Cosmic censorship is one of the most important open problems in general relativity and gravitational physics. If weak cosmic censorship fails, naked singularities would exist where the predictive power of general relativity breaks down observably. If strong cosmic censorship fails, determinism in general relativity would be violated. The conjecture is essential for the mathematical well-posedness of general relativity and for understanding the endpoint of gravitational collapse and black hole physics.

canonical.title: Gravitational collapse: the role of general relativity
On the global initial value problem and the issue of singularities

status_evidence.title: The mathematical analysis of black holes in general relativity

partial_results.summary: Christodoulou proved weak cosmic censorship for the spherically symmetric Einstein-scalar field system.

Dafermos and Luk proved that the Cauchy horizon of the Kerr black hole is C^0-extendible for the vacuum Einstein equations, showing that strong cosmic censorship in C^0 is false for Kerr, prompting reformulation in terms of Christoffel symbol regularity.
Dafermos proved strong cosmic censorship (C^0 inextendibility) for the spherically symmetric Einstein-Maxwell-scalar field system.
Kehle and Unger constructed a counterexample to weak cosmic censorship for the Einstein-Maxwell equations (charged black holes) without symmetry assumptions.

machine_generated.summary: The cosmic censorship conjecture asserts that singularities formed by gravitational collapse are generically hidden behind event horizons, preserving the predictive power of general relativity.
decomposition: Prove weak cosmic censorship for the vacuum Einstein equations with generic asymptotically flat initial data
Resolve the formulation of strong cosmic censorship in terms of appropriate regularity classes for Kerr-like spacetimes

title: Cosmic No-Hair Conjecture

statement.canonical: Prove the cosmic no-hair conjecture: all initially expanding cosmological solutions of the Einstein equations with a positive cosmological constant Lambda > 0 and satisfying the strong and dominant energy conditions asymptotically approach the de Sitter solution as t -> infinity. More precisely, prove that for generic initial data on a Cauchy surface, the future development is future geodesically complete and the spacetime metric approaches the de Sitter metric exponentially fast, with rate determined by Lambda.
statement.informal: Does every expanding universe with a positive cosmological constant eventually look like de Sitter space (exponentially expanding, empty space)? This conjecture suggests that the cosmological constant dominates all other forms of matter and energy at late times.

why_it_matters: With the discovery of the accelerating expansion of the universe (Nobel Prize 2011), the cosmic no-hair conjecture has become directly relevant to the far future of our actual universe. Proving it would establish the ultimate fate of the cosmos.

canonical.title: Cosmological event horizons, thermodynamics, and particle creation

status_evidence.title: The Cauchy Problem in General Relativity

partial_results.summary: Wald proved the conjecture for spatially homogeneous (Bianchi) cosmologies.
Friedrich proved future stability of de Sitter space (the linearized version of cosmic no-hair).
Ringstrom proved cosmic no-hair for certain matter models and symmetry classes.

machine_generated.summary: Proving that all expanding cosmologies with positive cosmological constant asymptotically approach de Sitter space.
decomposition: Extend Wald's proof beyond homogeneous cosmologies
Prove cosmic no-hair for generic inhomogeneous initial data

title: Dimer Model Exact Solutions in 3D

statement.canonical: Determine the free energy per site (entropy constant) of the dimer model on the simple cubic lattice in three dimensions. In 2D, Kasteleyn and Temperley-Fisher solved the dimer model exactly using Pfaffians, giving the free energy per site on the square lattice as (G/pi) where G is Catalan's constant. Determine whether an analogous exact solution exists in 3D, or prove that the 3D dimer model is not exactly solvable (in the sense of being expressible in closed form). The numerical value of the 3D entropy constant is approximately 0.4466.
statement.informal: How many ways can you perfectly tile a 3D box with dominoes? In 2D, there is a beautiful exact formula. In 3D, no exact solution is known, and it is unclear whether one exists.

why_it_matters: The dimer model is a fundamental exactly solvable model in 2D statistical mechanics. Understanding whether exact solvability extends to 3D would clarify the boundaries of exact solvability and has implications for combinatorics, algebra, and physics.

canonical.title: The statistics of dimers on a lattice

status_evidence.title: Lectures on Dimers

partial_results.summary: Kasteleyn and Temperley-Fisher independently solved the 2D dimer model using Pfaffians.
Hammersley showed that the 3D dimer entropy constant exists and provided bounds.
High-precision Monte Carlo estimates give the 3D entropy constant as approximately 0.4466.

machine_generated.summary: Determining the exact free energy of the dimer model on the simple cubic lattice in three dimensions.
decomposition: Determine whether an exact Pfaffian-type solution extends to 3D
Prove or disprove exact solvability of the 3D dimer model

title: Dyson's Conjecture Extensions

statement.canonical: Extend and generalize Dyson's conjecture (proved by Wilson and Gunson) and the Macdonald conjectures (proved by Cherednik using double affine Hecke algebras) to new settings. Specifically: (1) Prove or find combinatorial/physical proofs of the remaining open Macdonald-type constant term identities for exceptional root systems. (2) Determine the full connection between these identities and random matrix theory, particularly for the circular ensembles. (3) Extend the Selberg-Dyson-Macdonald integral framework to the elliptic level (elliptic Selberg integrals) and prove the conjectured evaluations.
statement.informal: Dyson conjectured a beautiful identity for the constant term of a Laurent polynomial associated to root systems. While the original conjecture is proved, extending it to elliptic and more general settings remains open.

why_it_matters: These identities lie at the intersection of random matrix theory, representation theory, special functions, and mathematical physics. They connect to partition functions in statistical mechanics and conformal field theory.

canonical.title: Statistical Theory of the Energy Levels of Complex Systems I

status_evidence.title: The importance of the Selberg integral

partial_results.summary: Dyson's original conjecture was proved by Wilson (1962) and Gunson (1962) independently.
Macdonald's conjectures were proved by Cherednik using double affine Hecke algebras.
Elliptic extensions of the Selberg integral have been partially established by Rains and Spiridonov.

machine_generated.summary: Extending Dyson's conjecture and Macdonald identities to elliptic and more general settings.
decomposition: Prove elliptic Selberg integral evaluations
Find combinatorial proofs for Macdonald-type identities

title: Optimal Rates of Entanglement Distillation

statement.canonical: Determine the distillable entanglement E_D(rho) for a general bipartite mixed state rho, defined as the maximum asymptotic rate at which Bell pairs (maximally entangled states) can be extracted from many copies of rho using LOCC. In particular, determine whether E_D(rho) equals the regularized relative entropy of entanglement, or find a computable formula for E_D.
statement.informal: Given many copies of a noisy entangled state shared between two distant parties, what is the best rate at which they can distill pure Bell pairs using only local operations and classical communication? Finding this rate for general states remains an open problem.

why_it_matters: Entanglement distillation is a core primitive for quantum communication, quantum key distribution, and quantum computing. Determining optimal rates would establish the fundamental value of entanglement as a resource.

canonical.title: Purification of Noisy Entanglement and Faithful Teleportation via Noisy Channels

status_evidence.title: Quantum entanglement

partial_results.summary: The hashing inequality provides a lower bound: E_D >= I_coh for any state.
Rains established the Rains bound as an upper bound on distillable entanglement.

machine_generated.summary: Determining the optimal asymptotic rate of entanglement distillation for general bipartite mixed states.
decomposition: Close the gap between hashing bound and Rains bound
Determine whether the regularized relative entropy of entanglement equals distillable entanglement

title: Entanglement Entropy Area Law in General

statement.canonical: Prove that the entanglement entropy S(rho_A) = -Tr(rho_A log rho_A) of the ground state of a local, gapped Hamiltonian in d spatial dimensions satisfies an area law: S(rho_A) <= C * |partial A|, where rho_A is the reduced density matrix of a region A, |partial A| is the area of the boundary of A, and C is a constant depending on the Hamiltonian but not on the size of A. This has been proved in d=1 by Hastings (2007) but remains open for d >= 2.
statement.informal: For quantum systems with a gap above the ground state, the entanglement between a region and its complement should be proportional to the boundary area, not the volume. This "area law" has been proven in one dimension but remains unproven in higher dimensions.

why_it_matters: The area law is fundamental to our understanding of quantum many-body physics. It explains why tensor network methods (DMRG, PEPS) work efficiently, connects to holographic entanglement entropy in quantum gravity, and is essential for classifying quantum phases of matter.

canonical.title: Quantum source of entropy for black holes
An Area Law for One Dimensional Quantum Systems

status_evidence.title: Area laws for the entanglement entropy

partial_results.summary: Hastings proved the area law for 1D gapped systems using Lieb-Robinson bounds.
Arad, Kitaev, Landau, and Vazirani gave an improved proof in 1D with better constants.
For specific models in d >= 2, area laws have been verified, but no general proof exists.

title: Existence of Magnetic Monopoles

statement.canonical: Determine whether magnetic monopoles exist as stable solutions in physically relevant gauge theories. Specifically: (1) Prove that the 't Hooft-Polyakov monopole solutions in spontaneously broken gauge theories (such as SU(2) Higgs theory) are stable and have finite energy in the full quantum theory. (2) Determine whether magnetic monopoles exist in the Standard Model or its grand unified extensions. (3) Prove Dirac's quantization condition eg = n hbar c / 2 from first principles in a rigorous quantum field theory framework. (4) Establish whether monopole solutions in Yang-Mills-Higgs theories on R^3 are classified by pi_2(G/H) as conjectured.
statement.informal: Do magnetic monopoles exist? Dirac showed they would explain charge quantization, and grand unified theories predict them. Proving their existence or non-existence as mathematical solutions, and understanding their properties, is a major challenge.

why_it_matters: Magnetic monopoles would explain one of the deepest mysteries in physics: why electric charge is quantized. They arise naturally in grand unified theories and are connected to important problems in topology, gauge theory, and quantum field theory.

canonical.title: Quantised Singularities in the Electromagnetic Field
Magnetic monopoles in unified gauge theories

partial_results.summary: 't Hooft and Polyakov independently found classical monopole solutions in SU(2) gauge-Higgs theory.
Jaffe and Taubes rigorously proved existence of the 't Hooft-Polyakov monopole in the BPS limit.
No experimental evidence for magnetic monopoles has been found despite extensive searches.

decomposition: Prove stability of 't Hooft-Polyakov monopoles in the full quantum theory

title: Final State Conjecture in General Relativity
aliases: Black Hole Stability and Final State

statement.canonical: The Final State Conjecture asserts that generic, asymptotically flat vacuum initial data (Sigma, h, K) for the Einstein vacuum equations Ric(g) = 0, satisfying the constraint equations and possessing sufficient mass concentration, evolve to a spacetime that asymptotically settles down to a finite number of Kerr black holes moving apart from each other, plus gravitational radiation dispersing to null infinity. More precisely, the maximal globally hyperbolic development (M, g) of generic asymptotically flat initial data, assuming cosmic censorship, decomposes in the far future into: (1) an exterior region approaching Minkowski space (for subcritical data) or a finite union of regions each asymptotic to a member of the Kerr family (M, g_{a_i, m_i}) with parameters (a_i, m_i), plus (2) gravitational radiation escaping to future null infinity. This encompasses as special cases the nonlinear stability of Minkowski space (proved by Christodoulou-Klainerman, 1993) and the nonlinear stability of the Kerr family (partially resolved).
statement.informal: According to general relativity, what is the ultimate fate of a gravitating system? The final state conjecture says that any isolated gravitational system will eventually settle down to a collection of Kerr black holes (rotating black holes described by just mass and spin) flying apart, plus gravitational waves radiating away. This is the dynamic version of the "no-hair theorem": black holes shed all their complexity and are completely characterized by mass and angular momentum.

why_it_matters: The final state conjecture is the central dynamical question in general relativity. It unifies black hole uniqueness, stability, and the long-time behavior of Einstein's equations into a single grand conjecture. Its resolution would confirm that Kerr black holes are the universal endpoints of gravitational collapse, validate the theoretical framework underlying gravitational wave astronomy (LIGO/Virgo observations), and represent a crowning achievement of the mathematical theory of general relativity.

canonical.title: Gravitational Field of a Spinning Mass as an Example of Algebraically Special Metrics
Lectures on black holes and linear waves

status_evidence.title: Kerr stability for small angular momentum

partial_results.summary: Christodoulou and Klainerman proved the nonlinear stability of Minkowski spacetime (the vacuum, zero-mass case of the final state conjecture).
Dafermos, Holzegel, Rodnianski, and Taylor proved the linear stability of the Schwarzschild black hole (non-rotating case).
Klainerman and Szeftel proved the nonlinear stability of Kerr black holes for small angular momentum |a| << m.
Giorgi, Klainerman, and Szeftel extended the Kerr stability result, and Dafermos, Holzegel, Rodnianski, and Taylor independently proved Kerr stability for the full subextremal range |a| < m.

machine_generated.summary: The final state conjecture asserts that generic gravitational systems in general relativity asymptotically settle down to a finite collection of Kerr black holes plus dispersing gravitational radiation.

title: Classification of Fractional Quantum Hall States

statement.canonical: Provide a complete mathematical classification of all possible fractional quantum Hall (FQH) states at a given filling fraction. Determine the full topological data (topological order, ground state degeneracy on a torus, edge state structure, braiding statistics of quasiparticles) for each filling fraction, and prove which topological orders are physically realizable in two-dimensional electron systems. In particular, determine whether non-abelian FQH states (such as the Moore-Read Pfaffian at nu = 5/2) are realized and classify all possible non-abelian anyonic excitations.
statement.informal: The fractional quantum Hall effect produces exotic states of matter with fractionally charged excitations. Classifying all possible such states and their exotic braiding statistics is a major open problem in condensed matter theory.

why_it_matters: FQH states are the prototypical examples of topological order and host anyonic quasiparticles that could be used for topological quantum computing. A complete classification would advance both fundamental physics and quantum computing.

canonical.title: Anomalous Quantum Hall Effect: An Incompressible Quantum Fluid with Fractionally Charged Excitations

Experimental evidence for non-abelian anyons at nu = 5/2 remains inconclusive.

title: Geometric Langlands Conjecture (Physics Approach)

statement.canonical: Rigorously establish the Kapustin-Witten approach to the geometric Langlands correspondence via topologically twisted N=4 super Yang-Mills theory. Specifically, prove that the S-duality of N=4 SYM, when twisted and compactified on a Riemann surface Sigma, produces the geometric Langlands correspondence as a mirror symmetry between the Hitchin moduli spaces M_H(G, Sigma) and M_H(G^L, Sigma) for a group G and its Langlands dual G^L. Make the physics derivation mathematically rigorous, establishing the equivalence of derived categories of coherent sheaves and D-modules on these moduli spaces.
statement.informal: Kapustin and Witten showed that the geometric Langlands correspondence, one of the deepest conjectures in mathematics, can be understood as an electromagnetic duality in physics. Making this physical argument into a proof is a grand challenge.

why_it_matters: The geometric Langlands program is one of the most important research directions in modern mathematics. The physics approach provides deep structural insights and suggests far-reaching generalizations. Making it rigorous would revolutionize our understanding of the Langlands program.

canonical.title: Electric-Magnetic Duality And The Geometric Langlands Program

Gaiotto and Witten extended the approach to boundary conditions and S-duality for surface operators.

title: Griffiths Singularities Rigorous Treatment

statement.canonical: Provide a complete rigorous characterization of Griffiths singularities in dilute and disordered spin systems. Prove that in the Griffiths phase (the temperature region between the critical temperatures of the pure and disordered systems), the free energy is an infinitely differentiable but non-analytic function of the magnetic field. Determine the exact nature of the essential singularity and its dependence on the disorder distribution. For quantum systems, prove that rare region effects lead to power-law (Griffiths-McCoy) singularities with continuously varying exponents.
statement.informal: In disordered magnets, rare regions can locally order at temperatures above the bulk critical temperature, creating unusual singularities. Rigorously characterizing these subtle effects is an important open problem.

why_it_matters: Griffiths singularities demonstrate that disorder can have profound effects on phase transitions. Understanding them rigorously is important for disordered systems ranging from random magnets to neural networks and biological systems.

canonical.title: Nonanalytic Behavior Above the Critical Point in a Random Ising Ferromagnet

status_evidence.title: Rare region effects at classical, quantum, and nonequilibrium phase transitions

McCoy and Wu showed that quantum Griffiths effects lead to power-law singularities in random transverse-field Ising models.
Rigorous bounds on Griffiths singularity strength have been obtained for specific models.

title: Haag's Theorem and Interaction Picture in QFT

statement.canonical: Resolve the implications of Haag's theorem for interacting quantum field theories. Haag's theorem states that the interaction picture does not exist in relativistic quantum field theory: the free and interacting fields cannot both satisfy the canonical commutation relations in the same Hilbert space if they are related by a unitary transformation. Determine a mathematically rigorous framework that either (a) circumvents Haag's theorem while preserving the physical content of perturbative QFT, or (b) provides a non-perturbative definition of interacting QFT in 4 dimensions that does not rely on the interaction picture.
statement.informal: Haag's theorem says that the standard textbook approach to interacting quantum field theory (the interaction picture) is mathematically inconsistent. How do we reconcile this with the spectacular empirical success of perturbative QFT?

why_it_matters: Haag's theorem exposes a fundamental mathematical inconsistency in the foundations of quantum field theory as commonly practiced. Resolving it would place perturbative QFT on solid mathematical foundations or point to the correct non-perturbative framework.

canonical.title: On quantum field theories

status_evidence.title: Haag's theorem and its implications for the foundations of quantum field theory

partial_results.summary: Constructive QFT has produced rigorous interacting theories in d=2 and d=3 that avoid Haag's theorem by not using the interaction picture.
Causal perturbation theory (Epstein-Glaser) provides a framework that avoids the direct conflict with Haag's theorem.

machine_generated.summary: Resolving the implications of Haag's theorem for the mathematical foundations of interacting quantum field theory.
decomposition: Develop a rigorous non-perturbative framework for interacting QFT avoiding the interaction picture

title: Mechanism of High-Temperature Superconductivity

statement.canonical: Determine the mechanism of high-temperature superconductivity in cuprate materials. Specifically, determine whether the two-dimensional Hubbard model (or the t-J model) on a square lattice has a d-wave superconducting ground state at physically relevant parameters (doping levels x ~ 0.1-0.2, U/t ~ 8). Prove or disprove that the repulsive Hubbard model supports superconductivity, and if so, determine the maximum critical temperature T_c and the nature of the pairing mechanism.
statement.informal: High-temperature superconductors conduct electricity with zero resistance at temperatures far above what conventional theory predicts. Despite decades of research, we still don't have a proven theory for how this works.

why_it_matters: High-temperature superconductivity is one of the most important unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Understanding it could lead to room-temperature superconductors, which would revolutionize technology from energy transmission to transportation.

title: Holographic Entanglement Entropy Conjectures

statement.canonical: Rigorously prove the quantum-corrected Ryu-Takayanagi (RT) formula and its covariant generalization (the Hubeny-Rangamani-Takayanagi or HRT formula) for computing entanglement entropy in holographic theories. Specifically, prove that for a boundary region A in a holographic CFT, the entanglement entropy is given by S(A) = min ext_gamma [Area(gamma)/(4 G_N) + S_bulk(Sigma_gamma)], where gamma is a quantum extremal surface homologous to A, and S_bulk is the bulk entropy. Extend this to prove the quantum maximin formula and establish the holographic entropy cone for arbitrary numbers of parties.
statement.informal: In the AdS/CFT correspondence, the entanglement entropy of a boundary region equals the area of a minimal surface in the bulk spacetime, plus quantum corrections. Proving this rigorously and characterizing all possible entanglement structures in holographic theories is an open problem.

why_it_matters: Holographic entanglement entropy is the most concrete quantitative tool connecting quantum information to quantum gravity. Proving these conjectures would establish the information-theoretic foundations of holography and constrain viable theories of quantum gravity.

canonical.title: Holographic Derivation of Entanglement Entropy from AdS/CFT

status_evidence.title: Quantum Extremal Surfaces: Holographic Entanglement Entropy beyond the Classical Regime

partial_results.summary: Lewkowycz and Maldacena gave a gravitational path integral derivation of the RT formula for static geometries.
The holographic entropy cone has been characterized for up to 5 parties.
Penington and Almheiri et al. used quantum extremal surfaces to resolve aspects of the information paradox.

title: KPZ Universality Conjecture

statement.canonical: Prove the KPZ universality conjecture: a wide class of one-dimensional random growth models, interacting particle systems, and directed polymers in random media belong to the KPZ universality class, sharing the same scaling exponents (growth exponent beta = 1/3, roughness exponent alpha = 1/2, dynamic exponent z = 3/2) and the same limiting distributions (Tracy-Widom for curved initial conditions, Baik-Rains for flat initial conditions). Extend KPZ universality to higher dimensions d >= 2, where even the scaling exponents are not known exactly.
statement.informal: Many different random growth processes, from burning paper to bacterial colonies, are believed to share the same universal statistical behavior described by the KPZ equation. Proving this universality and understanding it in higher dimensions is one of the major open problems in probability and mathematical physics.

why_it_matters: KPZ universality is one of the most important discoveries in non-equilibrium statistical mechanics. It connects random growth, interacting particles, random matrices, and combinatorics. Hairer's Fields Medal work on the KPZ equation solved part of the problem, but full universality remains open.

canonical.title: Dynamic Scaling of Growing Interfaces

title: Lee-Yang Zeros and Phase Transitions

statement.canonical: Extend the Lee-Yang theorem to characterize the distribution of zeros of the partition function in the complex fugacity (or magnetic field) plane for general lattice models beyond the ferromagnetic Ising model. Specifically, determine for which classes of Hamiltonians (multi-component spins, frustrated systems, quantum spin systems) the partition function zeros are confined to specific curves or regions, and prove that the density of zeros near the real axis determines the nature and order of the phase transition in the thermodynamic limit.

why_it_matters: Lee-Yang zeros provide a rigorous mathematical framework for understanding phase transitions. Extending this theory would give powerful tools for classifying phase transitions and could lead to new insights in quantum phase transitions and non-equilibrium statistical mechanics.

title: Many-Body Localization

statement.canonical: Prove or disprove the existence of many-body localization (MBL) in interacting quantum systems with disorder. Specifically, for the disordered Heisenberg chain H = sum_i [J(S_i . S_{i+1}) + h_i S_i^z] with random fields h_i drawn from a distribution of width W, prove that for sufficiently large W, all eigenstates at all energies are localized (area-law entanglement entropy, no thermalization, emergent integrability). why_it_matters: MBL would represent a fundamentally new phase of matter that violates the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis. It has implications for quantum information storage, quantum computing, and our understanding of thermalization in quantum systems.

title: Mass Gap in Non-Abelian Gauge Theories

statement.canonical: For a general compact simple gauge group G, prove that the pure Yang-Mills quantum field theory (without matter) in four spacetime dimensions has a mass gap Delta > 0. This means the energy spectrum has a gap between the vacuum energy E=0 and the first excited state E >= Delta > 0. While the Clay Millennium Problem focuses on existence and mass gap together, the mass gap alone (assuming existence) is already a deep open problem. Determine the dependence of Delta on the gauge group G and whether the mass gap is related to the dynamically generated scale Lambda_QCD.

why_it_matters: The mass gap explains why nuclear forces are short-range. Understanding its origin for general gauge groups would illuminate confinement, the structure of the hadron spectrum, and connections between gauge theory and geometry.

title: MIP* = RE

statement.canonical: The complexity class MIP* consists of all languages decidable by a polynomial-time verifier interacting with two (or more) provers who may share arbitrary quantum entanglement but cannot communicate during the protocol. The class RE (recursively enumerable) consists of all languages for which membership can be verified by a Turing machine that halts on positive instances. The MIP* = RE theorem states that these two classes are equal: MIP* = RE. Equivalently, the halting problem can be verified by a classical polynomial-time verifier interacting with two quantum provers sharing entanglement. This result implies a negative answer to Tsirelson's problem (the set of quantum correlations is strictly larger than the set of correlations achievable with tensor product strategies) and refutes the Connes embedding conjecture.
statement.informal: If two provers who share unlimited quantum entanglement try to convince a classical verifier of a statement, they can convince the verifier of any statement that is true (recursively enumerable), even undecidable ones like the halting problem. This was proven by Ji, Natarajan, Vidick, Wright, and Yuen in 2020, and it also disproved a famous conjecture in operator algebras by Connes.

why_it_matters: MIP* = RE is one of the most profound results in quantum complexity theory. It connects three seemingly unrelated areas: the theory of interactive proofs in computational complexity, the study of quantum correlations in quantum information (Tsirelson's problem), and the Connes embedding conjecture in operator algebras. The result shows that quantum entanglement gives interactive proof systems extraordinary power, far beyond what was previously imagined.

canonical.title: MIP* = RE
MIP* = RE

status_evidence.title: From Operator Algebras to Complexity Theory and Back

partial_results.summary: Ito and Vidick showed MIP* contains NEXP, greatly expanding the known power of entangled provers beyond the classical MIP = NEXP result.
Natarajan and Wright showed NEEXP is contained in MIP*, establishing super-exponential power for entangled-prover interactive proofs.
Ji, Natarajan, Vidick, Wright, and Yuen proved MIP* = RE, completely characterizing the class and refuting the Connes embedding conjecture.

title: Mirror Symmetry Mathematical Proof

statement.canonical: Prove mirror symmetry in full generality. Specifically: (1) Prove the Homological Mirror Symmetry (HMS) conjecture of Kontsevich: for a mirror pair (X, X^check) of Calabi-Yau manifolds, there is an equivalence of triangulated categories between D^b(Coh(X)) (derived category of coherent sheaves on X) and D^b(Fuk(X^check)) (derived Fukaya category of X^check). (2) Prove the SYZ (Strominger-Yau-Zaslow) conjecture: mirror Calabi-Yau manifolds admit dual special Lagrangian torus fibrations. (3) Establish these results for compact Calabi-Yau threefolds, not just for special cases (tori, K3 surfaces, toric varieties).
statement.informal: Mirror symmetry is a deep duality from string theory that relates pairs of Calabi-Yau manifolds, exchanging their complex and symplectic geometry. Proving this duality rigorously is one of the central challenges of modern mathematics.

why_it_matters: Mirror symmetry has been enormously influential in both mathematics and physics, generating deep connections between algebraic geometry, symplectic geometry, and string theory. A complete proof would be a landmark achievement.

title: No-Hair Theorem (Rigorous General Proof)

statement.canonical: Prove the no-hair conjecture in full generality: every stationary, asymptotically flat, vacuum black hole solution of the Einstein equations in 3+1 dimensions is uniquely characterized by its mass M, angular momentum J, and electric charge Q, and is therefore a member of the Kerr-Newman family. Remove the remaining technical assumptions (analyticity of the event horizon, non-degeneracy conditions) from existing uniqueness theorems. Extend to include relevant matter fields and higher dimensions.

why_it_matters: The no-hair theorem is fundamental to black hole physics and the information paradox. A complete proof would settle whether classical black holes can carry any additional information, with implications for quantum gravity.

title: Existence of NPT Bound Entangled States

statement.canonical: Determine whether there exist bipartite quantum states rho on H_A tensor H_B that have a non-positive partial transpose (NPT) but are nevertheless bound entangled, i.e., no pure entanglement can be distilled from any number of copies of rho via LOCC. Equivalently, is every NPT state distillable?
statement.informal: If a quantum state fails the PPT test (its partial transpose has negative eigenvalues), it is certainly entangled. But can we always extract useful entanglement from such states, or could there be NPT states whose entanglement is permanently locked away?

why_it_matters: This is one of the most fundamental open problems in entanglement theory. A positive answer would imply a radical restructuring of entanglement theory, while a negative answer would simplify the resource theory of entanglement significantly.

title: Penrose Inequality (General Case)

statement.canonical: Prove the general Penrose inequality: for an asymptotically flat initial data set (M, g, K) satisfying the dominant energy condition, with outermost apparent horizon of area A, the ADM mass m satisfies m >= sqrt(A / (16 pi)). The Riemannian case (K = 0, time-symmetric) was proved by Huisken-Ilmanen (for connected horizons) and Bray (general), but the full Lorentzian case with non-zero second fundamental form K remains open.
statement.informal: The Penrose inequality provides a lower bound on the total mass of a spacetime in terms of the area of its black hole horizons. It has been proven for the time-symmetric case but remains open in full generality.

why_it_matters: The Penrose inequality is deeply connected to cosmic censorship and the fundamental structure of black holes in general relativity. It provides a quantitative test of whether singularities are always hidden behind horizons.

canonical.title: Naked Singularities

status_evidence.title: Present status of the Penrose inequality

partial_results.summary: Huisken and Ilmanen proved the Riemannian Penrose inequality for a single connected horizon using inverse mean curvature flow.

The full Lorentzian Penrose inequality with general initial data remains open.

title: Percolation Threshold Exact Values

statement.canonical: Determine the exact percolation thresholds p_c for site percolation on common lattices where exact values are not known, including the simple cubic lattice in d=3 (numerically p_c approx 0.3116), the BCC lattice (p_c approx 0.2464), and the FCC lattice (p_c approx 0.198). For bond percolation, determine exact p_c for the simple cubic lattice (p_c approx 0.2488). Determine whether these thresholds can be expressed in closed form or are transcendental numbers.
statement.informal: At what probability does a random network first become connected across its whole extent? For some 2D lattices, exact values are known (like p_c = 1/2 for bond percolation on the square lattice), but for 3D lattices, only numerical estimates exist.

why_it_matters: Percolation thresholds are fundamental constants in statistical mechanics and probability theory. Finding exact values would deepen our understanding of phase transitions and could reveal hidden mathematical structures.

title: PPT Bound Entanglement Problem

statement.canonical: Determine whether every entangled quantum state with a positive partial transpose (PPT) is bound entangled, meaning no pure entanglement can be distilled from it by local operations and classical communication (LOCC). Equivalently, prove or disprove that there exist NPT (negative partial transpose) bound entangled states, or that the PPT condition is both necessary and sufficient for undistillability.
statement.informal: Some quantum states are entangled but their entanglement cannot be "purified" or concentrated into a useful form. The PPT bound entanglement problem asks whether having a positive partial transpose is exactly the dividing line between distillable and undistillable entanglement.

why_it_matters: Understanding bound entanglement is fundamental to quantum information theory. It determines the structure of entanglement as a resource, impacts quantum communication protocols, and connects to key questions in operator algebras and the geometry of quantum states.

canonical.title: Mixed-State Entanglement and Distillation: Is there a Bound Entanglement in Nature?

title: Quantum Channel Capacity Additivity (General Conjectures)

statement.canonical: Characterize which quantum channels satisfy additivity of the minimum output entropy: S_min(N_1 tensor N_2) = S_min(N_1) + S_min(N_2). Although the general additivity conjecture was disproved by Hastings (2009), determine the precise classes of channels for which additivity holds, and find computable single-letter formulas for the classical capacity of quantum channels beyond the known cases (degradable channels, entanglement-breaking channels, unital qubit channels).

why_it_matters: The classical capacity of quantum channels is the most fundamental quantity in quantum communication. Despite Hastings' counterexample, the problem of computing capacities for specific important channels (e.g., depolarizing channel) remains open and is critical for practical quantum communication.

title: Quantum Communication Complexity Exact Separations

statement.canonical: Determine the exact relationship between quantum and classical communication complexity for total Boolean functions. Specifically, prove or disprove that there exists a total Boolean function f:{0,1}^n x {0,1}^n -> {0,1} such that the quantum communication complexity Q(f) is exponentially smaller than the classical (randomized) communication complexity R(f). Determine whether the log-rank conjecture holds for quantum communication.

why_it_matters: Communication complexity is a foundational model in theoretical computer science. Understanding quantum advantages here has implications for quantum algorithms, circuit complexity, and the power of quantum computing.

title: Fundamental Limits of Quantum Error Correction

statement.canonical: Determine the quantum capacity Q(N) of a general quantum channel N, defined as the maximum asymptotic rate at which quantum information can be reliably transmitted through the channel. In particular, determine whether the regularized coherent information I_coh^{reg}(N) = lim_{n->infty} (1/n) max_{rho} I_coh(N^{tensor n}, rho) admits a single-letter formula, or prove that the quantum capacity is not additive and no single-letter formula exists.
statement.informal: How much quantum information can be reliably sent through a noisy quantum channel? Unlike classical channels where Shannon's theorem gives a clean formula, the quantum capacity requires an intractable regularization. The open problem is whether a computable formula exists.

why_it_matters: The quantum capacity is the most fundamental quantity in quantum communication theory. Finding computable formulas or proving their non-existence would resolve a central question in quantum Shannon theory and guide the design of practical quantum communication systems.

title: Exact Quantum Fault Tolerance Threshold

statement.canonical: Determine the exact value of the fault-tolerance threshold p_th for quantum computation under the most general noise model (adversarial, local, Markovian). Specifically, for the standard depolarizing noise model, determine the supremum of error rates p such that arbitrary quantum computations can be performed with polynomial overhead using concatenated codes, topological codes, or any fault-tolerant scheme. Current rigorous lower bounds give p_th >= 10^{-4} while numerical estimates suggest p_th ~ 10^{-2} for surface codes.

why_it_matters: The fault-tolerance threshold is the most important number in quantum computing engineering. It determines whether current and near-future quantum hardware can support scalable computation. Closing the gap between rigorous bounds and numerical estimates is crucial for quantum computing development.

title: Quantum Gravity and Quantum Information Connections

statement.canonical: Rigorously establish the connections between quantum information theory and quantum gravity. Specifically: (1) Prove or disprove the ER=EPR conjecture of Maldacena and Susskind, that quantum entanglement between two systems is equivalent to a non-traversable Einstein-Rosen bridge connecting them. (2) Determine whether quantum computational complexity has a geometric dual in AdS/CFT, as conjectured by Susskind's "complexity=volume" and "complexity=action" proposals. (3) Establish whether quantum error correcting codes underlie the structure of spacetime in holographic theories.
statement.informal: Are entanglement and wormholes the same thing? Is the geometry of spacetime built from quantum information? These deep conjectures connecting quantum information to gravity remain unproven.

why_it_matters: These conjectures represent a paradigm shift in our understanding of quantum gravity, suggesting that spacetime itself emerges from quantum information. Resolving them would fundamentally change our understanding of both quantum mechanics and general relativity.

title: Quantum Hamiltonian Complexity and QMA-completeness

statement.canonical: Prove or disprove the quantum PCP conjecture: for the k-local Hamiltonian problem with k = O(1), there exists a constant epsilon > 0 such that it is QMA-hard to determine whether the ground state energy of a k-local Hamiltonian is at most a or at least a + epsilon * n, where n is the number of qubits. This is the quantum analogue of the PCP theorem in classical complexity theory. Additionally, determine the exact complexity of computing ground state properties of local Hamiltonians on physically motivated lattice geometries.
statement.informal: Is approximating the ground state energy of quantum systems hard even when we only need a rough estimate? The quantum PCP conjecture says yes, which would have profound implications for quantum computing and physics.

why_it_matters: The quantum PCP conjecture is one of the most important open problems at the intersection of quantum information and computational complexity. It would imply that quantum entanglement in ground states is fundamentally different from what can be verified efficiently, with deep implications for condensed matter physics.

canonical.title: Guest Column: The Quantum PCP Conjecture

status_evidence.title: Quantum Hamiltonian Complexity

partial_results.summary: Kitaev proved that the local Hamiltonian problem is QMA-complete with inverse-polynomial promise gap.
The NLTS (No Low-energy Trivial States) conjecture was proved by Anshu, Breuckmann, and Nirkhe.

title: Optimal Key Rates in Quantum Key Distribution

statement.canonical: Determine the secret key capacity K(N) of a general quantum channel N, defined as the maximum asymptotic rate at which a secret key can be established between two parties connected by the channel, with unlimited public classical communication. In particular, determine whether the squashed entanglement or the relative entropy of entanglement provides the exact key rate, and find computable formulas or tight bounds for key rates of practical quantum channels (depolarizing, amplitude damping, bosonic channels).
statement.informal: What is the maximum rate at which two parties can generate a shared secret key using a quantum channel, even against the most powerful eavesdropper allowed by quantum mechanics? Finding exact answers for realistic channels is still open.

why_it_matters: Quantum key distribution is the most mature application of quantum information science. Determining exact key rates is essential for understanding the fundamental security limits of quantum cryptography and designing practical QKD systems.

title: Quantum Reverse Shannon Theorem Extensions

statement.canonical: Extend the quantum reverse Shannon theorem to determine the exact entanglement and communication costs of simulating arbitrary quantum channels (including channels with memory, feedback, or adaptive encodings) using shared entanglement and classical communication. Determine the exact trade-off between entanglement consumption rate and classical communication rate for channel simulation in the most general network settings.
statement.informal: The quantum reverse Shannon theorem says that any quantum channel can be simulated using shared entanglement and classical communication at a rate given by the channel's entanglement-assisted capacity. The open problem is to extend this to more general settings involving channels with memory, networks, and feedback.

why_it_matters: This problem is central to understanding the fundamental resource trade-offs in quantum communication theory. Its resolution would complete our understanding of quantum channel simulation and has implications for quantum network design.

title: Quantum Shannon Theory Capacity Regions

statement.canonical: Determine the full capacity region for quantum multi-user channels, including the quantum multiple access channel (QMAC), quantum broadcast channel (QBC), and quantum interference channel (QIC). This generalizes Shannon's classical network information theory to the quantum realm, where entanglement and superposition create fundamentally new phenomena.

why_it_matters: Multi-user quantum Shannon theory is essential for designing future quantum networks and quantum internet architectures. Resolving capacity regions would provide the theoretical foundation for quantum network protocols.

title: Quantum State Merging Generalizations

statement.canonical: Determine the optimal entanglement and communication costs for quantum state merging and state redistribution in multiparty settings. Specifically, for n parties sharing a quantum state rho on H_1 tensor ... tensor H_n, find the achievable rate region for redistributing parts of the state among the parties using LOCC, and determine whether the conditional quantum mutual information provides the exact answer in all multiparty scenarios.

title: Quantum Unique Games Conjecture
aliases: Unique Games Conjecture for Entangled Provers

statement.canonical: The classical Unique Games Conjecture (Khot, 2002) asserts that for every epsilon > 0, there exists a sufficiently large alphabet size k such that it is NP-hard to determine whether a unique game over alphabet [k] has value at least 1 - epsilon or at most epsilon. The quantum unique games conjecture asks whether an analogous hardness result holds when the provers are allowed to share quantum entanglement: specifically, is it the case that for entangled-prover unique games, distinguishing near-perfect completeness from low soundness remains computationally hard (under appropriate complexity-theoretic assumptions), or does entanglement fundamentally change the approximability landscape of constraint satisfaction problems?
statement.informal: The classical Unique Games Conjecture is a central assumption in computational complexity that implies tight hardness-of-approximation results. The quantum version asks: if two provers in a unique game are allowed to share quantum entanglement, does the problem remain hard, or can entanglement help them cheat in ways that change the computational difficulty of the problem?

why_it_matters: The Unique Games Conjecture is arguably the most important open conjecture in the theory of approximation algorithms and hardness of approximation. Its quantum variant connects quantum entanglement to computational complexity and has implications for quantum PCP theorems, nonlocal games, and the power of quantum proof systems. Understanding the quantum version illuminates the relationship between entanglement and computational hardness.

canonical.title: On the power of unique 2-prover 1-round games
Unique Games with Entangled Provers are Easy

status_evidence.title: Hypercontractivity, sum-of-squares proofs, and their applications

partial_results.summary: Kempe, Regev, and Toner showed that unique games with entangled provers can be solved efficiently when the alphabet size is constant, contrasting the classical hardness.
Ji, Natarajan, Vidick, Wright, and Yuen proved MIP* = RE (2020), which resolved Connes' embedding conjecture and showed the power of entangled provers, but the quantum UGC remains open in general.
Khot, Minzer, and Safra proved the 2-to-2 Games Conjecture, a variant of the classical UGC, but the quantum analog remains unresolved.

title: Random Matrix Theory Universality

statement.canonical: Prove universality of local eigenvalue statistics for general classes of random matrices beyond the Wigner ensemble. Specifically: (1) Prove that the local eigenvalue correlation functions of band matrices with band width W >> n^{1/2} converge to the Gaussian ensembles in the limit n -> infinity. (2) Prove universality for random matrices with general (non-Gaussian) i.i.d. entries with finite moments. (3) Establish universality at the spectral edge (Tracy-Widom statistics) for sparse random matrices and adjacency matrices of random graphs.
statement.informal: Random matrix theory predicts that the statistical properties of eigenvalue spacings are universal -- the same for a huge variety of random matrix ensembles. While this has been proven for certain classes, extending it to all physically relevant classes remains open.

why_it_matters: Random matrix universality has applications across physics (quantum chaos, nuclear physics, quantum gravity), mathematics (number theory, combinatorics), and data science. A complete universality theory would unify these connections.

canonical.title: Random Matrices (3rd edition)

status_evidence.title: A Dynamical Approach to Random Matrix Theory

Tao and Vu independently proved universality using the four moment theorem.
Band matrix universality for W >> n^{3/4} has been established, but the conjectured threshold W >> n^{1/2} remains open.
