title: Erdos-Mollin-Walsh Conjecture

statement.canonical: There do not exist three consecutive powerful numbers (a powerful number is a positive integer n such that if a prime p divides n, then p^2 also divides n). Equivalently, there is no positive integer n such that n, n+1, and n+2 are all powerful.
statement.informal: A powerful number is one where every prime factor appears at least to the second power (like 4, 8, 9, 25, 27, ...). The conjecture says you can never find three powerful numbers in a row.

why_it_matters: The Erdos-Mollin-Walsh conjecture addresses the distribution of powerful numbers, which are closely related to square-free numbers and the structure of the integers. Its resolution would illuminate the spacing properties of numbers with constrained factorizations.

canonical.title: On powerful numbers

status_evidence.title: Unsolved Problems in Number Theory (3rd edition), Problem B16

partial_results.summary: It is known that infinitely many pairs of consecutive powerful numbers exist (e.g., n=8, n+1=9).
Erdős conjectured the non-existence of triples, and this has been verified computationally for all n up to large bounds.
The abc conjecture implies that there are only finitely many triples of consecutive powerful numbers.

machine_generated.summary: The Erdos-Mollin-Walsh conjecture asserts that three consecutive powerful numbers cannot exist, a problem connected to the abc conjecture.
decomposition: Extending computational verification to larger ranges
Reducing to Diophantine equations and Pell-type equations
Connecting to the abc conjecture to obtain conditional finiteness

title: Erdos-Sos Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every graph G with average degree greater than k - 2 contains every tree T with k edges as a subgraph. Equivalently, if |E(G)| > (k-2)|V(G)|/2, then G contains a copy of every tree on k + 1 vertices.
statement.informal: If a graph has enough edges (average degree bigger than k - 2), then it must contain every possible tree with k edges. Having many edges forces all tree shapes to appear.

why_it_matters: The Erdos-Sos conjecture, posed in 1963, is a central conjecture in extremal graph theory about embedding trees in dense graphs. It strengthens the classical Erdos-Gallai theorem on paths and would be a key step toward understanding the Turan problem for trees. Ajtai, Komlos, Simonovits, and Szemeredi announced a proof for large trees around 2010, but the full manuscript has not been published.

canonical.title: On a problem in graph theory (problem)

status_evidence.title: The Erdos-Sos conjecture for trees of large size

partial_results.summary: The conjecture holds for paths (Erdos-Gallai theorem), stars, and caterpillars.
Ajtai, Komlos, Simonovits, and Szemeredi announced a proof for all sufficiently large trees (not yet published as a full paper).
The conjecture is proved for trees with bounded maximum degree by using the Regularity Lemma.
Besomi, Pavez-Signe, and Stein proved the conjecture for trees of given size in graphs of large girth.

machine_generated.summary: The Erdos-Sos conjecture states that every graph with average degree > k-2 contains every tree with k edges. An unpublished proof for large trees was announced by Ajtai, Komlos, Simonovits, and Szemeredi. The conjecture remains formally open.
decomposition: Completing and publishing the Ajtai-Komlos-Simonovits-Szemeredi proof
Proving the conjecture for all tree sizes without the Regularity Lemma
Extensions to sparse random graphs and digraphs

title: Erdos-Straus Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every integer n >= 2, there exist positive integers x, y, z such that 4/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z.
statement.informal: The conjecture claims that for any integer n >= 2, the fraction 4/n can always be written as a sum of exactly three unit fractions (fractions with numerator 1).

why_it_matters: The Erdos-Straus conjecture is a central problem in the theory of Egyptian fractions, connecting elementary number theory with Diophantine equations. Its resolution would advance understanding of the representability properties of rational numbers as sums of unit fractions.

canonical.title: On a Diophantine equation
Sur l'équation diophantienne 4/n = 1/x_1 + 1/x_2 + 1/x_3

status_evidence.title: Computational verification of the Erdős–Straus conjecture

partial_results.summary: Verified computationally for all n up to 10^14 by Allan Swett and later extended by others.
Schinzel showed the conjecture holds for all n not congruent to 1 (mod 24) by elementary parametric identities.
The density of integers n for which 4/n has a three-term Egyptian fraction representation is 1 (Vaughan, Elsholtz, Tao).

machine_generated.summary: The Erdos-Straus conjecture states that for every integer n >= 2, the fraction 4/n can be expressed as a sum of three unit fractions, verified computationally to very large bounds but still open in general.
decomposition: Analyzing residue classes modulo small numbers and finding parametric identity families
Extending computational verification and refining probabilistic heuristics
Connecting to sieve methods and the distribution of primes in special forms

title: Fatou Conjecture (Density of Hyperbolicity)

statement.canonical: In the space of rational maps of degree d >= 2 on the Riemann sphere, the hyperbolic maps form a dense open subset. Equivalently, for the quadratic family f_c(z) = z^2 + c, the set of parameters c in C for which f_c is hyperbolic (every critical orbit converges to an attracting cycle) is dense in the Mandelbrot set's complement, and hyperbolic components are dense in the Mandelbrot set.
statement.informal: Among all polynomial or rational maps that define dynamical systems on the complex plane, the "well-behaved" (hyperbolic) ones should be the typical case. The conjecture says that for almost any parameter value, the corresponding dynamical system is structurally stable -- chaotic maps that are not hyperbolic should be rare in a topological sense.

why_it_matters: The Fatou conjecture, originating from Fatou's work in the 1920s, is one of the most important open problems in complex dynamics. It implies that structurally stable systems are generic, which would give a comprehensive understanding of the parameter space of rational maps. It is closely related to the MLC conjecture and has profound implications for our understanding of the Mandelbrot set.

canonical.title: Sur les equations fonctionnelles
canonical.locator: Bulletin de la Societe Mathematique de France, vol. 48, pp. 33-94

status_evidence.title: Complex Dynamics and Renormalization
status_evidence.locator: Annals of Mathematics Studies, no. 135, Princeton University Press

partial_results.summary: Mane, Sad, and Sullivan proved the conjecture is equivalent to density of structural stability.
Lyubich proved density of hyperbolicity for real quadratic maps.
Graczyk and Swiatek independently proved density of hyperbolicity for real quadratic maps.
Kozlovski, Shen, and van Strien proved density of hyperbolicity for real polynomials of any degree.

machine_generated.summary: The Fatou conjecture asserts that hyperbolic rational maps are dense in the space of all rational maps of a given degree. Proved for real polynomials, the full complex case remains open.
decomposition: Quasiconformal deformation techniques for rational maps
Relationship to MLC and combinatorial rigidity
Extension from real to complex parameter spaces

title: Frankl's Conjecture: Optimal Abundance Bound
aliases: Frankl's Union-Closed Sets Conjecture Sharp Bound

statement.canonical: For a union-closed family F of sets (closed under pairwise union) with F not equal to {empty set}, determine the best constant c such that there always exists an element contained in at least c * |F| sets of F. The union-closed conjecture of Frankl asserts c = 1/2. Gilmer (2022) proved c >= 0.01, and subsequent work improved this to c >= (3 - sqrt(5))/2 ~ 0.382. Closing the gap to 1/2 remains open.
statement.informal: In a family of sets closed under union, there should be some element appearing in at least half the sets. The best proven constant is about 38.2% instead of 50%. What is the true threshold?

why_it_matters: Frankl's union-closed conjecture is one of the most famous open problems in combinatorics. While the qualitative conjecture (c = 1/2) remains open, the quantitative question of determining the sharp constant has attracted intense recent interest. Gilmer's 2022 breakthrough using entropy methods opened a new line of attack, but the gap between 0.382 and 0.5 persists.

canonical.title: The union-closed conjecture (problem communicated)
A constant lower bound for the union-closed sets conjecture

status_evidence.title: Approximate union closed conjecture

partial_results.summary: Gilmer proved that some element appears in at least 1% of the sets, the first constant fraction bound.
Chase and Lovett, Alweiss-Huang-Sellke, and Pebody independently improved the constant to (3-sqrt(5))/2 ~ 0.382.
The constant (3-sqrt(5))/2 is the natural barrier of the entropy approach and new ideas are needed to go further.
Knill showed that lattice-theoretic approaches give the full conjecture for large subclasses.

machine_generated.summary: The problem of determining the optimal constant in Frankl's union-closed conjecture. The entropy method gives c >= 0.382, but the conjectured c = 1/2 remains unproven. Breaking the entropy barrier is the key challenge.
decomposition: Breaking the entropy barrier of (3-sqrt(5))/2
New structural or algebraic approaches beyond information-theoretic methods
Connections to lattice theory and poset-based reformulations

title: Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa Conjecture

statement.canonical: If A is a finite subset of Z^n (or more generally an abelian group) with |A + A| <= K|A| (small doubling), then A can be covered by at most poly(K) translates of a subgroup H with |H| <= |A|. More precisely, there exists a generalized arithmetic progression of dimension at most C(K) containing A, where C(K) is polynomial in K.
statement.informal: Sets with small additive doubling (the sumset is not much larger than the set itself) must have a strong additive structure: they can be efficiently covered by a structured set (generalized arithmetic progression) of comparable size.

why_it_matters: The Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa conjecture is a central problem in additive combinatorics. Freiman's theorem (1973) provides exponential bounds; the conjecture sharpens these to polynomial. In late 2023, Gowers, Green, Manners, and Tao proved the conjecture over F_2^n using entropy methods, a major breakthrough. The integer (Z^n) case remains open with polynomial bounds.

canonical.title: An analog of Freiman's theorem in groups

status_evidence.title: On a conjecture of Marton

partial_results.summary: Gowers, Green, Manners, and Tao proved the conjecture over F_2^n.
Sanders gave quasi-polynomial bounds over the integers.
The polynomial bound over Z (the integer case) remains open.

systems: Lean 4 (formalized by Tao et al. in the PFR project)

machine_generated.summary: The Polynomial Freiman-Ruzsa conjecture sharpens Freiman's theorem to polynomial bounds. Proved over F_2^n by Gowers-Green-Manners-Tao; the integer case remains open.
decomposition: Entropy methods and information-theoretic techniques
Additive structure in abelian groups
Quasi-polynomial to polynomial improvement over Z

title: Furstenberg's x2 x3 Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let mu be a Borel probability measure on the circle R/Z that is invariant under both T_2: x -> 2x (mod 1) and T_3: x -> 3x (mod 1), and is ergodic with respect to the semigroup generated by T_2 and T_3. Then mu is either the Lebesgue measure or is supported on a finite set of rationals. More generally, Furstenberg conjectured that the only nonatomic measure jointly invariant and ergodic under multiplication by 2 and by 3 on R/Z is the Lebesgue measure.
statement.informal: Consider the circle (numbers from 0 to 1 with the ends glued together). The operations "multiply by 2" and "multiply by 3" (mod 1) each preserve certain natural probability distributions. The conjecture says the only "nice" probability distribution that is preserved by both operations simultaneously is the uniform distribution.

why_it_matters: Furstenberg's conjecture, posed in 1967, is a central open problem in ergodic theory and dynamical systems. It concerns the rigidity of measures under the action of non-lacunary multiplicative semigroups and has deep connections to number theory, fractal geometry, and the structure of invariant measures. It is related to the Mahler-Furstenberg conjecture on normal numbers.

canonical.title: Disjointness in ergodic theory, minimal sets, and a problem in Diophantine approximation

status_evidence.title: Invariant measures and arithmetic quantum unique ergodicity

partial_results.summary: Rudolph proved the conjecture under the additional assumption that the measure has positive entropy for one of the maps.
Johnson generalized Rudolph's result.
Einsiedler, Katok, and Lindenstrauss proved analogous results for higher-rank torus actions on homogeneous spaces.
Hochman showed that self-similar measures under non-commensurable contractions have the expected dimension, closely related to Furstenberg's conjecture.
Shmerkin proved Furstenberg's intersection conjecture (a related problem on dimensions of intersections).

machine_generated.summary: Furstenberg's x2 x3 conjecture asserts that the only nonatomic probability measure on the circle jointly invariant and ergodic under multiplication by 2 and 3 is the Lebesgue measure. It is a central problem in ergodic theory and dynamical systems.
decomposition: Entropy and dimension rigidity methods for jointly invariant measures
Higher-rank homogeneous dynamics and measure classification
Connections to normal numbers and fractal geometry of invariant sets

title: Freyd's Generating Hypothesis
aliases: Generating Hypothesis in Stable Homotopy Theory
Freyd's Conjecture on Stable Homotopy Groups

statement.canonical: If f: X -> Y is a map between finite spectra (finite CW-complexes stably) such that the induced map pi_*(f): pi_*(X) -> pi_*(Y) on stable homotopy groups is the zero map, then f is stably null-homotopic (i.e., f = 0 in the stable homotopy category). Equivalently, the functor pi_* from the stable homotopy category of finite spectra to graded abelian groups is faithful.
statement.informal: In stable homotopy theory, we study spaces "up to suspension." The generating hypothesis says that stable homotopy groups detect everything: if a map between finite spaces induces the zero map on all stable homotopy groups, then the map itself must be zero (stably).

why_it_matters: Freyd's generating hypothesis, posed in 1966, is one of the oldest open problems in stable homotopy theory and homological algebra. If true, it would dramatically simplify the structure of the stable homotopy category of finite spectra. It has deep consequences for the Picard group of the stable homotopy category and for understanding the structure of thick subcategories via the Hopkins-Smith theorem.

canonical.title: Stable homotopy
canonical.locator: Proceedings of the Conference on Categorical Algebra, La Jolla, Springer, pp. 121-172

status_evidence.title: The generating hypothesis revisited
status_evidence.locator: Stable and Unstable Homotopy, Fields Institute Communications, pp. 73-92

partial_results.summary: Freyd proved that the generating hypothesis implies the stable homotopy groups of spheres form a principal ideal domain module over pi_*(S^0).
Devinatz showed the hypothesis is equivalent to several other important structural statements about the stable homotopy category.
Hovey and Palmieri studied the generating hypothesis in the context of axiomatic stable homotopy categories.
Lockridge established that the generating hypothesis holds in various algebraic stable homotopy categories derived from ring spectra.

machine_generated.summary: Freyd's generating hypothesis asserts that stable homotopy groups detect all maps between finite spectra. It remains one of the oldest open problems in stable homotopy theory.
decomposition: Thick subcategory and chromatic methods
Algebraic stable homotopy category analogues
Computational approaches via Adams spectral sequence

title: Gilbreath's Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let p_1 = 2, p_2 = 3, p_3 = 5, ... be the sequence of prime numbers. Define the sequence of iterated absolute differences: d_1(n) = |p_{n+1} - p_n| and d_k(n) = |d_{k-1}(n+1) - d_{k-1}(n)| for k >= 2. Then d_k(1) = 1 for all k >= 1.
statement.informal: Write down the prime numbers. Take the absolute differences of consecutive terms. Repeat this process. The conjecture says the first element of each resulting row of differences is always 1.

why_it_matters: Gilbreath's conjecture is a striking regularity in the sequence of primes that has been verified extensively but resists all proof attempts. It encodes deep structural information about the distribution of prime gaps.

canonical.title: Gilbreath's conjecture (unpublished, communicated to Richard Guy)
Unsolved Problems in Number Theory

status_evidence.title: Iterated absolute values of differences of consecutive primes

partial_results.summary: Odlyzko verified Gilbreath's conjecture for the first 10^13 primes.
Odlyzko showed that a sufficiently strong form of the prime k-tuples conjecture would imply Gilbreath's conjecture.

machine_generated.summary: Gilbreath's conjecture asserts that the leading term in each row of iterated absolute differences of consecutive primes is always 1, verified computationally to enormous bounds.
decomposition: Connecting the conjecture to probabilistic models of prime gaps
Analyzing the dynamics of iterated differencing on structured sequences
Extending computational verification and developing rigorous sufficient conditions

title: Global Regularity for Supercritical Surface Quasi-Geostrophic Equation

statement.canonical: Consider the inviscid surface quasi-geostrophic (SQG) equation on R^2: ∂_t θ + u·∇θ = 0, where u = ∇^⊥(-Δ)^{-1/2}θ. Given smooth initial data θ_0, does the solution remain smooth for all time, or can finite-time singularities develop?
statement.informal: The surface quasi-geostrophic equation is a 2D model from geophysics that shares many structural features with the 3D Euler equations. The question is whether smooth solutions can develop singularities in finite time.

why_it_matters: The inviscid SQG equation is a key model in fluid dynamics and serves as a testing ground for understanding potential singularity formation in the 3D Euler equations, to which it bears a strong structural analogy. While the critical dissipative SQG equation was resolved by Caffarelli and Vasseur (2010) and Kiselev, Nazarov, and Volberg (2007), the inviscid case remains open and is considered one of the most important problems in mathematical fluid dynamics.

canonical.title: Formation of strong fronts in the 2-D quasigeostrophic thermal active scalar

status_evidence.title: Nonlocal maximum principles for active scalars

partial_results.summary: Constantin, Majda, and Tabak identified the SQG equation as a 2D analogue of 3D Euler and studied front formation.
Kiselev, Nazarov, and Volberg proved global regularity for the critical dissipative SQG equation.
Caffarelli and Vasseur gave an independent proof of critical SQG regularity using De Giorgi methods.
The inviscid case remains open; numerical simulations suggest possible singularity formation.

machine_generated.summary: The global regularity question for the inviscid surface quasi-geostrophic equation asks whether smooth solutions develop finite-time singularities. The dissipative critical case is resolved, but the inviscid case is open.
decomposition: Singularity scenarios and front formation analysis
Comparison with 3D Euler singularity models
Numerical simulation and adaptive mesh refinement studies

title: Goldbach's Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every even integer greater than 2 can be expressed as the sum of two prime numbers. That is, for every even integer n > 2, there exist primes p and q such that n = p + q.
statement.informal: Every even number bigger than 2 can be written as the sum of two primes. For example, 4 = 2+2, 6 = 3+3, 8 = 3+5, 10 = 5+5 = 3+7. The conjecture says this pattern never stops.

why_it_matters: Goldbach's conjecture, proposed in 1742, is one of the oldest unsolved problems in number theory and all of mathematics. It sits at the heart of additive number theory and is connected to deep questions about the distribution of primes and the structure of the integers.

canonical.title: Letter from Goldbach to Euler, June 7, 1742
Unsolved Problems in Number Theory

status_evidence.title: The ternary Goldbach conjecture is true

partial_results.summary: Helfgott proved the weak (ternary) Goldbach conjecture: every odd integer greater than 5 is the sum of three primes.
Chen Jingrun proved that every sufficiently large even integer is the sum of a prime and a product of at most two primes (Chen's theorem).
Verified computationally for all even integers up to 4 x 10^18.
Vinogradov proved that all sufficiently large odd integers are the sum of three primes.

machine_generated.summary: Goldbach's conjecture asserts every even integer greater than 2 is the sum of two primes. The weak (ternary) version was proved by Helfgott in 2013, but the binary case remains open.
decomposition: Strengthening circle method estimates for binary additive prime problems
Improving sieve bounds to bridge the gap between Chen's theorem and the full conjecture
Computational verification strategies and probabilistic models for exceptional sets

title: Graceful Tree Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every tree with n edges admits a graceful labeling. That is, for every tree T with n edges, there exists an injective function f: V(T) -> {0, 1, ..., n} such that the set of values {|f(u) - f(v)| : uv in E(T)} equals {1, 2, ..., n}.
statement.informal: Label the vertices of any tree using distinct numbers from 0 to n (where n is the number of edges). The conjecture claims you can always choose the labels so that the differences along the edges are exactly the numbers 1, 2, ..., n, each appearing exactly once.

why_it_matters: The graceful tree conjecture, proposed by Ringel in 1963 and refined by Kotzig and Rosa, is a central open problem in graph labeling theory. A proof would imply Ringel's conjecture that the complete graph K_{2n+1} can be decomposed into 2n+1 copies of any given tree with n edges. It has spawned a rich subfield of combinatorics.

canonical.title: On certain valuations of the vertices of a graph
canonical.locator: Theory of Graphs (Proc. Internat. Sympos., Rome, 1966), pp. 349-355

canonical.title: A dynamic survey of graph labeling

status_evidence.title: A dynamic survey of graph labeling

partial_results.summary: Caterpillars, paths, stars, and many special tree families have been shown to be graceful.
Trees with at most 35 vertices have been verified computationally.
Ringel's conjecture (decomposition of K_{2n+1} into copies of any tree) was proved for large n by Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov.
Adamaszek (2010) showed that trees with a vertex adjacent to many leaves are graceful.

machine_generated.summary: The graceful tree conjecture asserts that every tree admits a graceful labeling, where vertex labels produce all distinct edge differences from 1 to n. A proof would imply Ringel's conjecture on decompositions of complete graphs.
decomposition: Extending known graceful families via recursive and algebraic constructions
Computational verification for larger tree sizes

title: Grimm's Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every positive integer n, if n+1, n+2, ..., n+k are all composite numbers, then there exist distinct primes p_1, p_2, ..., p_k such that p_i divides n+i for each i = 1, 2, ..., k.
statement.informal: Given any run of consecutive composite (non-prime) numbers, one can assign to each composite number in the run a distinct prime factor. No two composites in the run need share the same assigned prime.

why_it_matters: Grimm's conjecture connects the combinatorial structure of prime factorizations with the distribution of primes. It implies Legendre's conjecture and would yield new insights into prime gaps and the matching structure of divisibility.

canonical.title: A conjecture on consecutive composite numbers

status_evidence.title: Matching the natural numbers up to n with distinct multiples in another interval

partial_results.summary: Erdős and Pomerance proved Grimm's conjecture holds if the prime gap satisfies p_{n+1} - p_n < p_n^{1/2 - epsilon} for some epsilon > 0.
Verified computationally for all composite gaps beginning below 10^9.
Ramachandra, Shorey, and Tijdeman showed the conjecture holds for all sufficiently large n under the assumption of a strong form of the prime number theorem.

machine_generated.summary: Grimm's conjecture states that in any run of consecutive composite numbers, one can assign a distinct prime divisor to each composite in the run.
decomposition: Applying Hall's marriage theorem to the bipartite graph of composites and their prime factors
Connecting prime gap estimates to the existence of the required matching
Extending computational verification to larger ranges

title: Grothendieck's Standard Conjectures

statement.canonical: Grothendieck's standard conjectures consist of four related conjectures for smooth projective varieties: (A) the Lefschetz standard conjecture - the inverse of the hard Lefschetz isomorphism in cohomology is induced by an algebraic correspondence; (B) the conjecture of Hodge type - a certain quadratic form on algebraic cycles defined via intersection pairing is positive definite on primitive classes; (C) the Kunneth standard conjecture - the Kunneth components of the diagonal are algebraic; (D) numerical and homological equivalence coincide for algebraic cycles.
statement.informal: These conjectures assert that deep topological structures on the cohomology of algebraic varieties (like the Lefschetz decomposition) have purely algebraic origins, bridging topology and algebra in a canonical way.

why_it_matters: Grothendieck proposed these conjectures in 1968 as the foundation for his theory of motives. They would establish the algebraicity of many cohomological constructions and would imply the Weil conjectures (now proved by other means), the Hodge conjecture in certain cases, and provide the basis for a universal cohomology theory. They remain among the deepest open problems in algebraic geometry.

canonical.title: Standard conjectures on algebraic cycles

status_evidence.title: The standard conjectures

partial_results.summary: Conjecture D is known to imply Conjecture C. Conjecture A implies all others.
All conjectures are proved for abelian varieties in characteristic zero (Lieberman-Kleiman).
Proved for varieties of dimension <= 2 and for certain special varieties.

machine_generated.summary: Grothendieck's standard conjectures assert the algebraicity of key cohomological structures on smooth projective varieties, foundational for the theory of motives.
decomposition: Hodge-theoretic and motivic approaches
Characteristic p methods and crystalline cohomology
Special cases via moduli spaces and Shimura varieties

title: Gyarfas-Sumner Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every tree T, there exists a function f_T such that every graph G with no induced copy of T satisfies chi(G) <= f_T(omega(G)), where chi(G) is the chromatic number and omega(G) is the clique number. In other words, every tree is chi-binding: forbidding it as an induced subgraph bounds the chromatic number in terms of the clique number.
statement.informal: If you forbid a specific tree as an induced subgraph, then graphs cannot have a huge chromatic number unless they also have a large clique. Trees are "well-behaved" as forbidden induced subgraphs in this sense.

why_it_matters: The Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture, independently posed by Gyarfas (1975) and Sumner (1981), is a central open problem in structural graph theory. It is one of the most natural questions about chi-boundedness and connects Ramsey theory, perfect graphs, and chromatic number theory. It is known that not all graphs are chi-bounded (e.g., cycles of odd length), making the role of trees special.

canonical.title: On Ramsey covering-numbers
Subtrees of a graph and the chromatic number

status_evidence.title: A survey of chi-boundedness

partial_results.summary: The conjecture holds when T is a path (Gyarfas, 1987).
The conjecture holds when T is a star (trivially, since K_{1,t}-free graphs have bounded degree if omega is bounded).
Scott and Seymour proved the conjecture for all trees with radius at most 2.
Kierstead and Penrice proved the conjecture for trees obtained from a star by subdividing each edge.

machine_generated.summary: The Gyarfas-Sumner conjecture states that for every tree T, the class of T-free graphs is chi-bounded. It is proved for paths, stars, and trees of small radius, but remains open for general trees.
decomposition: Proving the conjecture for trees of larger radius or diameter
Finding optimal chi-binding functions for specific trees
Connections to the Erdos-Hajnal conjecture and polynomial chi-boundedness

title: Hadamard Conjecture

statement.canonical: A Hadamard matrix of order n is an n x n matrix H with entries in {-1, +1} satisfying H H^T = n I_n. Hadamard's conjecture asserts that a Hadamard matrix of order n exists for every positive integer n that is divisible by 4 (and also for n = 1 and n = 2).
statement.informal: A Hadamard matrix is a square matrix whose entries are all +1 or -1, and whose rows are mutually orthogonal. Such matrices can only exist in sizes 1, 2, or multiples of 4. The conjecture is that they exist for every multiple of 4.

why_it_matters: Hadamard matrices have wide applications in signal processing, coding theory, combinatorial designs, and quantum computing. They achieve the maximum possible determinant for matrices with bounded entries (Hadamard's maximum determinant problem). Their existence for all multiples of 4 would resolve a fundamental question in combinatorial design theory.

canonical.title: Resolution d'une question relative aux determinants
Hadamard Matrices and Their Applications

status_evidence.title: Hadamard matrices of small order

partial_results.summary: Hadamard matrices have been constructed for all orders n = 4k with k <= 167 (i.e., n <= 668), as of the most recent surveys.
The Paley construction gives Hadamard matrices of order q+1 whenever q is a prime power congruent to 3 mod 4.
The smallest order for which existence is unknown is 668.
Seberry's asymptotic result: Hadamard matrices of order 2^t m exist for all odd m, for t sufficiently large depending on m.

machine_generated.summary: The Hadamard conjecture states that a Hadamard matrix of order n exists for every n divisible by 4. These matrices have applications in coding theory, signal processing, and quantum computing.
decomposition: Constructive algebraic approaches (Paley, Williamson, and generalized constructions)
Computer search and optimization methods for small unsolved orders
Asymptotic existence results via probabilistic and algebraic methods

title: Hadwiger Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every integer t >= 1, every graph with no K_t minor is (t-1)-colorable. Equivalently, if a graph G requires at least t colors for a proper vertex coloring (i.e., chi(G) >= t), then G contains K_t as a minor.
statement.informal: If a graph needs at least t colors to color its vertices (so that no two adjacent vertices share a color), then it must contain a "squished" version of the complete graph on t vertices as a substructure.

why_it_matters: Hadwiger's conjecture, posed in 1943, is considered one of the deepest open problems in graph theory. It generalizes the Four Color Theorem (the case t = 5) and strengthens it dramatically. Paul Seymour has called it "one of the most important open problems in graph theory, if not all of combinatorics."

canonical.title: Uber eine Klassifikation der Streckenkomplexe
Hadwiger's conjecture

status_evidence.title: Breaking the degeneracy barrier for coloring graphs with no K_t minor

partial_results.summary: The cases t <= 4 were proved by Hadwiger (1943) and Dirac (1952).
The case t = 5 is equivalent to the Four Color Theorem, proved by Appel and Haken.
The case t = 6 was proved by Robertson, Seymour, and Thomas (assuming the Four Color Theorem).
Norin, Song, and Postle broke the sqrt(log n) barrier: every graph with no K_t minor is O(t (log t)^beta)-colorable for some beta < 1.

machine_generated.summary: Hadwiger's conjecture states that every graph with chromatic number at least t contains K_t as a minor. It generalizes the Four Color Theorem and is considered one of the deepest open problems in graph theory.
decomposition: Extending Robertson-Seymour-Thomas techniques beyond t=6
Improving coloring bounds for K_t-minor-free graphs
Structural graph theory and extremal approaches to minor containment

title: Hadwiger-Nelson Problem

statement.canonical: What is the minimum number of colors needed to color all points of the Euclidean plane such that no two points at distance exactly 1 from each other share the same color? Equivalently, what is the chromatic number chi(G) of the unit-distance graph of the plane? It is known that 5 <= chi <= 7.
statement.informal: How many colors do you need to paint the plane so that no two points exactly 1 unit apart share the same color? The answer is known to be between 5 and 7, but the exact value remains unknown.

why_it_matters: The Hadwiger-Nelson problem, first posed around 1950, is one of the most famous open problems in combinatorial geometry. It connects discrete geometry, graph theory, and measure theory. Despite its simple statement, it has resisted resolution for over 70 years. In 2018, Aubrey de Grey made a breakthrough by improving the lower bound from 4 to 5, the first improvement since the problem was posed.

canonical.title: The Mathematical Coloring Book
The chromatic number of the plane is at least 5

status_evidence.title: Small unit-distance graphs in the plane

partial_results.summary: Nelson and Isbell independently showed chi >= 4 and Isbell showed chi <= 7 using a hexagonal tiling argument.
Aubrey de Grey constructed a unit-distance graph requiring 5 colors, improving the lower bound to 5.
The Polymath16 project refined de Grey's construction, finding smaller 5-chromatic unit-distance graphs.

title: Hall's Conjecture
aliases: Hall's Conjecture on the Distance Between Cubes and Squares

statement.canonical: For every epsilon > 0, there exists a constant c(epsilon) > 0 such that if x and y are positive integers with x^3 != y^2, then |x^3 - y^2| > c(epsilon) * x^{1/2 - epsilon}. The strong form asserts that |x^3 - y^2| > c * x^{1/2} for an absolute constant c > 0.
statement.informal: When a perfect cube and a perfect square are not equal, the gap between them grows at least roughly as the square root of the cube. It is hard for a cube and a square to be very close together without being equal.

why_it_matters: Hall's conjecture is a fundamental Diophantine inequality related to the abc conjecture and the theory of elliptic curves. It has direct implications for bounding solutions to Mordell's equation y^2 = x^3 + k.

canonical.title: The Diophantine equation x^3 - y^2 = k

status_evidence.title: Rational points near curves and small nonzero |x^3 - y^2| via lattice reduction

partial_results.summary: Danilov proved that |x^3 - y^2| >> x^{1/2} / (log x)^c for some constant c, assuming the abc conjecture.
Elkies found extremely close pairs (x^3, y^2) by lattice methods, but none violating the conjecture.
The weak form (with epsilon) follows from the abc conjecture (Langevin, 1986).

machine_generated.summary: Hall's conjecture bounds the minimal distance between perfect cubes and perfect squares, asserting that |x^3 - y^2| grows at least as fast as x^{1/2 - epsilon}.
decomposition: Connecting to the abc conjecture and deriving conditional results

Analyzing the equation via elliptic curve theory and heights

title: Halpern-Lauchli Theorem Extensions to Uncountable Cardinals

statement.canonical: The Halpern-Lauchli theorem states that for any finite number d of perfect binary trees T_1, ..., T_d of height omega, and any finite coloring of the level product Union_n (T_1(n) x ... x T_d(n)), there exist strong subtrees S_i subset T_i of height omega such that the level product of S_1, ..., S_d is monochromatic. The open problem asks: does the analogous statement hold when omega is replaced by an uncountable cardinal kappa? In particular, does it hold at kappa = omega_1?
statement.informal: The Halpern-Lauchli theorem is a powerful Ramsey-type result about coloring products of trees. It works beautifully for trees of countable height. The open question is whether this deep combinatorial principle extends to trees of uncountable height, which requires fundamentally new techniques or potentially new axioms.

why_it_matters: The Halpern-Lauchli theorem is a cornerstone of infinitary combinatorics with applications to Banach space theory and Ramsey theory. Extending it to uncountable cardinals is a central problem in set-theoretic combinatorics that connects to large cardinal axioms and forcing. Understanding this extension has implications for partition calculus and the structure of uncountable trees.

canonical.title: A partition theorem
canonical.locator: Transactions of the AMS, vol. 124, pp. 360-367

status_evidence.title: Halpern-Lauchli theorem at a measurable cardinal
status_evidence.locator: Journal of Symbolic Logic, vol. 82, no. 4, pp. 1560-1575

partial_results.summary: The original Halpern-Lauchli theorem was proved at omega by Halpern and Lauchli.
Laver-Shelah showed consistency of the theorem at a weakly compact cardinal.
Dobrinen proved versions of the Halpern-Lauchli theorem at a measurable cardinal.
The case at omega_1 without large cardinal assumptions remains open.

machine_generated.summary: The problem asks whether the Halpern-Lauchli partition theorem for products of trees extends from countable height to uncountable cardinals. Results exist at measurable and weakly compact cardinals, but the general case, especially at omega_1, remains open.
decomposition: Consistency strength of the uncountable Halpern-Lauchli theorem

Connections to Banach space geometry via trees

title: Happel's Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let A be a finite-dimensional algebra over an algebraically closed field k. If the derived category D^b(mod-A) of bounded complexes of finitely generated A-modules has no loops in its Auslander-Reiten quiver and has a tilting object, then A is derived equivalent to a hereditary algebra.
statement.informal: If the derived category of a finite-dimensional algebra is well-behaved (has no self-extensions of a certain type and possesses a tilting object), then the algebra should be essentially as simple as a hereditary algebra (one with no higher extension groups).

why_it_matters: Happel's conjecture connects the structure of derived categories with the representation-theoretic classification of algebras. It is a central open question in the representation theory of finite-dimensional algebras and homological algebra. The conjecture would provide a powerful criterion for recognizing derived-hereditary algebras.

canonical.title: On the derived category of a finite-dimensional algebra

status_evidence.title: A characterization of hereditary categories with tilting object

partial_results.summary: Happel proved the conjecture for piecewise hereditary algebras of Dynkin and extended Dynkin type.
Happel (2001) proved that abelian hereditary categories with a tilting object are derived equivalent to mod-H for H hereditary.
The conjecture has been verified for many special classes of algebras including gentle and string algebras.
The general case remains open.

decomposition: Verification for specific families of algebras
Tilting theory and derived equivalence classification
AR-quiver structure and homological conditions

title: Hardy-Littlewood k-Tuple Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let H = {h_1, h_2, ..., h_k} be an admissible k-tuple of non-negative integers (meaning for every prime p, the set H does not cover all residue classes modulo p). Let pi_H(x) denote the number of integers n <= x such that n + h_1, n + h_2, ..., n + h_k are all prime. Then pi_H(x) ~ S(H) * x / (log x)^k as x -> infinity, where S(H) = prod_{p prime} (1 - nu_H(p)/p) / (1 - 1/p)^k and nu_H(p) is the number of distinct residues of H modulo p. In particular, pi_H(x) -> infinity.
statement.informal: Consider any pattern of gaps between primes that is not ruled out by simple divisibility obstructions. The conjecture predicts that this pattern appears infinitely often among the primes, and gives an exact formula for how frequently it appears.

why_it_matters: The Hardy-Littlewood k-tuple conjecture is one of the most fundamental conjectures in analytic number theory. It implies the twin prime conjecture and predicts the fine distribution of prime constellations. It forms the backbone of quantitative predictions about primes.

canonical.title: Some problems of 'Partitio numerorum'; III: On the expression of a number as a sum of primes

status_evidence.title: Primes in tuples I

partial_results.summary: Goldston-Pintz-Yildirim (GPY) proved that lim inf (p_{n+1} - p_n) / log p_n = 0, a major step toward k-tuples.
Zhang proved bounded gaps between primes (gap at most 70 million), the first finite bound.
Maynard and Tao independently proved bounded gaps with a much smaller bound, and showed lim inf (p_{n+m} - p_n) < infinity for all m.
The full quantitative prediction for the constant S(H) has been confirmed numerically to high precision for many specific tuples.

machine_generated.summary: The Hardy-Littlewood k-tuple conjecture predicts the asymptotic density of prime constellations of any admissible shape, generalizing the twin prime conjecture.
decomposition: Extending the Maynard-Tao sieve to capture asymptotic densities rather than just existence
Proving the conjecture for specific small tuples (e.g., twin primes, prime triplets)
Connecting to the Elliott-Halberstam conjecture and distribution of primes in progressions

title: Hilbert's 16th Problem (Second Part)

statement.canonical: For a polynomial vector field of degree n in the plane, dx/dt = P_n(x,y), dy/dt = Q_n(x,y), where P_n and Q_n are polynomials of degree at most n, determine a uniform upper bound H(n) on the number of limit cycles (isolated periodic orbits), and investigate their possible configurations. In particular, is H(n) finite for each n?
statement.informal: Consider a system of two differential equations in the plane where the right-hand sides are polynomials of degree n. The question asks: what is the maximum number of closed loops (limit cycles) that the trajectories of such a system can form? Even for quadratic systems (n=2), this remains unsolved.

why_it_matters: The second part of Hilbert's 16th problem, posed in 1900, is one of the most enduring open problems in mathematics. It sits at the intersection of dynamical systems, real algebraic geometry, and qualitative theory of ODEs. Despite over a century of research, even the finiteness of the number of limit cycles for polynomial systems of any fixed degree has not been fully established in general.

canonical.title: Mathematische Probleme
canonical.locator: Nachrichten von der Gesellschaft der Wissenschaften zu Gottingen, pp. 253-297

status_evidence.title: Centennial history of Hilbert's 16th problem
status_evidence.locator: Bulletin of the AMS, vol. 39, no. 3, pp. 301-354

partial_results.summary: Dulac claimed finiteness for individual analytic vector fields in 1923, but the proof was incomplete.
Ecalle and Ilyashenko independently proved finiteness for individual analytic planar vector fields.
For quadratic systems, it is known that 4 limit cycles can occur, but the sharp upper bound remains unknown.
The infinitesimal (weak) Hilbert 16th problem was solved by Binyamini, Novikov, and Yakovenko for Abelian integrals.

machine_generated.summary: The second part of Hilbert's 16th problem asks for a uniform upper bound on the number of limit cycles for planar polynomial vector fields of degree n. Even finiteness of a uniform bound is unknown for quadratic systems.

title: Hilbert–Smith Conjecture

statement.canonical: If a locally compact group G acts faithfully and continuously on a connected topological manifold M, then G must be a Lie group. Equivalently, the p-adic integers Z_p cannot act faithfully and continuously on any connected topological manifold.
statement.informal: If a group acts continuously and faithfully on a manifold, the group must itself be a smooth object (a Lie group). In particular, exotic groups like the p-adic integers cannot act in this way.

why_it_matters: The Hilbert–Smith conjecture is a natural generalization of Hilbert's fifth problem (solved by Gleason, Montgomery, and Zippin in 1952). It connects the topology of transformation groups with the structure theory of locally compact groups and has implications for understanding symmetries of manifolds.

canonical.title: Periodic and nearly periodic transformations
Hilbert's fifth problem and related problems on transformation groups

status_evidence.title: The Hilbert-Smith conjecture for three-manifolds

partial_results.summary: Newman (1931) and Smith (1941) established foundational results on periodic transformations of manifolds.
The conjecture was proved for actions on 1- and 2-manifolds.
Repovš and Ščepin proved the conjecture for Lipschitz actions.
Pardon proved the conjecture for 3-manifolds.
The conjecture remains open for manifolds of dimension ≥ 4.

Extensions of Pardon's 3-manifold proof to higher dimensions

title: Hopf Conjecture (Positive Sectional Curvature)

statement.canonical: Let M be a closed (compact, without boundary) Riemannian manifold of even dimension 2n with positive sectional curvature. Then the Euler characteristic chi(M) > 0. Equivalently, does positive sectional curvature in even dimensions force the Euler characteristic to be positive?
statement.informal: In Riemannian geometry, sectional curvature measures how curved a space is in each 2-dimensional direction. If a compact even-dimensional manifold curves positively in every direction, Hopf conjectured that a topological invariant called the Euler characteristic must be positive. This is known in dimensions 2 and 4 but open in general.

why_it_matters: The Hopf conjecture, dating to the 1930s, is one of the most fundamental open problems in Riemannian geometry. It exemplifies the interaction between curvature and topology, which is the central theme of differential geometry. Despite its clean and elementary statement, it has resisted all approaches for nearly a century. It is closely related to the Chern conjecture on the Euler characteristic of affine manifolds.

canonical.title: Uber die Abbildungen der dreidimensionalen Sphare auf die Kugelflache

status_evidence.title: Riemannian manifolds with positive sectional curvature
status_evidence.locator: Geometry of Manifolds with Non-negative Sectional Curvature, Lecture Notes in Mathematics, vol. 2110

partial_results.summary: Chern proved the conjecture in dimension 4 using Gauss-Bonnet.
The conjecture is open already in dimension 6.
Positive Ricci curvature does not suffice, as shown by Sha and Yang's examples.
Under the stronger assumption of positive curvature operator, the manifold is a sphere (Bohm-Wilking), so the conjecture holds trivially.

Topological constraints from positive curvature

title: Hot Spots Conjecture

statement.canonical: For a bounded, connected planar domain D with smooth boundary, the second Neumann eigenfunction of the Laplacian attains its maximum and minimum on the boundary of D. Equivalently, if heat diffuses in D with insulating boundary conditions, the hottest and coldest points eventually migrate to the boundary for generic initial conditions.
statement.informal: If you heat a flat, insulated metal plate, the hottest and coldest spots will eventually end up on the boundary, not in the interior. This is conjectured to hold for all convex domains, but the general case for simply connected domains is open.

why_it_matters: The hot spots conjecture, posed by J. Rauch (1974), lies at the intersection of PDE theory, spectral geometry, and probability. It has stimulated research on nodal domains, probabilistic coupling methods, and spectral properties of the Laplacian. It remains open even for general simply connected planar domains.

canonical.title: Five problems: an introduction to the qualitative theory of partial differential equations

status_evidence.title: Hot Spots in Convex Domains are in the Tips of the Boundary

partial_results.summary: Proved for convex domains by Judge and Mondal (2020), building on earlier work of Jerison-Nadirashvili and Atar-Burdzy.
Proved for lip domains, thin domains, and domains with one axis of symmetry.
Burdzy and Werner found a counterexample for domains with holes (not simply connected).

title: Invariant Subspace Problem

statement.canonical: Does every bounded linear operator T on a separable, infinite-dimensional Hilbert space H have a non-trivial closed invariant subspace? That is, does there exist a closed subspace M of H with {0} ⊊ M ⊊ H such that T(M) ⊆ M?
statement.informal: Given any continuous linear transformation on an infinite-dimensional Hilbert space, must there always be a proper closed subspace that the transformation maps into itself?

why_it_matters: The invariant subspace problem is one of the most important open questions in functional analysis and operator theory. It is connected to the structure theory of operators and the understanding of how operators can be decomposed. Enflo (1976) and Read (1985) showed counterexamples exist on general Banach spaces, but the Hilbert space case remains open.

canonical.title: Invariant subspaces of polynomially compact operators
On the invariant subspace problem for Banach spaces

status_evidence.title: Modern Approaches to the Invariant-Subspace Problem

partial_results.summary: Aronszajn and Smith proved the result for compact operators on Hilbert spaces.
Lomonosov proved that operators commuting with a nonzero compact operator have invariant subspaces.
Enflo constructed a counterexample on a Banach space (published 1987, circulated 1976).
Read constructed a counterexample on ℓ^1.
The problem remains open on Hilbert spaces and reflexive Banach spaces.

machine_generated.summary: The invariant subspace problem asks whether every bounded linear operator on a separable infinite-dimensional Hilbert space has a non-trivial closed invariant subspace. Counterexamples exist on Banach spaces but the Hilbert space case remains open.
decomposition: Spectral-theoretic approaches and Brown's theorem

title: Inverse Galois Problem

statement.canonical: Is every finite group isomorphic to the Galois group of some Galois extension of the rational numbers Q? That is, for every finite group G, does there exist a Galois extension K/Q such that Gal(K/Q) is isomorphic to G?
statement.informal: Galois theory associates a symmetry group to every polynomial equation. The inverse Galois problem asks: can every possible finite symmetry group arise from some polynomial equation with rational coefficients?

why_it_matters: The inverse Galois problem is one of the most fundamental open questions in algebra. It connects group theory with algebraic number theory and arithmetic geometry. Hilbert posed it in the late 19th century, and it remains open despite major advances in our understanding of Galois groups, modular forms, and arithmetic geometry.

canonical.title: Ueber die Irreduzibilitat ganzer rationaler Funktionen mit ganzzahligen Koeffizienten
Inverse Galois Theory

status_evidence.title: Topics in Galois Theory

partial_results.summary: Hilbert's irreducibility theorem reduces the problem to finding regular extensions of Q(t).
Shafarevich proved every finite solvable group is a Galois group over Q.
All symmetric and alternating groups S_n and A_n are known to be Galois groups over Q.
All sporadic simple groups except possibly the Mathieu group M_23 are known to be Galois groups over Q.
Thompson and Voelklein verified many simple groups of Lie type as Galois groups over Q.

machine_generated.summary: The inverse Galois problem asks whether every finite group arises as a Galois group over the rational numbers. All solvable groups and most sporadic simple groups are known to occur, but the general case remains open.
decomposition: Rigidity methods and Hurwitz space approaches for simple groups of Lie type

title: Non-abelian Iwasawa Main Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let F_infty / F be a p-adic Lie extension of a number field F with Galois group G = Gal(F_infty / F), a compact p-adic Lie group (not necessarily abelian). The non-abelian Iwasawa main conjecture asserts that the characteristic element of the dual Selmer group (as a module over the Iwasawa algebra Lambda(G)) equals, up to a unit, a p-adic L-function element in an appropriate K_1 group of the Iwasawa algebra. This generalizes the classical abelian Iwasawa main conjecture (proved by Mazur-Wiles and Wiles) to non-commutative Galois extensions.
statement.informal: Iwasawa theory connects algebraic objects (ideal class groups in towers of number fields) with analytic objects (p-adic L-functions). The classical theory works for commutative (abelian) towers. The non-abelian conjecture extends this to non-commutative towers, requiring fundamentally new algebraic machinery involving noncommutative rings.

why_it_matters: The non-abelian Iwasawa main conjecture is a central goal of modern algebraic number theory. It would provide a deep unification of arithmetic invariants with L-values in the most general setting. Coates, Fukaya, Kato, Sujatha, and others have formulated precise versions, and partial results by Kakde and Ritter-Weiss have been achieved, but the general case remains wide open.

canonical.title: The GL_2 main conjecture for elliptic curves without complex multiplication
canonical.locator: Publications Mathematiques de l'IHES, vol. 101, pp. 163-208

status_evidence.title: The main conjecture of Iwasawa theory for totally real fields
status_evidence.locator: Inventiones Mathematicae, vol. 193, pp. 539-626

partial_results.summary: Mazur and Wiles proved the classical (abelian) Iwasawa main conjecture.
Coates-Fukaya-Kato-Sujatha-Venjakob formulated the non-abelian main conjecture precisely.
Kakde proved the non-abelian main conjecture for totally real number fields.
Ritter and Weiss independently proved results for one-dimensional p-adic Lie extensions.

title: Jacobian Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let F = (F_1, ..., F_n): C^n -> C^n be a polynomial map such that the Jacobian determinant det(dF_i/dx_j) is a nonzero constant. Then F is a polynomial automorphism, i.e., F has a polynomial inverse. Equivalently, if F: C^n -> C^n is a polynomial map with constant nonzero Jacobian determinant, then F is bijective.
statement.informal: If you have a system of polynomial equations in n variables whose Jacobian determinant (a measure of local invertibility) is everywhere nonzero, then the system must have a global polynomial inverse -- you can solve for the original variables using polynomials.

why_it_matters: The Jacobian conjecture, posed by Keller in 1939, is one of the most famous open problems in algebraic geometry and commutative algebra. It appears on Smale's list of problems for the 21st century. Despite its simple statement, it has resisted proof even in two dimensions and has surprising connections to many areas including D-modules, the Dixmier conjecture, and affine algebraic geometry.

canonical.title: Ganze Cremona-Transformationen
The Jacobian conjecture: Reduction of degree and formal expansion of the inverse

status_evidence.title: Polynomial Automorphisms and the Jacobian Conjecture

partial_results.summary: Bass, Connell, and Wright, and independently Yagzhev, reduced the conjecture to the case where each F_i has degree at most 3.
Druzkowski further reduced to the case where F(x) = x + (Ax)^{*3} for a nilpotent matrix A.
The conjecture is equivalent to the Dixmier conjecture about endomorphisms of the Weyl algebra.
The conjecture remains open even in dimension 2.

machine_generated.summary: The Jacobian conjecture states that a polynomial map from C^n to C^n with constant nonzero Jacobian determinant must be a polynomial automorphism. It appears on Smale's list and remains open even in dimension 2.

title: Jakobson's Conjecture (Prevalence of Stochastic Behavior)
aliases: Palis Global Conjecture for One-Dimensional Maps

statement.canonical: For a generic smooth one-parameter family of unimodal maps f_a: [0,1] -> [0,1] (such as the quadratic family f_a(x) = ax(1-x)), the set of parameters a for which f_a admits an absolutely continuous invariant probability measure (a.c.i.p.) -- i.e., exhibits stochastic behavior -- has positive Lebesgue measure. More broadly (Palis conjecture), for typical one-dimensional dynamical systems, the parameter space decomposes into a set where the system is hyperbolic (structurally stable) and a set where it supports an a.c.i.p., and together these sets have full Lebesgue measure.
statement.informal: Consider the family of maps x -> ax(1-x) as the parameter a varies. For some values of a the dynamics are orderly (periodic), and for others they are chaotic with a natural probability distribution. The conjecture predicts that "almost all" parameter values fall into one of these two categories -- there is essentially no third type of behavior.

why_it_matters: This conjecture, building on Jakobson's landmark 1981 theorem and extended by Palis, is fundamental to understanding the global structure of parameter spaces in dynamics. It predicts a clean dichotomy between regular and stochastic behavior that would provide a complete probabilistic picture of one-dimensional dynamics. The higher-dimensional generalization (Palis conjecture) is one of the most ambitious open problems in dynamical systems.

canonical.title: Absolutely continuous invariant measures for one-parameter families of one-dimensional maps
canonical.locator: Communications in Mathematical Physics, vol. 81, pp. 39-88

The higher-dimensional Palis conjecture remains wide open, even for Henon maps.

title: Kadison-Kaplansky Conjecture

statement.canonical: For a torsion-free discrete group G, the reduced group C*-algebra C*_r(G) contains no idempotent elements other than 0 and 1. Equivalently, the Kadison-Kaplansky conjecture asserts that for torsion-free groups, the K_0 group of C*_r(G) has no non-trivial projections.
statement.informal: If a discrete group has no elements of finite order (other than the identity), then a certain operator algebra built from the group should have no non-trivial projections.

why_it_matters: The Kadison-Kaplansky conjecture is a fundamental problem in operator algebras and noncommutative geometry. It is implied by the Baum-Connes conjecture, which is one of the most important conjectures connecting topology, geometry, and analysis. Progress on this conjecture has driven the development of K-theory for operator algebras and index theory.

status_evidence.title: Introduction to the Baum-Connes Conjecture

title: Kadison's Similarity Problem

statement.canonical: Let A be a C*-algebra and let pi: A -> B(H) be a bounded unital homomorphism into the bounded operators on a Hilbert space H. Is pi necessarily similar to a *-homomorphism? That is, does there exist an invertible operator S in B(H) such that rho(a) = S^{-1} pi(a) S is a *-homomorphism for all a in A?
statement.informal: When you represent a C*-algebra (a special algebra of operators) as operators on a Hilbert space, a well-behaved representation preserves the adjoint operation. Kadison asked whether every bounded (but not necessarily adjoint-preserving) representation can be "corrected" by a change of basis to become adjoint-preserving.

why_it_matters: Kadison's similarity problem, posed in 1955, is one of the most prominent unsolved problems in operator algebra theory. It is equivalent to several other important problems: every derivation from a C*-algebra into B(H) is inner, and it is related to the Dixmier problem for groups. Haagerup's work showed it holds for cyclic representations and for nuclear C*-algebras.

canonical.title: On the orthogonalization of operator representations

Haagerup proved the problem affirmatively for nuclear C*-algebras.
Christensen proved it for C*-algebras with sufficiently small cb-distance to a *-homomorphism.
Pisier introduced the similarity degree and showed equivalence with bounded derivation problems.

title: Kadison-Singer Problem Extensions (Infinite-Dimensional Paving)
aliases: Kadison-Singer Type Problems for General Algebras

statement.canonical: Does the paving property hold for operators on infinite-dimensional Hilbert spaces relative to more general maximal abelian subalgebras (MASAs) beyond l^infty? Specifically, for a type II_1 factor M with a diffuse MASA A, does every operator T in M with E_A(T) = 0 (where E_A is the conditional expectation onto A) satisfy an appropriate paving condition? More broadly, can the Marcus-Spielman-Srivastava framework be extended to infinite-dimensional settings?
statement.informal: The original Kadison-Singer problem (solved in 2013) asked about extending certain linear functionals on diagonal operators. The solved version dealt with finite-dimensional approximations. The remaining open extensions ask whether similar "paving" (breaking matrices into small blocks that are nearly diagonal) works in truly infinite-dimensional or continuous (type II_1) settings.

why_it_matters: While Marcus, Spielman, and Srivastava resolved the original Kadison-Singer conjecture in 2013, deep generalizations to von Neumann algebras, type II_1 factors, and continuous MASAs remain open. These problems are central to operator algebra theory and connect to quantum information theory, frame theory, and harmonic analysis.

title: Kakeya Conjecture

statement.canonical: A Besicovitch set (Kakeya set) in R^n is a compact set that contains a unit line segment in every direction. The Kakeya conjecture states that every Besicovitch set in R^n has Hausdorff dimension n (and equivalently, Minkowski dimension n).
statement.informal: A Kakeya set is a set in n-dimensional space that contains a unit-length needle pointing in every possible direction. In 2D, Besicovitch showed such sets can have zero area, but the conjecture says in higher dimensions they must be "full-dimensional" in a precise sense.

why_it_matters: The Kakeya conjecture is a central problem in geometric measure theory and harmonic analysis. It has deep connections to restriction estimates for the Fourier transform, Bochner-Riesz summability, local smoothing conjectures, and additive combinatorics. Progress on the Kakeya conjecture has driven advances across multiple areas of analysis.

canonical.title: Sur deux questions d'integrabilite des fonctions

partial_results.summary: The conjecture is fully resolved in dimension 2 (Davies, 1971).
In 3 dimensions, the Kakeya conjecture was proved by Hong Wang and Joshua Zahl.
Katz, Laba, and Tao improved the dimension lower bound to (2n+2)/(2+2) in n dimensions using additive combinatorics.
The finite field analogue of the Kakeya conjecture was proved by Dvir using the polynomial method.

title: Kalai's Conjecture on Noise Sensitivity of Boolean Functions

statement.canonical: Consider a monotone Boolean function f: {0,1}^n -> {0,1} that is balanced (Pr[f=1] ~ 1/2). If f has no "influential" variable (the influence of each variable is at most epsilon), then f is noise-sensitive: for any delta > 0, the correlation between f(x) and f(x^delta) tends to 0 as epsilon -> 0, where x^delta is obtained from x by independently flipping each bit with probability delta. More precisely, f is noise-sensitive if and only if the sum of squared Fourier coefficients above level k tends to zero for fixed k.
statement.informal: Boolean functions where no single variable has much influence are easily corrupted by random noise — a small amount of random bit-flipping destroys the correlation with the original output.

why_it_matters: Kalai's conjecture and related work by Benjamini, Kalai, and Schramm (1999) connects Boolean function analysis to probability theory, social choice theory, and percolation theory. It has applications to the hardness of approximation, voting theory (Arrow's theorem extensions), and critical phenomena in statistical mechanics.

canonical.title: Noise sensitivity of Boolean functions and applications to percolation

status_evidence.title: Noise Sensitivity of Boolean Functions and Percolation

partial_results.summary: Proved for monotone functions by Benjamini, Kalai, and Schramm under certain influence conditions.

title: Kaplansky's Unit Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let K be a field and G a torsion-free group. Then the only units in the group ring K[G] are the trivial units, i.e., elements of the form a·g where a ∈ K \ {0} and g ∈ G.
statement.informal: If you form the group ring of a torsion-free group over a field, the conjecture says the only invertible elements are the obvious ones: nonzero scalar multiples of single group elements.

why_it_matters: Kaplansky's unit conjecture is one of three famous conjectures (along with the zero-divisor and idempotent conjectures) about group rings of torsion-free groups. It is the strongest of the three. A counterexample was found by Gardam (2021) over the field F_2 for a virtually abelian group, but the conjecture remains open over fields of characteristic zero and for many classes of groups.

title: Keating-Snaith Conjecture
aliases: Moments of the Riemann Zeta Function Conjecture

statement.canonical: For each positive real k, the 2k-th moment of the Riemann zeta function on the critical line satisfies (1/T) integral_0^T |zeta(1/2 + it)|^{2k} dt ~ c_k (log T)^{k^2} as T -> infinity, where c_k is an explicit constant predicted by random matrix theory involving Barnes' G-function: c_k = g_k * a_k, with g_k from the CUE ensemble and a_k an arithmetic factor involving primes.
statement.informal: The moments of the Riemann zeta function along the critical line grow like specific powers of log T, with precise constants predicted by modeling the zeta function as a random unitary matrix.

why_it_matters: The Keating-Snaith conjecture (2000) provides a stunning bridge between number theory and random matrix theory. Confirming it would validate the deep analogy between the zeta function and random matrices. Only the cases k = 1 (Hardy-Littlewood) and k = 2 (Ingham) are proved. The conjecture has applications to the distribution of primes and L-functions.

canonical.title: Random matrix theory and zeta(1/2+it)

title: Kervaire Invariant Problem

statement.canonical: Does there exist a framed manifold of dimension 126 with Kervaire invariant one? The Kervaire invariant θ_j ∈ π^s_{2^{j+1}-2} exists only in dimensions 2^{j+1} - 2. Hill, Hopkins, and Ravenel (2016) proved that framed manifolds with Kervaire invariant one do not exist in dimensions 2^{j+1} - 2 for j ≥ 7 (i.e., dimensions ≥ 254). The problem is resolved positively in dimensions 2, 6, 14, 30, and 62, leaving dimension 126 as the sole remaining open case.
statement.informal: A classical topological invariant called the Kervaire invariant is known to be nontrivial in certain specific dimensions. The question of whether it is nontrivial in dimension 126 is the last unsettled case.

why_it_matters: The Kervaire invariant problem is one of the most celebrated questions in stable homotopy theory and geometric topology. The resolution by Hill, Hopkins, and Ravenel of all cases except dimension 126 was a landmark achievement that introduced powerful new techniques in equivariant stable homotopy theory. The remaining dimension 126 case is a fundamental open question with deep connections to exotic spheres and surgery theory.

canonical.title: A manifold which does not admit any differentiable structure

title: Khintchine's Flatness Conjecture

statement.canonical: Determine the optimal flatness constant f(n): the smallest constant such that every convex body K in R^n that contains no integer lattice point in its interior has width at most f(n) in some lattice direction. The flatness theorem (Khintchine, 1948) guarantees f(n) is finite. The conjecture is that f(n) = O(n^{2/3}). Current best bounds give f(n) = O(n^{3/2}) (Banaszczyk) and f(n) = Omega(n^{1/3}) (Kannan-Lovász).
statement.informal: How "thin" must a convex body be in some direction if it avoids all integer points? The precise growth rate of the flatness constant with dimension is unknown.

why_it_matters: The flatness theorem connects the geometry of numbers, integer programming, and Diophantine approximation. It is a key tool in Lenstra's algorithm for integer programming in fixed dimension. Determining the optimal constant has implications for algorithmic efficiency and for understanding the interaction between convex bodies and lattices.

canonical.title: A quantitative formulation of Kronecker's theory of approximation

status_evidence.title: Inequalities for convex bodies and polar reciprocal lattices in R^n

Kannan and Lovász showed f(n) >= c * n^{1/3}.
Rudelson improved bounds in special cases using entropy methods.

title: KLS Conjecture

statement.canonical: For any log-concave probability distribution mu on R^n, the Cheeger isoperimetric constant psi_mu satisfies psi_mu >= c * sigma_mu, where sigma_mu is the square root of the smallest eigenvalue of the covariance matrix and c > 0 is a universal constant independent of n.
statement.informal: For any log-concave probability distribution in high dimensions, the isoperimetric constant (measuring the minimal boundary-to-volume ratio of partitions) is controlled by the variance. The conjecture says no dimension-dependent loss occurs.

why_it_matters: The KLS conjecture (Kannan, Lovasz, Simonovits, 1995) is a cornerstone conjecture in high-dimensional probability and geometry. It implies Bourgain's slicing conjecture and the thin-shell conjecture. It would also yield optimal mixing times for Markov chains on convex bodies, with major implications for sampling and optimization algorithms.

canonical.title: Isoperimetric problems for convex bodies and a localization lemma

title: Kontsevich-Zagier Conjecture

statement.canonical: If two periods (numbers expressible as integrals of algebraic functions over semi-algebraic domains defined over Q) are equal, then there exists a finite sequence of elementary operations — additivity of integrals, change of variables with algebraic Jacobians, and Stokes' theorem — that transforms one integral representation into the other.
statement.informal: All relations between periods (a class of numbers including pi, log(2), and values of the zeta function at integers) should follow from three basic rules of calculus: linearity, substitution, and Stokes' theorem.

why_it_matters: The Kontsevich-Zagier conjecture (2001) proposes a complete set of "axioms" for equality among periods, a vast class of numbers arising throughout mathematics. It would imply the transcendence of many specific numbers and connects to the Grothendieck period conjecture, motives, and mixed Hodge structures. It provides a vision for a "calculus of periods."

status_evidence.title: Periods and Nori Motives

title: Kothe Conjecture

statement.canonical: If a ring R has no nonzero two-sided nil ideal, then R has no nonzero one-sided nil ideal. Equivalently, the sum of two left nil ideals of any ring is again a nil ideal. This can also be stated as: the upper nil radical of any ring contains every left nil ideal.
statement.informal: In a ring (an algebraic structure with addition and multiplication), a nil ideal is one where every element, when raised to some power, gives zero. The conjecture asks: if there is no "global" nil ideal, can there still be a "one-sided" nil ideal?

why_it_matters: The Kothe conjecture, posed in 1930, is one of the oldest and most important open problems in ring theory. It addresses the fundamental structure of nil ideals and radicals in noncommutative rings. Its resolution would clarify the relationship between one-sided and two-sided nil ideals, a basic question in abstract algebra.

canonical.title: Die Struktur der Ringe, deren Restklassenring nach dem Radikal vollstandig reduzibel ist

status_evidence.title: Some results in noncommutative ring theory

Krempa showed the Kothe conjecture is equivalent to the statement that polynomial rings over nil rings are Jacobson radical.
Smoktunowicz constructed a nil ring whose polynomial ring is not nil, but this does not resolve the Kothe conjecture.
Amitsur proved the conjecture for algebras over uncountable fields.

title: Lander-Parkin-Selfridge Conjecture
aliases: Equal Sums of Like Powers Conjecture

statement.canonical: If the sum a_1^k + a_2^k + ... + a_s^k = b_1^k + b_2^k + ... + b_t^k holds with all a_i, b_j positive integers and k >= 2, then s + t >= k. Equivalently, the Diophantine equation x_1^k + x_2^k + ... + x_m^k = 0 in nonzero integers requires m >= k + 1 terms.
statement.informal: When expressing an equation of equal sums of k-th powers on both sides, the total number of terms on both sides combined must be at least k. You cannot write an identity with too few k-th power terms.

why_it_matters: The Lander-Parkin-Selfridge conjecture generalizes Fermat's Last Theorem and Euler's conjecture on sums of powers, providing a unified framework for understanding the minimal complexity of power sum identities.

canonical.title: A survey of equal sums of like powers

status_evidence.title: Unsolved Problems in Number Theory (3rd edition)

partial_results.summary: Euler's conjecture (that a_1^k + ... + a_{k-1}^k = b^k has no solutions) was disproved for k=4 and k=5, but these counterexamples are consistent with LPS.
Fermat's Last Theorem (the case s=2, t=1, k>=3) was proven by Andrew Wiles.
Extensive computational searches have found no counterexamples to the LPS conjecture for k <= 20.

title: Lang's Conjecture on Rational Points
aliases: Lang's Conjecture on Varieties of General Type

statement.canonical: Let X be a smooth projective variety of general type defined over a number field K. Then the set of K-rational points X(K) is not Zariski dense in X. More precisely, there exists a proper closed subvariety Z subset X such that X(K) subset Z(K), i.e., all but finitely many rational points lie on a proper subvariety.
statement.informal: Varieties of general type are the "most curved" algebraic varieties, the higher-dimensional analogues of hyperbolic curves. Lang conjectured that such varieties over number fields can only have a "sparse" set of rational points -- they must all be confined to a fixed lower-dimensional subset. This vastly generalizes Faltings' theorem (Mordell conjecture) from curves to higher dimensions.

why_it_matters: Lang's conjecture, formulated in the 1970s-80s, is one of the grand conjectures in arithmetic geometry linking the geometry of varieties (via Kodaira dimension) to the arithmetic of their rational points. It would unify and extend Faltings' theorem, the Bombieri-Lang conjecture, and provide a geometric framework for understanding Diophantine equations in higher dimensions.

canonical.title: Hyperbolic and Diophantine analysis
canonical.locator: Bulletin of the AMS, vol. 14, no. 2, pp. 159-205

status_evidence.title: Diophantine Geometry: An Introduction
status_evidence.locator: Graduate Texts in Mathematics, vol. 201, Springer

title: Langlands Functoriality Conjecture

statement.canonical: For reductive groups G and H over a number field F, every homomorphism of L-groups r: ^L H -> ^L G should induce a transfer of automorphic representations from H to G. More precisely, if pi is an automorphic representation of H(A_F) with L-parameter phi_pi, then there exists an automorphic representation Pi of G(A_F) with L-parameter r ∘ phi_pi.
statement.informal: There should be a systematic way to transfer automorphic forms between different algebraic groups, governed by homomorphisms between their dual groups. This "functoriality principle" is the central organizing conjecture of the Langlands program.

why_it_matters: The Langlands functoriality conjecture, proposed by Robert Langlands in 1970, is one of the most ambitious conjectures in mathematics. It unifies number theory, representation theory, and algebraic geometry. Specific instances include the Artin conjecture, the Ramanujan-Petersson conjecture, and Serre's modularity conjecture. It subsumes much of modern number theory.

canonical.title: Problems in the theory of automorphic forms

title: Large Cardinal Consistency Strength Hierarchy
aliases: Inner Model Problem for Supercompact Cardinals

statement.canonical: Construct an inner model (a canonical model of set theory analogous to Godel's L) for a supercompact cardinal. More broadly, develop inner model theory for the large cardinal hierarchy at and beyond the level of supercompact cardinals, establishing the consistency strength of these axioms relative to each other and to combinatorial set-theoretic principles.
statement.informal: Large cardinal axioms form a linear hierarchy of increasingly strong assumptions about the universe of sets. Inner model theory provides canonical models that "tame" these axioms. Building inner models has succeeded up to Woodin cardinals, but extending the theory to supercompact cardinals and beyond remains a major open challenge.

why_it_matters: The inner model program is one of the central projects in set theory, aiming to understand the consistency strength of large cardinal axioms. Inner models have been constructed up to the level of Woodin cardinals (by Steel, Mitchell, and others), but extending beyond to supercompact and stronger cardinals is considered one of the hardest and most important open problems in mathematical logic. Success would illuminate the structure of the set- theoretic universe and the relationship between different foundational axioms.

title: Lattice Distortion Problem

statement.canonical: For finite lattices L of size n (as partially ordered sets), what is the minimum distortion required to embed the lattice metric (shortest path distance in the Hasse diagram) into L^1 (Manhattan distance) space? Specifically, determine the worst-case distortion c_1(L_n) = inf_f sup_{x,y} d_{L^1}(f(x),f(y)) / d_L(x,y) * sup_{x,y} d_L(x,y) / d_{L^1}(f(x),f(y)) over all lattices of size n and all embeddings f.
statement.informal: How well can the metric structure of lattices be preserved when embedded into familiar geometric spaces? The worst-case distortion for embedding lattice metrics into L^1 is poorly understood.

why_it_matters: The metric embedding theory of lattices connects order theory, discrete geometry, and theoretical computer science. Low-distortion embeddings have applications to algorithm design (approximation algorithms), data structures, and the geometry of Banach spaces. The distortion of general lattice metrics is a challenging open question.
