title: Lebesgue's Universal Covering Problem

statement.canonical: What is the convex region of smallest area in the plane that can contain a congruent copy of every planar set of diameter one? Such a region is called a universal covering or universal cover.
statement.informal: Find the smallest convex shape that can cover (after suitable rotation and translation) any shape whose widest extent is at most 1.

why_it_matters: Lebesgue's universal covering problem, dating to 1914, is one of the oldest open problems in geometry. Despite its elementary statement, determining the exact minimum area has resisted all attempts. The best bounds have been improved incrementally over more than a century, with the current best upper bound around 0.8441 (Gibbs, 2018).

title: Legendre's Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every positive integer n, there exists a prime number p such that n^2 < p < (n+1)^2. Equivalently, there is always at least one prime between consecutive perfect squares.
statement.informal: Between any two consecutive perfect squares (like 100 and 121, or 144 and 169), there is always at least one prime number. Despite strong numerical evidence, this has never been proved.

why_it_matters: Legendre's conjecture is one of Landau's four "unattackable" problems presented at the 1912 International Congress of Mathematicians. It is weaker than the Cramer conjecture and stronger than Bertrand's postulate. Resolving it would significantly advance our understanding of prime gaps and the distribution of primes in short intervals.

Verified computationally for n up to very large values (exceeding 10^18).

title: Lehmer's Conjecture (Mahler Measure)
aliases: Lehmer's Question on Minimal Mahler Measure

statement.canonical: There exists an absolute constant c > 0 such that for every monic irreducible polynomial P(x) in Z[x] that is not a cyclotomic polynomial, the Mahler measure M(P) = prod_{i} max(1, |alpha_i|), where the alpha_i are the roots of P, satisfies M(P) >= 1 + c. Equivalently, the infimum of Mahler measures of non-cyclotomic integer polynomials is strictly greater than 1, and is conjectured to be Lehmer's number, M(x^10 + x^9 - x^7 - x^6 - x^5 - x^4 - x^3 + x + 1) ~ 1.17628.
statement.informal: The Mahler measure of a polynomial is a way to measure its "size" using its roots. For cyclotomic polynomials (whose roots are roots of unity), this measure equals 1. Lehmer asked whether there is a gap -- is there a minimum amount by which the Mahler measure of any other integer polynomial must exceed 1?

why_it_matters: Lehmer's conjecture, posed in 1933, connects number theory, algebraic dynamics, and harmonic analysis. The Mahler measure appears in heights of algebraic numbers, entropy of algebraic dynamical systems, volumes of hyperbolic 3-manifolds, and values of L-functions. A resolution would have implications across these diverse areas.

canonical.title: Factorization of certain cyclotomic functions
canonical.locator: Annals of Mathematics, vol. 34, no. 3, pp. 461-479

partial_results.summary: Smyth proved the conjecture for non-reciprocal polynomials with the sharp bound M(P) >= M(x^3 - x - 1) ~ 1.3247.

title: Lehmer's Totient Problem

statement.canonical: There is no composite positive integer n such that Euler's totient function phi(n) divides n - 1. Equivalently, if phi(n) | (n - 1), then n is prime.
statement.informal: Euler's totient function phi(n) counts how many integers from 1 to n are coprime to n. For primes p, phi(p) = p - 1, which divides p - 1. The question asks whether any composite number can also have the property that phi(n) divides n - 1.

why_it_matters: Lehmer's totient problem is a fundamental question in multiplicative number theory connecting the structure of Euler's totient function to primality testing. Its resolution would clarify the extent to which the divisibility condition phi(n) | (n-1) characterizes primes.

Any counterexample must exceed 10^30 (current computational bounds).
Luca showed that there are at most finitely many counterexamples with a fixed number of prime factors.

title: Leray's Conjecture on Self-Similar Blow-Up

statement.canonical: There are no non-trivial self-similar blow-up solutions to the three-dimensional incompressible Navier-Stokes equations. Specifically, if u(x,t) = (T-t)^{-1/2} U(x / (T-t)^{1/2}) is a solution to the Navier-Stokes equations that blows up at time T, then U must be identically zero (no non-zero self-similar profile exists with finite energy).
statement.informal: If the Navier-Stokes equations describing fluid flow were to develop a singularity (blow-up), it cannot happen in a self-similar way -- the simplest type of singularity is ruled out.

why_it_matters: Leray's conjecture (1934) is intimately connected to the Navier-Stokes existence and smoothness Millennium Problem. If non-trivial self-similar blow-up solutions were found, it would settle the regularity question negatively. Ruling them out, as conjectured, is a necessary step toward proving regularity. Necas, Ruzicka, Sverak proved it for solutions in L^3, but the general case remains open.

Tsai extended the result to solutions with slightly weaker integrability conditions.
The general case (allowing infinite energy self-similar profiles) remains open.

title: Strongly Polynomial Linear Programming
aliases: Strongly Polynomial Simplex or Interior Point Method

statement.canonical: Does there exist a strongly polynomial-time algorithm for linear programming? That is, an algorithm whose running time is bounded by a polynomial in the number of variables n and number of constraints m only, independent of the bit-size of the coefficients. Current polynomial-time algorithms (ellipsoid method, interior point methods) are weakly polynomial — their complexity depends on log of the input numbers.
statement.informal: Can linear programs be solved in a number of steps that depends only on the number of variables and constraints, not on the size of the numbers involved?

why_it_matters: This is Smale's 9th problem for the 21st century and one of the most important open questions in mathematical optimization. Linear programming is ubiquitous in operations research, economics, and engineering. A strongly polynomial algorithm would be a fundamental advance in the theory of computation and optimization, resolving one of the deepest questions about the complexity of a problem we solve billions of times daily.

No strongly polynomial algorithm is known even for minimum-cost flow (a special case).

title: List Coloring Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every multigraph G, the list chromatic index equals the chromatic index: chi'_l(G) = chi'(G). Equivalently, if the edges of a graph can be properly colored with k colors, then they can be properly colored even if each edge has its own list of k available colors.
statement.informal: Edge coloring with lists is no harder than ordinary edge coloring. If k colors suffice for a proper edge coloring, then you can still color properly even when each edge is restricted to its own palette of k colors.

why_it_matters: The List Coloring Conjecture, independently posed by several researchers in the 1970s and 1980s, is a major open problem connecting list coloring with ordinary coloring. If true, it would unify many results about edge-coloring. It implies that Vizing's theorem extends to list edge-coloring: the list chromatic index of a simple graph is at most Delta + 1.

title: Littlewood Conjecture
aliases: Littlewood's Conjecture on Simultaneous Diophantine Approximation

statement.canonical: For every pair of real numbers alpha, beta, the limit inferior lim inf_{n -> infinity} n * ||n*alpha|| * ||n*beta|| = 0, where ||x|| denotes the distance from x to the nearest integer.
statement.informal: For any two irrational numbers alpha and beta, one can find integers n making n, n*alpha, and n*beta all simultaneously close to integers, in the sense that the product of the three distances tends to zero.

why_it_matters: Littlewood's conjecture (circa 1930) is a central problem in Diophantine approximation and dynamics on homogeneous spaces. Einsiedler, Katok, and Lindenstrauss (2006) showed that the set of counterexamples has Hausdorff dimension zero using measure rigidity on SL(3,R)/SL(3,Z), work contributing to Lindenstrauss's Fields Medal. The conjecture remains open.

title: Log-Brunn-Minkowski Conjecture

statement.canonical: For origin-symmetric convex bodies K and L in R^n and lambda in [0,1], the inequality Vol((1-lambda) cdot K +_0 lambda cdot L) >= Vol(K)^{1-lambda} Vol(L)^lambda holds, where +_0 denotes the log-Minkowski addition (geometric mean of support functions). Equivalently, the cone-volume measure satisfies a log-concavity property stronger than the classical Brunn-Minkowski inequality.
statement.informal: The conjecture proposes a multiplicative (logarithmic) strengthening of the classical Brunn-Minkowski inequality for origin-symmetric convex bodies, replacing the usual Minkowski addition with a geometric-mean operation on support functions.

why_it_matters: The log-Brunn-Minkowski conjecture, proposed by Boroczky, Lutwak, Yang, and Zhang (2012), would unify and strengthen several fundamental inequalities in convex geometry. It implies the B-conjecture, the log-Minkowski inequality, and the uniqueness of the cone-volume measure. It represents the deepest conjectured strengthening of the Brunn-Minkowski inequality.

title: Lonely Runner Conjecture

statement.canonical: Consider k >= 2 runners on a circular track of circumference 1, starting at the same point and running with pairwise distinct constant speeds. For each runner, there exists a time at which that runner is at distance at least 1/k from all other runners. Equivalently, for every set of k-1 positive integers v_1, ..., v_{k-1}, there exists a real number t such that for all i, the fractional part of t * v_i is in [1/k, (k-1)/k].
statement.informal: Imagine k runners jogging around a circular track, all starting at the same point but at different constant speeds. The conjecture says that each runner will, at some point in time, be "lonely" -- separated from every other runner by at least 1/k of the track's length.

why_it_matters: The lonely runner conjecture, posed by Wills (1967) and independently by Cusick (1982), is a beautiful problem at the intersection of combinatorics, number theory, and Diophantine approximation. It connects to view-obstruction problems in geometry, to flows in graphs, and to chromatic numbers of distance graphs.

title: Mahler Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every centrally symmetric convex body K in R^n, the volume product Vol(K) * Vol(K^o) >= 4^n / n!, where K^o is the polar body of K. Equality is conjectured to hold if and only if K is a hypercube (or its affine image). In the non-symmetric case, the conjectured minimum is (n+1)^{n+1} / (n!)^2, achieved by the simplex.
statement.informal: Among all centrally symmetric convex bodies, the hypercube (and its dual, the cross-polytope) should minimize the product of the volume of the body and the volume of its polar dual.

why_it_matters: Mahler's conjecture (1939) is a central open problem in convex geometry and geometric functional analysis. The reverse direction (the Blaschke-Santalo inequality, maximizing the volume product) is long established. The conjecture has connections to number theory, symplectic geometry, and information theory.

title: Matrix Mortality Problem

statement.canonical: Given a finite set of n x n integer matrices {M_1, ..., M_k}, is it decidable whether there exists a product M_{i_1} M_{i_2} ... M_{i_m} equal to the zero matrix? This is known to be undecidable for n >= 3 (Paterson, 1970). The question is open for 2x2 matrices: given a finite set of 2x2 integer matrices, is the mortality problem (does some product equal the zero matrix?) decidable?
statement.informal: Given a collection of 2x2 integer matrices, can we always determine whether some product of these matrices (with repetition allowed) gives the zero matrix?

why_it_matters: The matrix mortality problem is a fundamental question in decidability theory and non-commutative algebra. It connects automata theory, the theory of matrix semigroups, and computational complexity. The 2x2 case remains one of the most prominent open decidability questions, with connections to the Post correspondence problem and word problems in semigroups.

canonical.title: Unsolvability in 3x3 matrices

title: MLC Conjecture (Mandelbrot Set Locally Connected)
aliases: Local Connectivity of the Mandelbrot Set

statement.canonical: The Mandelbrot set M = {c in C : the orbit of 0 under f_c(z) = z^2 + c is bounded} is locally connected. That is, for every point c in M and every open set U containing c, there exists a connected open set V with c in V subset U intersect M.
statement.informal: The Mandelbrot set is the famous fractal that catalogs the behavior of quadratic maps z -> z^2 + c. The MLC conjecture asserts that this set has no "infinitely thin filaments" -- every point can be surrounded by an arbitrarily small connected neighborhood within the set.

why_it_matters: The MLC conjecture is perhaps the most important open problem in holomorphic dynamics. If true, it would imply that the Mandelbrot set is topologically completely described by Thurston's combinatorial model, that the Fatou conjecture (density of hyperbolicity) holds for quadratic polynomials, and that every quadratic polynomial is combinatorially rigid. It would provide a complete topological classification of quadratic dynamics.

title: Moving Sofa Problem

statement.canonical: What is the largest area of a connected planar region that can be moved around a right-angled corner in an L-shaped hallway of unit width? The region must be translated and rotated continuously through the hallway without overlap.
statement.informal: What is the largest sofa (measured by area) that can be moved around a right-angle turn in a hallway of fixed width?

why_it_matters: The moving sofa problem is a deceptively simple geometric optimization problem that has remained open since 1966. It exemplifies the difficulty of optimization over shapes and continuous motions. The best known lower bound is Gerver's sofa (area ≈ 2.2195) from 1992, and Romik's ambidextrous sofa handles a symmetric variant. A 2024 preprint by Jineon Baek claims to prove that Gerver's sofa is optimal, but the proof is not yet fully verified.

title: n^2 + 1 Prime Conjecture

statement.canonical: There are infinitely many primes of the form n^2 + 1 where n is a positive integer. Equivalently, the polynomial f(x) = x^2 + 1 takes prime values for infinitely many positive integers x.
statement.informal: If you take perfect squares 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ... and add 1, you get 2, 5, 10, 17, 26, ... Some of these are prime (2, 5, 17, ...). The conjecture says there are infinitely many primes in this sequence.

why_it_matters: This is one of Landau's four problems presented at the 1912 International Congress of Mathematicians. It is the simplest open case of the Bunyakovsky conjecture for a specific quadratic polynomial and represents a fundamental barrier in our understanding of prime values of polynomials.

title: Navier-Stokes Existence and Smoothness

statement.canonical: In three spatial dimensions, given any initial velocity field v_0(x) that is smooth and divergence-free (and decays sufficiently rapidly at infinity), prove one of the following: (A) There exist smooth functions v(x,t) (velocity) and p(x,t) (pressure) defined for all t > 0 that satisfy the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations dv/dt + (v . nabla)v = -nabla p + nu Delta v, div v = 0, with v(x, 0) = v_0(x), and v has bounded energy for all t > 0; or (B) There exists a smooth, divergence-free initial condition v_0 for which no such smooth solution exists for all t > 0 (i.e., singularities develop in finite time).
statement.informal: The Navier-Stokes equations describe the motion of fluids like water and air. In three dimensions, we do not know whether smooth solutions always exist for all time, or whether the fluid can develop a "singularity" where the velocity becomes infinite. This problem asks for a definitive answer.

why_it_matters: The Navier-Stokes existence and smoothness problem is one of the seven Millennium Prize Problems. The Navier-Stokes equations are the fundamental equations governing fluid dynamics and are used throughout science and engineering. Understanding whether solutions can blow up in finite time is crucial for the mathematical foundations of fluid mechanics and has implications for turbulence theory.

title: Global Regularity of 3D Euler Equations

statement.canonical: Do there exist smooth initial data for the three-dimensional incompressible Euler equations (partial_t u + (u . nabla)u + nabla p = 0, div u = 0) on R^3 or T^3 such that the solution develops a singularity (blow-up of the vorticity) in finite time?

Starting from smooth initial conditions, does the fluid flow always remain smooth, or can it develop infinite vorticity in finite time? This is one of the fundamental open problems in mathematical fluid dynamics.

why_it_matters: The blow-up problem for the 3D Euler equations is one of the most important open problems in mathematical physics and PDE theory. It is closely related to the Navier-Stokes regularity problem (one of the Millennium Prize Problems). Understanding Euler blow-up would shed light on turbulence, energy cascades, and the physical limits of classical fluid mechanics. Recent work by Elgindi (2021) established blow-up for certain classes of initial data with limited regularity, and Chen-Hou (2023) provided computer-assisted proofs of blow-up for C^{1,alpha} data.

title: Neggers-Stanley Conjecture

statement.canonical: For a finite poset P, the order polynomial Omega(P, t) (counting the number of order-preserving maps from P to {1, 2, ..., t}) has only real zeros when expressed in the basis of binomial coefficients. Equivalently, the W-polynomial (a transformation of the order polynomial) has only real roots. The original Neggers conjecture (for naturally labeled posets) was disproved; the modified version due to Stanley remains open.
statement.informal: A polynomial that counts the number of ways to assign values from {1,...,t} to elements of a partially ordered set in an order-respecting way should, after a suitable transformation, have only real roots.

why_it_matters: The Neggers-Stanley conjecture connects enumerative combinatorics with the theory of real-rooted polynomials. Real-rootedness implies log-concavity and unimodality, which are central topics in algebraic combinatorics. The conjecture stimulates research on P-partitions, Ehrhart theory, and symmetric function theory.

title: Consistency of Quine's New Foundations

statement.canonical: Is Quine's set theory NF (New Foundations) consistent relative to standard set-theoretic axioms? NF is the set theory with a universal set, stratified comprehension, and extensionality. The question is whether NF is consistent relative to ZFC or to some natural extension of ZFC. Holmes (2012+) has claimed a proof of consistency of NF, which is being formalized in Lean, but the proof has not yet been fully verified and published in a peer-reviewed journal.
statement.informal: Quine proposed an alternative set theory in 1937 that allows a universal set (the set of all sets). Despite decades of study, nobody has been able to prove whether this theory is consistent. A claimed proof exists but has not been fully verified.

why_it_matters: NF is one of the oldest alternative set theories, proposed by Quine in 1937. Despite nearly 90 years of study, its consistency relative to standard axioms remains unclear. The problem is intimately connected to the foundations of mathematics, the nature of stratification, and questions about the cumulative hierarchy vs. universal set approaches to set theory. A formalized proof would be a landmark in the foundations of mathematics.

title: Nonnegative Rank vs Extension Complexity

statement.canonical: For a polytope P, the extension complexity xc(P) is the minimum number of facets of any polytope Q that linearly projects onto P. Yannakakis showed xc(P) equals the nonnegative rank of the slack matrix of P. The open question is to determine the extension complexity of fundamental polytopes: in particular, does the matching polytope of K_n have extension complexity 2^{Omega(n)}, and what is the extension complexity of the TSP polytope?

why_it_matters: Extension complexity bridges combinatorial optimization and convex geometry. Rothvoss (2017) proved that the matching polytope has exponential extension complexity, resolving a major open question. But many related questions remain: the extension complexity of the TSP polytope, the cut polytope, and the role of semidefinite extended formulations are open.

title: Novikov Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let M be a closed oriented smooth manifold with fundamental group π. For any class x ∈ H^*(Bπ; Q) in the rational cohomology of the classifying space, the higher signature ⟨L(M) ∪ f^*(x), [M]⟩ is an oriented homotopy invariant, where L(M) is the Hirzebruch L-class and f: M → Bπ classifies the universal cover.
statement.informal: Certain numerical invariants of manifolds defined using their fundamental group (called higher signatures) should be preserved when one manifold is continuously deformed into another in a way that preserves orientation.

why_it_matters: The Novikov conjecture is one of the most important open problems in topology. It implies that a large class of topological invariants are actually homotopy invariants. The conjecture has been verified for many classes of groups and has driven the development of major areas including index theory, K-theory of group C*-algebras, and coarse geometry.

canonical.title: Topological invariance of rational classes of Pontrjagin
Novikov Conjectures, Index Theorems and Rigidity

status_evidence.title: Equivariant KK-theory and the Novikov conjecture

partial_results.summary: Lusztig verified the conjecture for free abelian groups using analytic methods.
Kasparov proved the conjecture for discrete subgroups of Lie groups.
Connes, Gromov, and Moscovici proved it for hyperbolic groups.
Yu proved the conjecture for groups that admit a uniform embedding into Hilbert space.
The full conjecture remains open for general groups.

machine_generated.summary: The Novikov conjecture asserts that higher signatures of closed oriented manifolds are oriented homotopy invariants. Verified for many classes of groups, the full conjecture remains open.
decomposition: Analytic approaches via Kasparov's KK-theory
Coarse geometric methods and uniform embeddings
Assembly maps and the Baum-Connes conjecture

title: Overfull Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let G be a graph with maximum degree Delta(G) > n/3, where n = |V(G)|. Then the chromatic index chi'(G) = Delta(G) + 1 (i.e., G is Class 2) if and only if G contains an overfull subgraph H with Delta(H) = Delta(G). A graph H is overfull if |E(H)| > Delta(H) * floor(|V(H)|/2).
statement.informal: For graphs with sufficiently high maximum degree, the only reason a graph needs one extra color for edge-coloring (beyond its maximum degree) is if it contains a dense substructure (an overfull subgraph) that forces this.

why_it_matters: The Overfull conjecture, posed by Chetwynd and Hilton in 1986, would provide a complete characterization of the chromatic index for graphs with large maximum degree. By Vizing's theorem, every simple graph has chromatic index Delta or Delta + 1. Determining which case holds is NP-complete in general, but the Overfull conjecture implies it can be decided in polynomial time for dense graphs.

canonical.title: Star multigraphs with three vertices of maximum degree

status_evidence.title: Edge-colorings

partial_results.summary: The conjecture is proved for graphs with Delta(G) >= (2/3)|V(G)| by Chetwynd and Hilton.
Plantholt and Tipnis proved the conjecture for graphs with Delta(G) >= (sqrt(7/2))|V(G)|^{1/2}.
The conjecture holds for regular graphs (a regular graph is overfull if and only if it has odd order).

machine_generated.summary: The Overfull conjecture characterizes when a dense graph needs Delta+1 colors for edge-coloring: precisely when it contains an overfull subgraph. It is proved for graphs with maximum degree at least 2n/3 but remains open in general.
decomposition: Reducing the degree threshold for the conjecture
Algorithmic implications for chromatic index computation
Connections to Goldberg's conjecture on multigraph edge-coloring

title: Pierce-Birkhoff Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every piecewise-polynomial function f: R^n -> R (i.e., a continuous function such that R^n can be partitioned into finitely many closed semialgebraic regions on each of which f agrees with a polynomial) can be expressed as a finite combination of polynomials using only the operations of max and min. Equivalently, f belongs to the lattice generated by the polynomial ring R[x_1,...,x_n] under sup and inf operations.
statement.informal: Any continuous function that is polynomial on each piece of a finite partition of R^n can be written using max and min of finitely many polynomials.

why_it_matters: The Pierce-Birkhoff conjecture (1956) lies at the intersection of real algebraic geometry, commutative algebra, and lattice-ordered rings. It is proved for n <= 2 by Mahé (1984) but remains open in higher dimensions. It connects to the theory of f-rings, real spectra, and semialgebraic geometry.

canonical.title: Lattice-ordered rings

status_evidence.title: On the Pierce-Birkhoff conjecture

partial_results.summary: Proved for n <= 2 by Mahé using the real spectrum.
Madden reformulated the conjecture in terms of the real spectrum of polynomial rings.
Lucas, Madden, Schaub, and Spivakovsky established the Connectedness Conjecture in dimension 3, a key step toward Pierce-Birkhoff.

machine_generated.summary: The Pierce-Birkhoff conjecture asserts piecewise-polynomial functions are sup-inf combinations of polynomials. Proved for n <= 2; open in higher dimensions.
decomposition: Real spectrum and semialgebraic techniques
Connectedness conjectures and separating ideals
Lattice-ordered ring theory

title: Pillai's Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every positive integer k, there are only finitely many pairs of perfect powers (a^m, b^n) with a, b, m, n >= 2 and |a^m - b^n| = k. Equivalently, the gaps between consecutive perfect powers tend to infinity.
statement.informal: Perfect powers are numbers like 4, 8, 9, 16, 25, 27, ... (squares, cubes, etc.). Pillai's conjecture says that as you go further out, consecutive perfect powers get increasingly far apart, so any fixed gap k occurs only finitely many times.

why_it_matters: Pillai's conjecture generalizes Catalan's conjecture (now Mihailescu's theorem) and is a central open problem in the theory of exponential Diophantine equations. It is implied by the abc conjecture and connects to deep questions about integral points on curves.

canonical.title: On the equation 2^x - 3^y = 2^X + 3^Y
On A^x - B^y = C

status_evidence.title: Perfect powers: Pillai's works and their developments

partial_results.summary: Mihailescu proved Catalan's conjecture: the only solution to x^p - y^q = 1 with x, y, p, q >= 2 is 3^2 - 2^3 = 1.
The abc conjecture implies Pillai's conjecture (Tijdeman, 1976 and later work).
Specific cases like |2^m - 3^n| = k have been resolved for many small values of k.

machine_generated.summary: Pillai's conjecture generalizes Catalan's conjecture, asserting that the gaps between consecutive perfect powers tend to infinity.
decomposition: Deriving effective bounds from Baker's theory of linear forms in logarithms
Reducing to the abc conjecture and analyzing implications
Computational enumeration of solutions for specific bases and exponents

title: Polya Conjecture on Dirichlet Characters

statement.canonical: For a non-principal Dirichlet character chi modulo q, the maximum of |sum_{n<=x} chi(n)| over all x should be bounded by O(sqrt(q) * log log q). The classical Polya-Vinogradov inequality gives the bound O(sqrt(q) * log q). The conjecture asserts that the log q factor can be replaced by log log q, at least for primitive characters.
statement.informal: Sums of Dirichlet characters (which generalize the Legendre symbol) should be much smaller than what the classical Polya-Vinogradov inequality proves. The conjecture sharpens the best known general bound.

why_it_matters: Improving the Polya-Vinogradov inequality is a central goal in analytic number theory. Better character sum bounds would improve results on the least quadratic residue, the distribution of primes in arithmetic progressions, and many sieve-theoretic applications. Montgomery and Vaughan proved the conjectured bound assuming GRH.

canonical.title: Uber die Verteilung der quadratischen Reste und Nichtreste

status_evidence.title: Large character sums: pretentious characters and the Polya-Vinogradov theorem

partial_results.summary: Montgomery and Vaughan proved the O(sqrt(q) log log q) bound assuming GRH.
Granville and Soundararajan improved the unconditional bound for characters of odd order.
Goldmakher proved the conjectured bound for characters of fixed order.

machine_generated.summary: The conjecture sharpens the Polya-Vinogradov inequality for character sums from O(sqrt(q) log q) to O(sqrt(q) log log q). Proved under GRH and for fixed-order characters.
decomposition: Pretentious number theory and multiplicative functions
GRH-conditional and unconditional approaches
Connections to least quadratic residue bounds

title: Ramsey Number R(5,5)

statement.canonical: Determine the exact value of the Ramsey number R(5,5), the smallest integer n such that every 2-coloring of the edges of the complete graph K_n contains a monochromatic K_5. It is currently known that 43 <= R(5,5) <= 48.
statement.informal: What is the smallest number of people you need to invite to a party to guarantee that either 5 of them all know each other, or 5 of them are all mutual strangers? The answer is known to be between 43 and 48.

why_it_matters: Determining exact Ramsey numbers is notoriously difficult. Only R(1,k), R(2,k), R(3,k) for small k, and R(4,4)=18 are known exactly. Finding R(5,5) has been called one of the hardest open problems in combinatorics. Paul Erdos famously said that if aliens demanded we compute R(5,5) or face destruction, we should mobilize all our computers; but if they asked for R(6,6), we should prepare to fight.

canonical.title: On a problem of formal logic
Small Ramsey numbers

partial_results.summary: Erdos and Szekeres proved R(s,t) <= C(s+t-2, s-1), giving R(5,5) <= 70.
McKay and Radziszowski showed R(5,5) >= 43 using computational methods.
Angeltveit and McKay improved the upper bound to R(5,5) <= 48.
Vigleik Angeltveit improved certain flag algebra bounds related to the upper bound.

machine_generated.summary: The Ramsey number R(5,5) is the smallest n such that every 2-coloring of K_n contains a monochromatic K_5. Currently known bounds: 43 <= R(5,5) <= 48. Determining its exact value is one of the most prominent open problems in Ramsey theory.

title: Anderson Localization in Higher Dimensions

statement.canonical: Consider the Anderson model H = -Δ + V on ℓ^2(Z^d) where V is an i.i.d. random potential with bounded distribution. For d ≥ 3 and sufficiently small disorder (small coupling constant λ), does the operator H have absolutely continuous spectrum and extended (delocalized) eigenstates? Prove the existence of a mobility edge separating localized and extended states in dimensions d ≥ 3.
statement.informal: In a disordered quantum system on a lattice, particles can get trapped (localized) by randomness. In low dimensions or with strong disorder, all states are localized. In higher dimensions with weak disorder, there should be a transition where some states become delocalized — waves can propagate. Proving this transition exists is one of the great open problems in mathematical physics.

why_it_matters: The Anderson localization-delocalization transition is one of the most important open problems in mathematical physics and probability theory. Anderson's 1958 prediction of localization won him the Nobel Prize in Physics. While localization has been rigorously proved (Fröhlich-Spencer, Aizenman-Molchanov), the existence of delocalized states and the mobility edge in d >= 3 remains completely open from a rigorous mathematical standpoint.

canonical.title: Absence of Diffusion in Certain Random Lattices
Absence of diffusion in the Anderson tight binding model for large disorder or low energy

status_evidence.title: Random Operators: Disorder Effects on Quantum Spectra and Dynamics

partial_results.summary: Fröhlich and Spencer proved localization for large disorder and at band edges via multi-scale analysis.
Aizenman and Molchanov proved localization via the fractional moment method.
Erdős, Knowles, Yau, and Yin proved delocalization for random band matrices of sufficient width.
The existence of extended states and mobility edges for the Anderson model on Z^d remains open for all d ≥ 3.

machine_generated.summary: Proving the existence of delocalized states and a mobility edge for the Anderson model on Z^d (d >= 3) with weak disorder is a major open problem in mathematical physics and probability.
decomposition: Supersymmetric methods and sigma models
Random band matrix analogues and delocalization
Multi-scale analysis extensions and renormalization group approaches

title: Reconstruction Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every simple finite undirected graph G on n >= 3 vertices is determined up to isomorphism by its deck, the multiset of (isomorphism classes of) vertex-deleted subgraphs {G - v : v in V(G)}. In other words, if two graphs G and H with at least 3 vertices have the same deck, then G and H are isomorphic.
statement.informal: Imagine you have a graph and you create a collection of smaller graphs, each obtained by removing one vertex (and all its edges). The conjecture says that you can always figure out the original graph from this collection of subgraphs, as long as it has at least 3 vertices.

why_it_matters: The reconstruction conjecture, posed by Kelly and Ulam in 1941-1942, is one of the foremost unsolved problems in graph theory. It asks a fundamental question about the information content of local substructures of a graph and has deep connections to graph isomorphism and structural graph theory.

canonical.title: On isometric transformations
A Collection of Mathematical Problems

status_evidence.title: A graph reconstructor's manual

partial_results.summary: The conjecture is verified for all graphs with at most 13 vertices.
Regular graphs, trees, disconnected graphs, and unit interval graphs are known to be reconstructible.
Muller (1977) proved that almost all graphs are reconstructible.
Bollobas proved that almost all graphs can be reconstructed from just three cards in the deck.

machine_generated.summary: The reconstruction conjecture asserts that every simple graph on at least 3 vertices is uniquely determined (up to isomorphism) by its multiset of vertex-deleted subgraphs. It is one of the foremost open problems in graph theory.
decomposition: Algebraic and polynomial-based reconstruction methods
Extending computational verification beyond 13 vertices
Probabilistic and counting arguments for graph parameters recoverable from the deck

title: Resolution of Singularities in Positive Characteristic
aliases: Resolution of Singularities in Characteristic p

statement.canonical: Does every algebraic variety over a field of positive characteristic p admit a resolution of singularities? That is, given an integral scheme X of finite type over a field k with char(k) = p > 0, does there exist a proper birational morphism Y → X with Y smooth?
statement.informal: Every algebraic variety (solution set of polynomial equations) over a field of characteristic zero can be "desingularized" — replaced by a smooth variety that is birationally equivalent. Can the same always be done when the characteristic of the field is a prime number?

why_it_matters: Hironaka's celebrated 1964 proof of resolution of singularities in characteristic zero is one of the landmarks of algebraic geometry. Extending this to positive characteristic is one of the most important open problems in the field. Resolution of singularities has deep applications to number theory (via arithmetic geometry), the minimal model program, and the classification of algebraic varieties.

canonical.title: Resolution of Singularities of an Algebraic Variety over a Field of Characteristic Zero
On the problem of resolution of singularities in positive characteristic

status_evidence.title: Resolution of Singularities of Arithmetical Threefolds

partial_results.summary: Hironaka resolved singularities in characteristic zero in all dimensions.
Abhyankar proved resolution for surfaces in all characteristics and for 3-folds in characteristic > 5.
Cossart and Piltant proved resolution for 3-folds in all characteristics.
The problem remains open for varieties of dimension ≥ 4 in characteristic p > 0.

machine_generated.summary: The problem asks whether every algebraic variety over a field of positive characteristic admits a resolution of singularities. Known in dimension up to 3, but open for dimension >= 4.
decomposition: Extending Hironaka's inductive approach to positive characteristic
Local uniformization and valuation-theoretic methods
Connections to the minimal model program in mixed characteristic

title: Riemann Hypothesis

statement.canonical: All non-trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function zeta(s) = sum_{n=1}^{infinity} n^{-s} have real part equal to 1/2. That is, if zeta(s) = 0 and s is not a negative even integer, then Re(s) = 1/2.
statement.informal: The Riemann zeta function encodes deep information about the distribution of prime numbers. The hypothesis states that all its "interesting" zeros lie on a single vertical line in the complex plane (the critical line Re(s) = 1/2).

why_it_matters: The Riemann hypothesis is widely regarded as the most important unsolved problem in pure mathematics. It is one of the seven Millennium Prize Problems. Its truth would imply the best possible error bound in the Prime Number Theorem and would have profound consequences across number theory, random matrix theory, quantum physics, and cryptography.

canonical.title: Ueber die Anzahl der Primzahlen unter einer gegebenen Groesse
The Riemann Hypothesis (Millennium Prize Problem description)

status_evidence.title: The Riemann Hypothesis

partial_results.summary: Hardy proved infinitely many zeros lie on the critical line.
Selberg proved a positive proportion of zeros lie on the critical line.
Conrey proved at least 40% of non-trivial zeros lie on the critical line.
Over 10^13 non-trivial zeros have been verified computationally to lie on the critical line.

machine_generated.summary: The Riemann hypothesis states that all non-trivial zeros of the Riemann zeta function have real part 1/2. It is the most important open problem in pure mathematics, with far-reaching implications for prime number distribution.
decomposition: Spectral and operator-theoretic approaches linking zeros to eigenvalues
Random matrix theory connections and statistical distribution of zeros
Function-theoretic and de Branges space methods for zero-free regions

title: Ringel's Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every tree T with n edges, the complete graph K_{2n+1} can be decomposed into 2n+1 edge-disjoint copies of T. Equivalently, every tree with n edges packs 2n+1 times into the complete graph K_{2n+1}.
statement.informal: Any tree with n edges can tile the complete graph on 2n+1 vertices perfectly -- the edges of the complete graph can be partitioned into 2n+1 copies of the tree with no overlaps.

why_it_matters: Ringel's conjecture (1963) is a foundational problem in graph theory and combinatorial design. It generalizes the well-known fact that K_{2n+1} can be decomposed into Hamiltonian cycles. Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov proved it for sufficiently large n (2021), but the conjecture remains open for small and moderate values of n for all trees.

canonical.title: Problem 25

status_evidence.title: A proof of Ringel's Conjecture

partial_results.summary: Montgomery, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov proved the conjecture for all sufficiently large n.
Previously known for paths, stars, caterpillars, and various special tree families.
The related graceful tree conjecture (Kotzig-Ringel) remains open and would imply this conjecture.

machine_generated.summary: Ringel's conjecture states that K_{2n+1} decomposes into copies of any n-edge tree. Proved for large n; open for explicit small cases and for all trees uniformly.
decomposition: Absorption method and randomized algebraic constructions
Explicit constructions for specific tree families
Connections to graceful labeling

title: Rota-Welsh Conjecture (Extensions)

statement.canonical: The original Rota-Welsh conjecture — that the absolute values of coefficients of the characteristic polynomial of any matroid form a log-concave sequence — was proved by Adiprasito, Huh, and Katz (2018). The extended conjecture asks whether the same sequence is ultra-log-concave: |a_k|^2 >= (1 + 1/k)(1 + 1/(r-k)) |a_{k-1}| |a_{k+1}| for all relevant k, where r is the rank. This stronger property remains open for general matroids.
statement.informal: The coefficients of the characteristic polynomial of a matroid are log-concave (proved). The remaining open question is whether a stronger "ultra-log-concavity" property holds, which would imply even tighter control on coefficient ratios.

why_it_matters: The resolution of the Rota-Welsh conjecture by Adiprasito, Huh, and Katz was a landmark achievement connecting combinatorics to algebraic geometry (June Huh's Fields Medal 2022). The remaining ultra-log-concavity extension would complete the picture and has implications for combinatorial probability and negative dependence theory.

canonical.title: Combinatorial theory, old and new

status_evidence.title: Hodge theory for combinatorial geometries

partial_results.summary: Adiprasito, Huh, and Katz proved log-concavity for all matroids.
Ultra-log-concavity proved for representable matroids by Anari, Liu, Oveis Gharan, Vinzant.
Ultra-log-concavity for general matroids remains open.

machine_generated.summary: The Rota-Welsh log-concavity conjecture is proved. The remaining extension asks for ultra-log-concavity of characteristic polynomial coefficients for all matroids.
decomposition: Combinatorial Hodge theory and Lorentzian polynomials
Negative dependence and strongly Rayleigh measures
Extension to non-representable matroids

title: Rota's Basis Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let B_1, B_2, ..., B_n be n bases of a rank-n matroid M. Then one can always find n disjoint transversals of (B_1, ..., B_n), each of which is itself a basis of M. Equivalently, if the elements of the n bases are arranged as rows of an n x n matrix, one can permute within each row so that every column is also a basis.
statement.informal: Given n bases of a matroid, each with n elements, arrange them in a grid. Can you always rearrange each row so that every column also forms a basis? Rota conjectured yes.

why_it_matters: Rota's basis conjecture, attributed to Gian-Carlo Rota in the 1980s, is a fundamental open problem in matroid theory and combinatorics. It connects to Latin squares, scheduling theory, and algebraic combinatorics. It has inspired significant work on partial results and has been called one of the most beautiful conjectures in matroid theory.

canonical.title: On the relations of various conjectures on Latin squares and straightening coefficients

status_evidence.title: On Rota's basis conjecture

partial_results.summary: The conjecture is known to hold for paving matroids and for strongly base-orderable matroids.
Geelen and Humphries proved one can always find at least 2 disjoint common transversals that are bases.
Wild proved the conjecture for matroids of rank at most 3.
Bucic, Kwan, Pokrovskiy, and Sudakov proved that at least (1/2 - o(1))n disjoint transversal bases exist.

machine_generated.summary: Rota's basis conjecture states that n bases of a rank-n matroid can be arranged in an n x n matrix so that every column is also a basis. It remains open despite significant partial results.
decomposition: Proving the conjecture for graphic matroids or representable matroids
Improving the fraction of disjoint transversal bases beyond 1/2

title: Ryser's Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every Latin square of odd order n has a transversal (a set of n entries, one from each row and one from each column, with all symbols distinct). More generally, the Ryser-Brualdi-Stein conjecture asserts that every Latin square of order n has a partial transversal of size at least n - 1.
statement.informal: In an n x n Latin square (where each symbol appears exactly once in each row and column), can you always find n entries, one per row and one per column, that are all different? Ryser conjectured yes when n is odd; the Brualdi-Stein extension asks for n - 1 such entries in all cases.

why_it_matters: Ryser's conjecture, posed in 1967, is a central open problem in combinatorics with connections to design theory, graph decompositions, proper edge colorings of bipartite graphs, and rainbow matchings. It has inspired a large body of work on transversals in Latin squares and more general combinatorial structures.

canonical.title: Neuere Probleme der Kombinatorik
Incomplete transversals in Latin squares

status_evidence.title: A proof of the Ryser-Brualdi-Stein conjecture for large even n

partial_results.summary: Hatami and Shor proved every Latin square of order n has a partial transversal of size n - O(log^2 n).
The conjecture is verified for small orders by exhaustive computation.
Keevash, Pokrovskiy, Sudakov, and Yepremyan proved the Brualdi-Stein conjecture for large n (existence of transversal of size n-1).
Montgomery proved the full Ryser-Brualdi-Stein conjecture for large even n.

machine_generated.summary: Ryser's conjecture asserts that every Latin square of odd order has a transversal. The more general Brualdi-Stein conjecture (partial transversal of size n-1) was proved for large n. The original odd-order conjecture remains open for all n.

title: Sarnak's Mobius Disjointness Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every continuous function f on a compact metric space X and every homeomorphism T: X -> X with zero topological entropy, and for every point x in X, the sum (1/N) sum_{n=1}^{N} mu(n) f(T^n x) converges to 0 as N -> infinity, where mu is the Mobius function. Equivalently, every zero-entropy dynamical system is disjoint from the Mobius function.
statement.informal: The Mobius function, which encodes information about prime factorizations, should be "unrelated" to any dynamical system with low complexity (zero topological entropy). Their correlation should vanish as you average over more and more terms.

why_it_matters: Sarnak's conjecture, posed in 2010, bridges analytic number theory and ergodic theory. It generalizes the prime number theorem (which corresponds to the case where T is the identity) and implies the Chowla conjecture on correlations of the Mobius function. It has stimulated extensive work connecting dynamics, combinatorics, and multiplicative number theory.

canonical.title: Three lectures on the Mobius function, randomness, and dynamics
canonical.locator: Lecture notes, Institute for Advanced Study

status_evidence.title: Sarnak's conjecture: what's new
status_evidence.locator: Ergodic Theory and Dynamical Systems in their Interactions with Arithmetics and Combinatorics, Springer, pp. 163-235

partial_results.summary: Davenport proved the conjecture for linear sequences (periodic systems), building on the prime number theorem.
Green and Tao proved the conjecture for nilsystems.
Bourgain, Sarnak, and Ziegler proved it for a large class of zero-entropy systems including horocycle flows.
Matomaki, Radziwill, and Tao established the conjecture for averaged versions and short interval Mobius correlations.
Kanigowski, Lemanczyk, and Radziwill proved the conjecture for all smooth flows on surfaces.

machine_generated.summary: Sarnak's conjecture asserts that the Mobius function is disjoint from every zero-entropy dynamical system. It has been verified for many classes of systems but remains open in full generality.

title: Generalized Sato-Tate Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let A be an abelian variety of dimension g over a number field. The normalized Frobenius angles (or normalized L-function coefficients) at primes of good reduction are equidistributed with respect to the Haar measure on a specific compact group ST(A), the Sato-Tate group of A. For elliptic curves without CM, this was proved to be SU(2) (the classical Sato-Tate). For higher-dimensional abelian varieties, the identity of ST(A) and the equidistribution remain conjectural in general.
statement.informal: The classical Sato-Tate conjecture (now a theorem for elliptic curves over totally real fields) predicts the distribution of point counts on elliptic curves mod p. The generalization to higher-dimensional abelian varieties and more general motives remains open.

why_it_matters: The Sato-Tate conjecture connects arithmetic statistics, random matrix theory, and automorphic forms. Its proof for elliptic curves (Taylor, Clozel, Harris, Shepherd-Barron, 2011) was a triumph of the Langlands program. The generalization to higher genus varieties is a major open frontier, with deep connections to motives and L-functions.

canonical.title: Lectures on N_X(p)

status_evidence.title: Sato-Tate distributions and Galois endomorphism modules in genus 2

partial_results.summary: Classical Sato-Tate proved for elliptic curves over totally real fields by Taylor et al.
The Sato-Tate group classified for abelian surfaces by Fite, Kedlaya, Rotger, Sutherland.
Equidistribution proved for some abelian surfaces over totally real fields.

machine_generated.summary: The generalized Sato-Tate conjecture predicts equidistribution of Frobenius data for abelian varieties with respect to the Haar measure on the Sato-Tate group. Open in higher genus.

title: Schanuel's Conjecture

statement.canonical: If z_1, z_2, ..., z_n are complex numbers that are linearly independent over the rational numbers Q, then the field extension Q(z_1, ..., z_n, e^{z_1}, ..., e^{z_n}) has transcendence degree at least n over Q.
statement.informal: Given n complex numbers that are linearly independent over the rationals, the 2n numbers formed by these numbers together with their exponentials contain at least n that are algebraically independent. This would settle nearly all open problems in transcendental number theory.

why_it_matters: Schanuel's conjecture is arguably the most important open problem in transcendental number theory. It implies the Lindemann-Weierstrass theorem, the Gelfond-Schneider theorem, and would resolve the algebraic independence of e and pi, among many other fundamental questions.

canonical.title: Introduction to Transcendental Numbers

status_evidence.title: Transcendence of periods: the state of the art

partial_results.summary: The Lindemann-Weierstrass theorem (1882/1885) establishes the case where z_1, ..., z_n are algebraic numbers.
The Gelfond-Schneider theorem (1934) handles the case n=2 with one algebraic z_i.
The six exponentials theorem (Siegel, Lang, Ramachandra) proves a special case with a matrix of exponents.
Ax proved a function field analogue of Schanuel's conjecture.

machine_generated.summary: Schanuel's conjecture gives a sweeping lower bound on the transcendence degree of exponentials, implying virtually all known and conjectured results in transcendental number theory.
decomposition: Proving special cases involving small collections of algebraically related numbers
Extending Baker's theory of linear forms in logarithms toward algebraic independence

title: Schinzel's Hypothesis H

statement.canonical: Let f_1(x), ..., f_s(x) be irreducible polynomials with integer coefficients and positive leading coefficients. Suppose that no prime p divides the product f_1(n)*...*f_s(n) for all integers n (the "no fixed prime divisor" condition). Then there exist infinitely many positive integers n such that f_1(n), ..., f_s(n) are all simultaneously prime.
statement.informal: Any collection of integer polynomials that could conceivably all be prime at the same time (no obvious obstruction from a single prime dividing the product) will indeed all be simultaneously prime for infinitely many inputs.

why_it_matters: Schinzel's Hypothesis H (1958) is one of the most general conjectures in prime number theory. It subsumes the twin prime conjecture, Landau's conjecture (n^2+1 primes), Bunyakovsky's conjecture, and many others. It remains completely open for any system of two or more polynomials. Even single polynomial cases beyond linear are unproven (e.g., n^2+1).

canonical.title: Sur certaines hypotheses concernant les nombres premiers

status_evidence.title: Selecta

partial_results.summary: The case s=1 with linear polynomials is Dirichlet's theorem (proved 1837).
Green-Tao proved the case of k linear polynomials forming an arithmetic progression.

title: Scholz Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let l(n) denote the length of the shortest addition chain for the positive integer n (the minimum number of additions needed to reach n starting from 1, where each step adds two previously computed values). Then l(2^n - 1) <= l(n) + n - 1 for all positive integers n.
statement.informal: An addition chain for n is a sequence starting at 1 where each term is the sum of two earlier terms, ending at n. The conjecture says that computing 2^n - 1 (a Mersenne number) never requires more than n - 1 extra steps beyond what is needed to compute n itself.

why_it_matters: The Scholz conjecture connects the combinatorial theory of addition chains to the structure of Mersenne numbers. Addition chains are fundamental in efficient exponentiation algorithms used in cryptography and computer algebra.

canonical.title: Aufgabe 253
On addition chains

status_evidence.title: The Art of Computer Programming, Vol. 2: Seminumerical Algorithms (3rd ed.)

partial_results.summary: Brauer proved the conjecture for the case when the shortest addition chain for n is a Brauer chain (star chain).
Verified computationally for all n up to several thousand.
Thurber proved the conjecture for n <= 128 by exhaustive computation of all shortest addition chains.

title: Selberg Eigenvalue Conjecture

statement.canonical: For any congruence subgroup Gamma of SL(2, Z), every nonzero eigenvalue lambda of the Laplacian on the modular surface Gamma\H satisfies lambda >= 1/4, where H is the upper half-plane. Equivalently, for Maass cusp forms on congruence subgroups, the spectral parameter satisfies |Re(s) - 1/2| = 0 (i.e., s lies on the critical line Re(s) = 1/2).
statement.informal: The Laplacian on modular surfaces coming from congruence subgroups should have no "small eigenvalues" — all non-trivial eigenvalues should be at least 1/4. This is an analogue of the Ramanujan conjecture for non-holomorphic modular forms.

why_it_matters: The Selberg eigenvalue conjecture (1965) is a cornerstone of the theory of automorphic forms and spectral geometry. It is equivalent to the Ramanujan conjecture for GL(2) Maass forms. It has applications to the distribution of primes in arithmetic progressions, quantum chaos, and expander graph constructions. Selberg proved lambda >= 3/16; the best known bound is 975/4096.

canonical.title: On the estimation of Fourier coefficients of modular forms

status_evidence.title: Refined estimates towards the Ramanujan and Selberg conjectures

partial_results.summary: Selberg proved lambda >= 3/16 = 0.1875.
Kim and Sarnak improved the bound to lambda >= 975/4096 ~ 0.238.
The conjecture is proved for specific families of congruence subgroups via functoriality.

title: Selfridge's Conjecture on Primality

statement.canonical: There is no composite number n that is simultaneously a base-2 Fermat pseudoprime (2^{n-1} ≡ 1 mod n) and a Lucas pseudoprime with respect to some Lucas sequence with Jacobi symbol parameters chosen appropriately. Equivalently, there is no PSW pseudoprime: no composite passes both a strong base-2 probable prime test and a strong Lucas probable prime test with the standard parameter selection.
statement.informal: The Pomerance-Selfridge-Wagstaff (PSW) conjecture says that no composite number can fool both a Fermat primality test with base 2 and a Lucas primality test at the same time. If true, the combination would be a deterministic primality test.

why_it_matters: The PSW conjecture, if true, would provide an extremely efficient deterministic primality test. The Baillie-PSW test based on this conjecture is widely used in practice (e.g., in Mathematica and Maple) and has never been found to fail, despite extensive search.

canonical.title: The pseudoprimes to 25 * 10^9

status_evidence.title: Lucas pseudoprimes

partial_results.summary: No PSW pseudoprime has been found below 2^64, nor in any systematic search conducted to date.

The Baillie-PSW test has been verified against all composites below 2^64 with no counterexample (Feitsma and Galway).

title: Sendov's Conjecture
aliases: Sendov Conjecture on Zeros and Critical Points

statement.canonical: Let p(z) be a polynomial of degree n >= 2 with all zeros contained in the closed unit disk |z| <= 1. Then for each zero a of p, there exists a critical point w (zero of p') with |a - w| <= 1. That is, every zero of p has a critical point of p within distance 1 from it.
statement.informal: Consider a polynomial whose roots all lie within the unit circle in the complex plane. Sendov conjectured that near each root, there is always a critical point (a place where the derivative vanishes) within distance 1. Despite its elementary statement, this conjecture has been open since 1958.

why_it_matters: Sendov's conjecture, posed in 1958, is one of the most famous open problems in the geometry of polynomials and complex analysis. It connects the zero distribution of a polynomial with the zeros of its derivative, a relationship going back to Gauss-Lucas theorem. Despite extensive partial results (verified for degree up to 9, for zeros on the unit circle, etc.), the general case remains open. Tao proved it for sufficiently high degree polynomials in 2022, but small degrees remain unresolved.

canonical.title: Hausdorff geometry of polynomials (problem attributed)

Brown and Xiang proved the conjecture for zeros on the unit circle.
Degot proved the conjecture for polynomials of degree up to 9.
Tao proved Sendov's conjecture for all polynomials of sufficiently large degree.

title: Seymour's Second Neighborhood Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every finite simple directed graph (with no loops or parallel edges) has a vertex v whose second neighborhood (the set of vertices reachable from v by a directed path of length exactly 2, but not directly from v) is at least as large as its first neighborhood (out-neighborhood). That is, there exists v with |N^+(N^+(v)) \ N^+(v)| >= |N^+(v)|.
statement.informal: In any directed graph, there is always some vertex that has at least as many "friends of friends" (reachable in exactly two steps, excluding direct neighbors) as it has direct friends (outgoing neighbors).

why_it_matters: Seymour's second neighborhood conjecture, posed in the 1990s, is a fundamental open problem about the structure of directed graphs. It has connections to tournament theory, network structure, and influence propagation. Despite its simple statement, it has resisted proof for general digraphs.

Chen, Shen, and Yuster proved the conjecture for digraphs with minimum out-degree at most 6.

title: Shelah's Categoricity Conjecture
aliases: Categoricity Conjecture for Abstract Elementary Classes

statement.canonical: Let K be an abstract elementary class (AEC) with amalgamation. If K is categorical in some cardinal lambda >= Hanf(K) (meaning K has exactly one model of size lambda up to isomorphism), then K is categorical in all cardinals mu >= Hanf(K). This would generalize Morley's categoricity theorem from first-order logic to the much broader framework of abstract elementary classes.
statement.informal: In first-order logic, Morley proved that if a theory has exactly one model of some uncountable size, it has exactly one model of every uncountable size. Shelah conjectured this "transfer of uniqueness" should hold in a much more general logical setting called abstract elementary classes, which encompass many non-first-order logics.

why_it_matters: Shelah's categoricity conjecture is the central open problem in the classification theory of non-elementary classes. A positive resolution would dramatically extend the reach of model-theoretic classification theory beyond first-order logic, impacting infinitary logic, homogeneous model theory, and the study of algebraic structures not axiomatizable in first-order logic.

canonical.title: Classification Theory for Abstract Elementary Classes
canonical.locator: Draft monograph, later published in revised form by College Publications, 2009

status_evidence.title: Classification theory for abstract elementary classes
status_evidence.locator: Contemporary Mathematics, vol. 302, pp. 165-204

Boney proved that assuming large cardinals, every AEC is tame, yielding partial categoricity transfer.

title: Sierpinski Problem

statement.canonical: The Sierpinski problem asks to determine the smallest odd positive integer k such that k * 2^n + 1 is composite for all positive integers n. It is conjectured that the smallest such Sierpinski number is k = 78557. This requires showing that for every odd k < 78557 that is not already eliminated by covering set arguments, there exists some n for which k * 2^n + 1 is prime.
statement.informal: A Sierpinski number is an odd number k for which k * 2^n + 1 is never prime, no matter what n you choose. It is known that 78557 is a Sierpinski number (it has a covering set). The problem is to prove it is the smallest one, by finding a prime of the form k * 2^n + 1 for each remaining candidate k.

why_it_matters: The Sierpinski problem connects covering systems, sieve theory, and computational searches for large primes. Its resolution would complete our understanding of which multipliers k can produce primes in the family k * 2^n + 1.

canonical.title: Sur un problème concernant les nombres k * 2^n + 1

Sierpinski proved the existence of infinitely many Sierpinski numbers using covering sets.
The covering set for k=78557 was found by Selfridge: {3, 5, 7, 13, 17, 241}.

title: Simplex Conjecture in Information Theory

statement.canonical: Among all codes (finite subsets) of M points on the unit sphere S^{n-1} in R^n, the configuration that maximizes the minimum pairwise distance is the regular simplex when M = n+1. For general M > n+1, the conjecture extends to the arrangement maximizing the minimum distance, which remains open in most dimensions for M not equal to special values. In the information-theoretic formulation, the simplex code achieves the optimal error exponent for equal-energy codes on the Gaussian channel.
statement.informal: When packing M points on a sphere to maximize the minimum distance between any two points, the optimal arrangement for n+1 points in n dimensions forms a regular simplex. The problem is open for many other values of M and n.

why_it_matters: The simplex conjecture connects discrete geometry (sphere packing), information theory (channel coding), and mathematical optimization. The optimal arrangement of points on spheres has applications in signal constellation design, error-correcting codes, and quantum information theory.

canonical.title: The closest packing of spherical caps in n dimensions

status_evidence.title: Regular Polytopes

partial_results.summary: Proved for M = n+1 (simplex is optimal) in all dimensions.
Proved for M = 2n (cross-polytope is optimal) by Rankin.
Open for most intermediate values of M between n+1 and 2n.

title: Singmaster's Conjecture

statement.canonical: There exists a finite upper bound N such that every integer greater than 1 appears at most N times in Pascal's triangle. Equivalently, for each integer k > 1, the number of solutions to the equation C(n, m) = k (with 0 < m < n) is bounded by a universal constant. Singmaster conjectured N = 8, the multiplicity achieved by the number 3003.
statement.informal: In Pascal's triangle, each number appears some number of times. The number 1 appears infinitely often (it borders both sides), but every other number seems to appear only finitely many times. The conjecture says there is a universal upper bound on how many times any number greater than 1 can appear.

why_it_matters: Singmaster's conjecture sits at the intersection of combinatorics and number theory. It concerns the structure of Pascal's triangle, one of the most fundamental objects in combinatorics. The conjecture relates to the Diophantine problem of when binomial coefficients coincide, connecting to questions about factorials and divisibility.

canonical.title: How often does an integer occur as a binomial coefficient?
canonical.locator: American Mathematical Monthly, vol. 78, no. 4, pp. 385-386

status_evidence.title: Singmaster's conjecture revisited

partial_results.summary: Singmaster showed that N(x), the maximum multiplicity of integers up to x in Pascal's triangle, satisfies N(x) = O(log x / (log log x)^2).
Abbott, Erdos, and Hanson improved the bound to N(x) = O(log x / log log x).
Kane proved the upper bound N(x) = O((log x)^{2/3 + epsilon}).
The number 3003 = C(78,2) = C(15,5) = C(14,6) is known to appear 8 times; no number is known to appear more.

title: Slice-Ribbon Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every slice knot is a ribbon knot. A knot K in S^3 is slice if it bounds a smoothly embedded disk D^2 in the 4-ball B^4. It is ribbon if it bounds an immersed disk in S^3 whose only singularities are "ribbon singularities" (the disk passes through itself in arcs, with no "clasp" singularities). The conjecture asserts that these two conditions are equivalent.
statement.informal: A knot is "slice" if it can bound a smooth disk in 4-dimensional space. A knot is "ribbon" if it can bound a disk that crosses itself only in a simple way (ribbon crossings). The conjecture says these two notions are the same: any knot that bounds a disk in 4D can do so without complicated self-intersections.

why_it_matters: The slice-ribbon conjecture, posed by Ralph Fox in 1962, is one of the most important open problems in 4-dimensional topology and knot theory. It connects 3-dimensional knot invariants (ribbon structure) to 4-dimensional smooth topology (slice condition). Its resolution would clarify the relationship between topological and smooth categories in dimension 4, and it relates to handle decompositions of 4-manifolds.

canonical.title: Some problems in knot theory

Lisca verified the conjecture for 2-bridge knots.
Greene and Jabuka verified the conjecture for 3-strand pretzel knots.

title: Smale's 17th Problem
aliases: Solving Polynomial Equations in Polynomial Time

statement.canonical: Can a zero of a system of n polynomial equations in n complex unknowns be found approximately in time polynomial in the size of the input (the number of coefficients)? More precisely, is there a uniform algorithm that, given a system f = (f_1, ..., f_n) of polynomials, produces an approximate zero in expected time polynomial in N = sum of the number of monomials, with the expectation over a suitable probability distribution?
statement.informal: Is there an efficient algorithm to find approximate solutions to systems of polynomial equations? Smale asks whether this can be done in polynomial time on average.

why_it_matters: Smale's 17th problem (from his 1998 list for the 21st century) connects numerical analysis, algebraic geometry, and complexity theory. It asks about the fundamental computational complexity of solving polynomial systems, bridging continuous and discrete mathematics. Progress relates to homotopy methods, condition numbers, and alpha-theory.

canonical.title: Mathematical Problems for the Next Century

status_evidence.title: Fast linear homotopy to find approximate zeros of polynomial systems

partial_results.summary: Beltran and Pardo gave a randomized algorithm with complexity N^{O(log log N)} on average.
Burgisser and Cucker developed a smoothed analysis framework for the problem.
Lairez gave a deterministic algorithm with similar subexponential bounds.

title: Smale's 18th Problem
aliases: What Are the Limits of Intelligence?

statement.canonical: What are the limits of intelligence, both artificial and human? Smale's 18th problem asks for a rigorous mathematical framework to understand the fundamental limits of learning and intelligence. In a more precise formulation: what is the computational complexity of learning and optimization in high-dimensional spaces, and what are the inherent limitations of any learning algorithm given finite data?
statement.informal: Can we mathematically characterize the ultimate limits of what any intelligent system (biological or artificial) can learn, given bounded computational resources and finite data? This is the most open-ended of Smale's problems for the 21st century.

why_it_matters: Smale's 18th problem (1998) connects mathematical optimization, learning theory, and computational complexity. It motivates research on PAC learning bounds, statistical learning theory, neural network approximation theory, and the mathematical foundations of AI. Its resolution would have profound implications for understanding both artificial and natural intelligence.

title: Smooth 4-Dimensional Poincare Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every closed smooth 4-manifold that is homeomorphic to the 4-sphere S^4 is also diffeomorphic to S^4. Equivalently, there are no exotic smooth structures on S^4.
statement.informal: The standard 4-dimensional sphere has a natural "smooth structure" that lets us do calculus on it. The conjecture asks: is this the only way to make the 4-sphere smooth? Unlike other dimensions (where the answer is known), the 4-dimensional case remains wide open.

why_it_matters: The smooth 4-dimensional Poincare conjecture is the last remaining case of the generalized Poincare conjecture in the smooth category. Dimension 4 is uniquely difficult in topology: it is the only dimension where the smooth and topological categories diverge dramatically (as shown by the existence of exotic R^4s). Its resolution would be a landmark in 4-manifold topology.

canonical.title: The topology of four-dimensional manifolds
canonical.locator: Journal of Differential Geometry, vol. 17, pp. 357-453

title: Strong Cosmic Censorship Conjecture (Mathematical Formulation)
aliases: Christodoulou's Formulation of Strong Cosmic Censorship

statement.canonical: The maximal globally hyperbolic development of generic compact or asymptotically flat initial data for the Einstein vacuum equations R_{μν} = 0 is inextendible as a suitably regular Lorentzian manifold. In Christodoulou's C^0 formulation: the maximal Cauchy development is C^0-inextendible for generic initial data.
statement.informal: The laws of general relativity, given generic initial conditions, should determine the spacetime uniquely — there should be no way to extend the solution beyond a certain boundary (the Cauchy horizon) in a physically meaningful way. This is the mathematical version of the statement that "physics is deterministic in general relativity."

why_it_matters: The strong cosmic censorship conjecture is one of the most important open problems at the intersection of mathematical physics and PDE theory. It addresses the fundamental question of determinism in general relativity. Recent work by Dafermos and Luk (2017) on the interior of black holes has shown that the C^0 formulation is true for the Kerr solution, but the full conjecture remains wide open.

title: Tate Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let X be a smooth projective variety over a finitely generated field k, and let l be a prime different from the characteristic of k. The cycle class map from algebraic cycles modulo homological equivalence tensored with Q_l to the l-adic cohomology group H^{2i}(X_{\bar{k}}, Q_l(i))^{Gal(\bar{k}/k)} is surjective. Equivalently, every Galois-invariant cohomology class in the appropriate degree comes from an algebraic cycle.
statement.informal: For varieties over finite fields or number fields, every cohomology class that is fixed by the Galois symmetry group actually comes from a geometric object (an algebraic cycle).

why_it_matters: The Tate conjecture (1965) is one of the central conjectures in arithmetic geometry, parallel to the Hodge conjecture in complex geometry. It has profound implications for the theory of motives, for the Birch-Swinnerton-Dyer conjecture, and for the Langlands program. It remains open in general, though proved in many important special cases.

title: Telescope Conjecture

statement.canonical: In stable homotopy theory, the telescope conjecture (Ravenel, 1984) asserts that the v_n-periodic homotopy groups of a finite complex X, computed via the telescope (direct limit of v_n self-maps), agree with the L_n-local homotopy groups (computed via Bousfield localization at the Johnson-Wilson theory E(n)). Equivalently, the natural map from the telescope to the L_n-localization is an equivalence.
statement.informal: There are two ways to isolate periodic phenomena in stable homotopy theory: one concrete (telescopes) and one abstract (Bousfield localization). The telescope conjecture predicted they agree. This was recently disproved at height 2 and above.

why_it_matters: The telescope conjecture was one of Ravenel's conjectures that organized chromatic homotopy theory for 40 years. While five of Ravenel's seven conjectures were proved by Devinatz, Hopkins, and Smith, the telescope conjecture was recently disproved by Burklund, Hahn, Levy, and Schlank (2023) at heights >= 2. Understanding the precise failure and its consequences for chromatic homotopy theory is a major open direction.

title: Tight Closure Localization Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let R be a Noetherian ring of prime characteristic p > 0, I an ideal of R, and S a multiplicative set. If an element z belongs to the tight closure I^* of I in R, then the image of z in S^{-1}R belongs to the tight closure (S^{-1}I)^* of S^{-1}I in the localization S^{-1}R. That is, tight closure commutes with localization: (I^*)S^{-1}R = (IS^{-1}R)^*.
statement.informal: Tight closure is an important operation on ideals in commutative algebra defined using the Frobenius endomorphism in characteristic p. The conjecture says that this operation behaves well under localization — restricting to a smaller piece of the ring preserves the tight closure.

why_it_matters: Tight closure, introduced by Hochster and Huneke in 1990, is one of the most powerful tools in commutative algebra and algebraic geometry over fields of positive characteristic. The localization problem is the most fundamental open question about tight closure and affects many applications including the understanding of singularities and the homological conjectures.

canonical.title: Tight closure, invariant theory, and the Briançon-Skoda theorem

title: Total Coloring Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every simple graph G with maximum degree Delta(G), the total chromatic number chi''(G) satisfies chi''(G) <= Delta(G) + 2. The total chromatic number is the minimum number of colors needed to color both the vertices and edges of G so that no two adjacent or incident elements share the same color.
statement.informal: If you want to color both the vertices and edges of a graph so that no two neighboring elements share a color, you never need more than Delta + 2 colors, where Delta is the maximum degree.

why_it_matters: The Total Coloring Conjecture, independently posed by Behzad (1965) and Vizing (1968), is one of the most famous open problems in graph coloring. The trivial lower bound is Delta + 1, and the conjecture asserts that at most one extra color suffices. It has been verified for many graph classes but remains open in general.

The conjecture holds for complete graphs, bipartite graphs, and planar graphs with Delta >= 7.

The conjecture is verified for graphs with large enough maximum degree via the probabilistic method.

title: Turan's Brick Factory Problem
aliases: Crossing Number of Complete Bipartite Graphs

statement.canonical: Determine the crossing number cr(K_{m,n}) of the complete bipartite graph K_{m,n}. The conjecture states that cr(K_{m,n}) = floor(m/2) * floor((m-1)/2) * floor(n/2) * floor((n-1)/2), which is the number of crossings in the natural drawing of K_{m,n}.
statement.informal: What is the minimum number of edge crossings when drawing the complete bipartite graph K_{m,n} in the plane? Turan conjectured that the obvious symmetric drawing is optimal.

why_it_matters: Turan's brick factory problem, originating from his work in a forced labor camp during World War II, is one of the oldest and most fundamental open problems in topological graph theory. It led to the development of crossing number theory, which has applications in VLSI design, network visualization, and computational geometry.

Christian, Richter, and Salazar verified the conjecture for K_{7,n} and K_{8,n} for small n values.

title: Twin Prime Conjecture

statement.canonical: There are infinitely many primes p such that p + 2 is also prime. Equivalently, lim inf_{n -> infinity} (p_{n+1} - p_n) = 2, where p_n denotes the n-th prime number.
statement.informal: Twin primes are pairs of prime numbers that differ by exactly 2, such as (3, 5), (11, 13), and (17, 19). The twin prime conjecture asserts that there are infinitely many such pairs.

why_it_matters: The twin prime conjecture is one of the oldest open problems in number theory, intimately connected to the distribution of prime numbers. Its resolution would deepen our understanding of prime gaps and has connections to sieve methods, the Hardy-Littlewood conjectures, and the structure of the primes.

title: Uhl's Conjecture on Vector Measures

statement.canonical: A Banach space X has the Radon-Nikodym property (RNP) if and only if every X-valued measure of bounded variation that is absolutely continuous with respect to a scalar measure has a Radon-Nikodym derivative (density). Uhl's conjecture characterizes which Banach spaces have the RNP in terms of geometric properties: X has RNP if and only if every closed bounded convex subset of X is the closed convex hull of its strongly exposed points.
statement.informal: Which Banach spaces have the property that vector-valued measures always have densities? The conjecture connects this analytic property to the geometry of convex sets in the space.

why_it_matters: The Radon-Nikodym property is one of the most important properties in the interaction between measure theory and Banach space geometry. Understanding which spaces have RNP connects to probability (martingale convergence), optimization (dentability of convex sets), and harmonic analysis (vector-valued singular integrals).
