title: Ungar's Conjecture on Direction Sets

statement.canonical: Let S be a set of n points in R^d (d >= 2), not all on a single hyperplane. The set of directions determined by pairs of points in S has cardinality at least 2*floor(n/2). In the plane (d=2), Ungar proved this bound of 2*floor(n/2) directions in 1982. The conjecture extends to higher dimensions: for d >= 3, what is the minimum number of directions determined by n points in general position in R^d?
statement.informal: Given n points in d-dimensional space not all on a hyperplane, how few distinct directions can the connecting lines determine? Ungar proved the 2D case; the higher-dimensional analogue is open.

why_it_matters: This problem connects discrete geometry, combinatorics, and algebraic methods. Ungar's elegant proof in 2D used allowable sequences. Higher-dimensional extensions relate to the Sylvester-Gallai theorem, the orchard problem, and extremal combinatorial geometry.

title: Union-Closed Sets Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let F be a finite family of finite sets, not all empty, that is union-closed (i.e., the union of any two sets in F is also in F). Then there exists an element that belongs to at least half of the sets in F. Formally, there exists x in the union of F such that |{A in F : x in A}| >= |F|/2.
statement.informal: Consider a collection of sets that is closed under taking unions (combining any two sets in the collection produces another set that is also in the collection). The conjecture says there must be some element that appears in at least half the sets.

why_it_matters: Frankl's conjecture, posed in 1979, is one of the most tantalizing open problems in extremal combinatorics. It is extremely easy to state but has resisted all proof attempts. Gilmer's 2022 breakthrough proved the first constant-fraction bound, and subsequent improvements have brought renewed attention to the problem.

The conjecture is known to hold for families with at most 50 sets.
Knill proved the conjecture holds when the union-closed family is a lattice.

title: Unitary Dual Problem for Reductive Groups

statement.canonical: Classify all irreducible unitary representations of a real reductive Lie group G. That is, determine the unitary dual G-hat = {equivalence classes of irreducible unitary representations of G on Hilbert spaces} for all real reductive groups G. The problem remains open for general real reductive groups beyond the classical groups of small rank.
statement.informal: A "representation" describes how a symmetry group can act on a vector space by rotations and reflections. The unitary dual problem asks for a complete list of all the basic (irreducible) ways a given Lie group can act while preserving lengths and angles (unitarily). This is the fundamental classification problem in representation theory.

why_it_matters: The classification of unitary representations is one of the central problems in mathematics, with applications to number theory (automorphic forms), physics (quantum mechanics), and harmonic analysis. Despite Vogan's program and major advances including the Atlas of Lie Groups project (computing unitary duals algorithmically for specific groups), the general problem remains open. It connects to the Langlands program and the theory of automorphic forms.

title: Vaught's Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let T be a complete countable first-order theory in a countable language. Then the number of countable models of T up to isomorphism (denoted I(T, aleph_0)) is either countable (i.e., finite or equal to aleph_0) or exactly 2^{aleph_0} (the cardinality of the continuum). In other words, no complete countable first-order theory has exactly aleph_1 many countable models (assuming the continuum hypothesis fails).
statement.informal: When studying a mathematical theory (a consistent set of axioms in first-order logic), one can ask: how many essentially different countable structures satisfy these axioms? Vaught conjectured that the answer is either small (countably many) or as large as possible (continuum many) -- there is no intermediate case.

why_it_matters: Vaught's conjecture, posed in 1961, is the most important open problem in model theory and one of the central questions in mathematical logic. It sits at the intersection of model theory, descriptive set theory, and set theory. Its resolution would deepen our understanding of the classification of mathematical structures and the nature of definability.

canonical.title: Denumerable models of complete theories

Steel proved the conjecture for theories in the language of linear orders.
Shelah and Harrington-Makkai proved the conjecture for omega-stable theories.
Buechler proved the conjecture for superstable theories of finite rank.
Vaught's conjecture is known to hold for theories with fewer than aleph_1 many types.

title: Vizing's Conjecture

statement.canonical: For any two graphs G and H, the domination number of the Cartesian product satisfies gamma(G square H) >= gamma(G) * gamma(H), where gamma denotes the domination number and G square H is the Cartesian product of graphs G and H.
statement.informal: The minimum number of vertices needed to dominate the Cartesian product of two graphs is at least the product of the domination numbers of the individual graphs. In other words, domination does not become easier in the product.

why_it_matters: Vizing's conjecture, posed in 1968, is one of the most famous open problems in domination theory and graph products. It has been called the most important unsolved problem in the theory of graph domination. Understanding domination in product graphs has applications in network design, coding theory, and distributed computing.

Suen and Tarr improved the bound for specific graph classes including chordal graphs.
Bresar proved the conjecture when one factor is a tree.

title: Volume Conjecture

statement.canonical: For any hyperbolic knot K in S^3, let J_N(K; q) denote the colored Jones polynomial colored by the N-dimensional irreducible representation of sl(2,C), evaluated at q = exp(2πi/N). Then lim_{N→∞} (2π/N) log |J_N(K; e^{2πi/N})| = vol(S^3 \ K), where vol denotes the simplicial (hyperbolic) volume of the knot complement.
statement.informal: The colored Jones polynomial is an algebraic knot invariant. The volume conjecture says that if you evaluate it at special roots of unity and take a limit, you recover the hyperbolic volume of the knot complement — linking quantum algebra to hyperbolic geometry.

why_it_matters: The volume conjecture is a remarkable bridge between quantum topology (specifically, quantum invariants of knots) and hyperbolic geometry. It connects the algebraic world of Jones polynomials and quantum groups to the geometric world of Thurston's hyperbolic structures, and is a key conjecture in the broader program of understanding 3-manifold topology through quantum invariants.

canonical.title: The hyperbolic volume of knots from the quantum dilogarithm

title: Waring's Problem (Exact Values of g(k))

statement.canonical: Let g(k) denote the smallest s such that every positive integer can be written as a sum of at most s k-th powers of non-negative integers. It is conjectured that for all k >= 2, g(k) = 2^k + floor((3/2)^k) - 2, provided that the fractional part {(3/2)^k} satisfies {(3/2)^k} <= 1 - (3/4)^k + floor((3/4)^k) * 2^{-k}. Prove this formula for all k, or determine g(k) exactly for all k >= 2.
statement.informal: Waring's problem asks: for each power k, what is the maximum number of k-th powers needed to represent any positive integer as their sum? A conjectured formula gives the answer for all k, but proving it requires showing a certain inequality about powers of 3/2 holds for all k.

why_it_matters: Waring's problem is one of the most classical problems in additive number theory. While Hilbert proved g(k) is finite for all k, the exact determination for all k remains open and connects to deep questions about the distribution of powers of 3/2 modulo 1.

canonical.title: Meditationes Algebraicae

The conjectured formula is verified for all k <= 471,600,000 (Kubina and Wunderlich, 1990, extended by others).
Mahler (1957) proved the key inequality {(3/2)^k} <= 1 - 2^{-k} holds for all sufficiently large k, but the result is ineffective.
The formula g(k) = 2^k + floor((3/2)^k) - 2 has been proven for specific ranges, contingent on bounds for {(3/2)^k}.

title: Wegner's Conjecture on Disk Graphs

statement.canonical: For every unit disk graph G with clique number ω(G), the chromatic number χ(G) satisfies χ(G) ≤ (3/2)ω(G) - 1/2 for ω(G) ≥ 3. More generally, for disk graphs (not necessarily unit), the chromatic number should be bounded by a function that is at most 3/2 times the clique number (up to lower-order terms).
statement.informal: If you represent a graph by disks in the plane where vertices are connected when their disks overlap, how many colors do you need? Wegner conjectured that the answer is at most about 3/2 times the size of the largest clique.

why_it_matters: Wegner's conjecture is a fundamental question in geometric graph theory connecting chromatic number, clique number, and geometric representation. It has been open since 1977 and is important for frequency assignment problems in wireless networks, where disk graphs naturally model interference patterns.

title: Weinstein Conjecture

statement.canonical: Every closed contact manifold (M, xi) admits at least one closed orbit of the Reeb vector field associated to any contact form alpha defining xi. Equivalently, for any contact form on a closed manifold, the Reeb flow has at least one periodic orbit.
statement.informal: On any compact odd-dimensional manifold equipped with a contact structure (a geometric structure arising naturally in mechanics and optics), there is always a trajectory of the associated dynamical system that returns to its starting point -- a periodic orbit must exist.

why_it_matters: The Weinstein conjecture, posed in 1979, is a foundational problem in symplectic and contact topology. It connects the global topology of contact manifolds with the dynamics of Reeb flows. Its resolution in dimension 3 by Taubes (2007) using Seiberg-Witten theory was a landmark achievement, but the general case in higher dimensions remains open.

title: Weiss Conjecture on Admissibility

statement.canonical: Let A be the generator of a bounded analytic semigroup on a Hilbert space H, and let C: D(A) -> Y be an observation operator. Then C is an infinite-time admissible observation operator for the semigroup if and only if the Carleson measure condition holds: sup_{Re(s)>0} ||C(sI - A)^{-1}|| < infinity. In other words, resolvent boundedness characterizes admissibility.
statement.informal: For certain infinite-dimensional linear systems (e.g., heat equations, wave equations), the question is whether observability (can you recover the state from measurements?) is equivalent to a simple resolvent estimate.

why_it_matters: The Weiss conjecture (1989) is a central open problem in infinite-dimensional systems theory. It connects operator semigroup theory, control theory for PDEs, and Carleson measure theory. Jacob and Partington proved it for specific semigroups, but the general case remains open. It has implications for PDE control and stabilization.

title: Welsh's Conjecture
aliases: Dominic Welsh's Conjecture on Matroid Growth

statement.canonical: Let m_n denote the number of matroids on an n-element ground set. Welsh conjectured that the sequence log(m_n)/n^2 converges, and more generally asked for the asymptotic growth rate of the number of matroids. It is known that log(m_n)/n^2 is bounded between positive constants. The conjecture is that the limit exists and equals a specific constant.
statement.informal: How many matroids are there on n elements? The number grows super-exponentially, roughly as 2^{cn^2}. Welsh conjectured that the growth rate (in a logarithmic sense) converges to a precise constant.

why_it_matters: Understanding the number of matroids is a fundamental question in matroid theory and enumerative combinatorics. The explosive growth means most matroids are "wild" and not representable over any field. Welsh's conjecture guides research on the structure of typical matroids and has connections to entropy methods in combinatorics.

title: Woodin's Ultimate L Conjecture

statement.canonical: There exists a canonical inner model (called "Ultimate L") that is compatible with all large cardinal axioms and satisfies V = Ultimate L. More precisely, assuming the existence of an extendible cardinal, there is a definable inner model L_Omega that is close to V (satisfies a strong form of the Weak Covering Lemma), is compatible with all large cardinals, and in which the Continuum Hypothesis and other combinatorial principles hold. The conjecture asserts that such a model exists and that V = L_Omega is consistent with all known large cardinal axioms.
statement.informal: The Ultimate L Conjecture proposes that there is a "best" canonical model of set theory that looks like Godel's constructible universe L but is large enough to accommodate all known large cardinal axioms. This would resolve the Continuum Hypothesis (it would be true in this model) and provide a definitive answer to "what sets exist."

why_it_matters: Woodin's Ultimate L program represents one of the most ambitious attempts to resolve the fundamental independence phenomena in set theory, including the Continuum Hypothesis. If successful, it would provide a canonical model of set theory compatible with all large cardinals, resolving questions left open since Cohen's independence proof of 1963. It represents a competing approach to the forcing axiom/Martin's Maximum program.

title: Yau's Conjecture on the First Eigenvalue
aliases: First Eigenvalue of Embedded Minimal Surfaces

statement.canonical: Let Sigma be a closed embedded minimal hypersurface in the unit sphere S^{n+1}. Then the first eigenvalue of the Laplacian on Sigma equals n, i.e., lambda_1(Sigma) = n. This is known to hold for totally geodesic equators (where lambda_1 = n) and the conjecture asserts it holds for all such embedded minimal hypersurfaces.
statement.informal: Minimal surfaces in spheres are surfaces that locally minimize area. Yau conjectured that for any such surface embedded (without self-intersections) in the sphere, the lowest nontrivial frequency of vibration (first eigenvalue of the Laplacian) takes a specific universal value that depends only on the dimension.

why_it_matters: Yau's conjecture, posed in 1982, is a major open problem in spectral geometry connecting minimal surface theory with eigenvalue problems. It relates the extrinsic geometry of minimal submanifolds to their intrinsic spectral properties. Progress on this conjecture has stimulated the development of new tools in geometric analysis.

The conjecture has been verified computationally for the Clifford torus and Lawson surfaces.

title: Zarankiewicz Problem

statement.canonical: Determine the exact value of the Zarankiewicz number z(m,n;s,t), defined as the maximum number of 1s in an m x n binary matrix that contains no s x t all-ones submatrix. Equivalently, determine the Turan number ex(n; K_{s,t}), the maximum number of edges in a bipartite graph on n+n vertices containing no K_{s,t} subgraph. In particular, the exact asymptotics of ex(n; K_{s,t}) for general s,t remain unknown.
statement.informal: How many edges can a bipartite graph have while avoiding a complete bipartite subgraph K_{s,t}? The Kovari-Sos-Turan theorem gives an upper bound, but matching constructions exist only for special cases.

why_it_matters: The Zarankiewicz problem is a central question in extremal graph theory, connecting to incidence geometry, algebraic combinatorics, and additive number theory. The gap between upper and lower bounds for general parameters has persisted for decades and resolving it would have major implications for Turan-type extremal problems.

canonical.title: Problem P 101
On a problem of K. Zarankiewicz

status_evidence.title: Graphs with few paths of prescribed length between any two vertices

partial_results.summary: The Kovari-Sos-Turan theorem gives ex(n; K_{s,t}) = O(n^{2-1/s}) for s <= t.
Algebraic constructions match the upper bound for K_{2,2}, K_{3,3}, and K_{2,t} for all t, using finite geometry.
Kollar, Ronyai, and Szabo gave matching lower bounds for ex(n; K_{s,t}) when t > (s-1)! using algebraic constructions.
Bukh and Conlon improved upper bounds for K_{s,t} when t is moderately large relative to s using random algebraic methods.

machine_generated.summary: The Zarankiewicz problem asks for the maximum number of edges in a bipartite graph avoiding K_{s,t} as a subgraph. The exact answer is known only for special cases, and the general problem remains one of the central challenges in extremal combinatorics.

New algebraic or probabilistic constructions for dense K_{s,t}-free graphs
Connections to polynomial method and incidence geometry

title: Zimmer's Conjecture

statement.canonical: Let Gamma be a lattice in a simple Lie group G of real rank r >= 2. If Gamma acts faithfully on a compact manifold M by C^2 diffeomorphisms, then dim(M) >= dim(G/P) where P is a maximal parabolic subgroup of G. In particular, for SL(n, Z) acting on a compact manifold M, one needs dim(M) >= n - 1.
statement.informal: Higher-rank lattices (such as SL(n,Z) for n >= 3) are very rigid algebraic objects. Zimmer's conjecture predicts that they cannot act nontrivially on manifolds that are "too small" in dimension -- there is a minimum dimension for the manifold that depends on the rank of the lattice.

why_it_matters: Zimmer's conjecture, formulated in the 1980s, is a centerpiece of Zimmer's program aiming to extend Margulis superrigidity from the linear setting to smooth actions on manifolds. It connects Lie theory, ergodic theory, and smooth dynamics. The breakthrough by Brown, Fisher, and Hurtado (2017-2022) resolved the conjecture for cocompact lattices, but extensions to non-cocompact lattices and other aspects remain open.

canonical.title: Ergodic Theory and Semisimple Groups

status_evidence.title: Zimmer's conjecture for actions of SL(m,Z)
status_evidence.locator: Inventiones Mathematicae, vol. 229, pp. 1001-1060

partial_results.summary: Zimmer proved cocycle superrigidity for lattice actions, providing the foundational framework.
Polterovich showed SL(n,Z) cannot act faithfully by area-preserving diffeomorphisms on surfaces for n >= 3.
Brown, Fisher, and Hurtado proved Zimmer's conjecture for cocompact lattices in SL(n,R) for n >= 3.
Brown, Fisher, and Hurtado extended the result to all lattices in SL(n,R), confirming the conjecture for SL(n,Z).

machine_generated.summary: Zimmer's conjecture predicts a sharp lower bound on the dimension of compact manifolds admitting faithful smooth actions of higher-rank lattices. It has been proved for cocompact lattices by Brown-Fisher-Hurtado, but extensions to broader classes of lattices and other Lie groups remain open.
decomposition: Non-cocompact lattice actions and the role of unipotent elements
Analytic and regularity assumptions: C^1 vs C^2 actions
Extension to exceptional Lie groups and non-split forms

title: 3SUM Complexity: Is There an O(n log n) Algorithm?

statement.canonical: The 3SUM problem asks: given a set S of n integers, do there exist three elements a, b, c in S such that a + b + c = 0? The classical algorithm solves this in O(n^2) time. The 3SUM conjecture asserts that no algorithm can solve 3SUM in O(n^{2-epsilon}) time for any constant epsilon > 0 (in the real RAM model or comparison-based model). Is there an algorithm for 3SUM running in time O(n log n), or more generally, in truly subquadratic time o(n^2)?
statement.informal: Given a list of n numbers, can we find three of them that add up to zero faster than checking almost all triples? The obvious approach takes time proportional to n-squared. Can we do significantly better, perhaps in time proportional to n times log(n)? Most researchers believe we cannot do much better than n-squared.

why_it_matters: The 3SUM problem serves as a cornerstone of fine-grained complexity theory. Many problems in computational geometry, pattern matching, and graph algorithms have been shown to be "3SUM-hard," meaning a truly subquadratic algorithm for them would imply a truly subquadratic algorithm for 3SUM. The 3SUM conjecture is one of the key hypotheses (alongside SETH and APSP conjecture) used to establish conditional lower bounds throughout algorithm design. Understanding the true complexity of 3SUM would clarify the landscape of polynomial-time problems.

canonical.title: On a Class of O(n^2) Problems in Computational Geometry
On Some Fine-Grained Questions in Algorithms and Complexity
canonical.locator: Proceedings of the ICM 2018, Vol. 3, pp. 3447-3487

status_evidence.title: Higher Lower Bounds from the 3SUM Conjecture

partial_results.summary: Gronlund and Pettie gave a decision-tree algorithm for 3SUM using O(n^{3/2} sqrt(log n)) comparisons, breaking the n^2 barrier in the comparison model.
Freund gave a randomized algorithm solving 3SUM in expected O(n^2 / (log n / log log n)^{2/3}) time in the real RAM model.
Chan showed 3SUM can be solved in O(n^2 (log log n)^{O(1)} / log^2 n) time by combining hashing with word-RAM techniques.
Kane, Lovett, and Moran proved that the 3SUM problem requires Omega(n log n) comparisons in the linear decision tree model.

machine_generated.summary: The 3SUM complexity problem asks whether three numbers summing to zero can be found in truly subquadratic time, serving as a foundational hypothesis in fine-grained complexity theory.
decomposition: Develop new algorithmic techniques beyond hashing and word-level parallelism to break the quadratic barrier
Prove unconditional lower bounds in realistic computational models such as the real RAM model

title: Aanderaa-Karp-Rosenberg Conjecture (Evasiveness Conjecture)

statement.canonical: A graph property is a class of graphs closed under isomorphism. A graph property P on n vertices is called evasive if every deterministic algorithm that determines whether an input graph on n vertices has property P by querying individual edges must, in the worst case, query all n(n-1)/2 possible edges. The Aanderaa-Karp-Rosenberg conjecture (evasiveness conjecture) states that every nontrivial monotone graph property is evasive. A monotone graph property is one that is preserved under the addition of edges, and nontrivial means it is not satisfied by all graphs or by no graphs.
statement.informal: Suppose you want to determine whether a graph has some property (such as being connected, containing a triangle, or being planar) that is preserved when you add edges. You learn about the graph by asking about one edge at a time: "Is there an edge between vertex i and vertex j?" The conjecture says that for any such property, an adversary can always force you to ask about every possible edge before you can determine the answer.

why_it_matters: The evasiveness conjecture connects combinatorics, topology, and computational complexity. It concerns the query complexity (decision tree complexity) of graph properties, which is a fundamental model of computation. Proofs for special cases have used surprising topological methods (Oliver's theorem on fixed points of group actions, simplicial complexes). The conjecture, if true, would give a complete characterization of the deterministic query complexity of monotone graph properties.

canonical.title: On the Time Required to Recognize Properties of Graphs: A Problem
On the Complexity of Combinatorial Problems

status_evidence.title: A Topological Approach to Evasiveness

partial_results.summary: Kahn, Saks, and Sturtevant proved the conjecture for graphs on a prime power number of vertices, using Oliver's theorem from algebraic topology on fixed points of p-group actions on acyclic simplicial complexes.
Rivest and Vuillemin proved that every nontrivial monotone graph property has decision tree complexity at least n^2/4, establishing a quadratic lower bound.
The conjecture has been verified for specific properties: connectivity, planarity, containing a Hamiltonian cycle, k-colorability.
Chakrabarti, Khot, and Shi proved near-optimal lower bounds on the randomized query complexity of monotone graph properties.

machine_generated.summary: The Aanderaa-Karp-Rosenberg conjecture states that every nontrivial monotone graph property requires querying all edges in the worst case, connecting query complexity to algebraic topology.
decomposition: Extend the topological approach of Kahn-Saks-Sturtevant beyond prime power vertex counts
Develop new combinatorial or algebraic tools to handle general vertex counts

title: ACC0 Circuit Lower Bounds

statement.canonical: Let ACC^0 denote the class of languages computable by constant-depth polynomial-size circuits with AND, OR, NOT, and MOD_m gates for any fixed m. Can we prove that NP is not contained in ACC^0? More generally, can we prove explicit superpolynomial lower bounds against ACC^0 circuits for problems in NP?
statement.informal: Can we prove that there are problems in NP that cannot be solved by small constant-depth circuits even when those circuits are allowed to use modular counting gates?

why_it_matters: Proving that NP is not in ACC^0 would be a major step in circuit complexity. While Williams (2011) showed NEXP is not in ACC^0, extending this to NP or even NP-complete problems would be a dramatic improvement and would advance our understanding of the P vs NP question.

canonical.title: Nonuniform ACC Circuit Lower Bounds

status_evidence.title: Certifying Polynomials for AC^0[p] circuits

partial_results.summary: Razborov (1987) and Smolensky (1987) proved exponential lower bounds for AC^0[p] for prime p, but these techniques fail for composite moduli.
Williams (2011) proved NEXP is not in ACC^0, the first superpolynomial lower bound against ACC^0 for any explicit function.
The gap between the NEXP lower bound and the desired NP lower bound against ACC^0 remains wide open.

machine_generated.summary: The ACC^0 lower bounds problem asks for explicit superpolynomial lower bounds against constant-depth circuits with modular counting gates for NP problems.
decomposition: Extend Williams' NEXP lower bound techniques to work for NP
Overcome barriers for proving lower bounds against composite modulus gates

title: Active Learning Query Complexity

statement.canonical: In active learning, the learner can adaptively choose which examples to request labels for. What is the optimal label complexity (number of label queries) as a function of the VC dimension, error parameter, and distribution? For which concept classes does active learning provably improve over passive learning, and by how much?
statement.informal: If a learning algorithm can choose which data points to get labels for (rather than receiving random labeled data), how many labels does it need? When does this adaptive querying help significantly?

why_it_matters: Active learning is crucial in practice because labeled data is expensive. Understanding the fundamental limits of label efficiency guides the design of active learning systems in applications like medical diagnosis and NLP.

canonical.title: A Bound on the Label Complexity of Disagreement-Based Active Learning

status_evidence.title: Two Faces of Active Learning

partial_results.summary: Hanneke (2007) showed that the disagreement coefficient characterizes the label complexity of active learning.
For linear classifiers under log-concave distributions, exponential savings over passive learning are possible.
General characterization of when active learning helps and by exactly how much remains open.

machine_generated.summary: Determining the exact label complexity of active learning and characterizing when it provably improves over passive learning are fundamental open problems.
decomposition: Provide tight characterization of label complexity for natural concept classes
Determine the exact benefit of active learning for specific distributions

title: Agnostic Learning Fundamental Limits

statement.canonical: In agnostic learning, the target function may not belong to the hypothesis class. The learner seeks the best approximation within the class. What are the fundamental computational limits of agnostic learning? For which concept classes (halfspaces, decision trees, DNF) is agnostic learning computationally hard, and what are the best achievable approximation guarantees in polynomial time?
statement.informal: When learning without assuming the data fits a model perfectly, how well can we learn? For basic concept classes like linear classifiers, even approximately finding the best-fitting classifier is open.

why_it_matters: Agnostic learning is the realistic learning setting since real data rarely fits any model perfectly. Understanding its computational limits is essential for understanding what machine learning can and cannot achieve efficiently.

canonical.title: Toward Efficient Agnostic Learning

status_evidence.title: Complexity Theoretic Limitations on Learning Halfspaces

partial_results.summary: Kearns, Schapire, and Sellie (1994) introduced the agnostic learning framework and gave algorithms for simple classes.
Daniely (2016) showed that agnostic learning of halfspaces is hard under the assumption that random K-SAT is hard.
For decision trees and DNF, the complexity of agnostic learning is still poorly understood.

machine_generated.summary: Understanding the computational limits of agnostic learning, where the target may not lie in the hypothesis class, is fundamental to realistic machine learning theory.
decomposition: Determine the computational complexity of agnostically learning halfspaces
Establish tight bounds for agnostic learning of natural concept classes

title: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Lower Bounds

statement.canonical: For the c-approximate nearest neighbor problem in d-dimensional Euclidean space, what is the optimal tradeoff between space, query time, and approximation factor c? Specifically, is the n^(1/c^2) query time achieved by locality-sensitive hashing optimal for polynomial space data structures? Can we prove matching lower bounds?
statement.informal: What is the best possible tradeoff for approximate nearest neighbor search? We have good algorithms (like LSH) but do not know if they are optimal.

why_it_matters: Approximate nearest neighbor is used throughout machine learning, recommendation systems, and search engines. Establishing tight lower bounds would show whether existing algorithms like LSH are essentially optimal or can be improved.

status_evidence.title: Optimal Data-Dependent Hashing for Approximate Near Neighbors

partial_results.summary: Andoni and Razenshteyn (2015) achieved optimal data-dependent LSH with query time n^(1/c^2 + o(1)) for l2 distance.
Motwani, Naor, and Panigrahy (2007) proved lower bounds for LSH-based approaches.
Unconditional cell probe lower bounds for ANN remain logarithmic, far from the polynomial upper bounds.

title: All-Pairs Shortest Paths in Truly Subcubic Time

statement.canonical: Can the All-Pairs Shortest Paths (APSP) problem on a graph with n vertices and arbitrary edge weights be solved in O(n^(3-epsilon)) time for some constant epsilon > 0? The best known algorithm runs in O(n^3 / 2^(Omega(sqrt(log n)))) time, barely improving on the cubic bound.
statement.informal: Can we find shortest paths between all pairs of vertices in a weighted graph significantly faster than the naive cubic-time algorithm?

why_it_matters: APSP is one of the most fundamental graph problems and serves as a central problem in fine-grained complexity theory. Many other problems have been shown to be equivalent in complexity to APSP, so a truly subcubic algorithm would have far-reaching consequences.

canonical.title: Faster All-Pairs Shortest Paths via Circuit Complexity

status_evidence.title: Subcubic Equivalences Between Path, Matrix, and Triangle Problems

partial_results.summary: Floyd-Warshall and Johnson's algorithms achieve O(n^3) time for dense graphs.
Williams (2014) achieved the best known improvement: O(n^3 / 2^(Omega(sqrt(log n)))) using circuit complexity techniques.
Vassilevska Williams and Williams (2018) showed subcubic equivalence between APSP, negative triangle detection, and other problems.

title: Art Gallery Problem Computational Complexity

statement.canonical: The art gallery problem asks for the minimum number of guards needed to see every point of a simple polygon. While Chvátal's theorem gives an upper bound of floor(n/3) guards for an n-vertex polygon, the computational problem of finding the minimum number of guards is known to be NP-hard. What is the exact complexity? Is the problem in NP? Is it in the existential theory of the reals (ETR-complete)?
statement.informal: How hard is it to compute the minimum number of guards needed to watch every point in an art gallery (polygon)? We know it is NP-hard, but is it even in NP?

why_it_matters: The art gallery problem is a classic problem in computational geometry with applications in surveillance, sensor placement, and robotics. Its exact computational complexity is surprisingly subtle and connects to the existential theory of the reals.

canonical.title: A Combinatorial Theorem in Plane Geometry

status_evidence.title: The Art Gallery Problem is ∃R-complete

partial_results.summary: Lee and Lin (1986) proved the art gallery problem is NP-hard.
Abrahamsen, Adamaszek, and Miltzow (2018) proved the problem is ETR-complete, placing it between NP and PSPACE.
For special cases (orthogonal polygons, simple polygons with holes), various complexity results are known.

machine_generated.summary: The art gallery problem was recently shown to be ETR-complete, but many variants and approximation questions remain open.
decomposition: Determine the approximability of the art gallery problem

title: Berman-Hartmanis Isomorphism Conjecture

statement.canonical: The Berman-Hartmanis conjecture states that all NP-complete languages are polynomial-time isomorphic to each other. That is, for any two NP-complete languages A and B, there exists a polynomial-time computable bijection f with a polynomial-time computable inverse such that x is in A iff f(x) is in B.
statement.informal: Are all NP-complete problems essentially the same under efficient renaming? Can we always find a bijective efficient translation between any two NP-complete problems?

why_it_matters: The isomorphism conjecture, if true, would show that all NP-complete problems share a deep structural similarity beyond mere polynomial-time reducibility. Its resolution is connected to the existence of one-way functions: Agrawal (2002) showed the conjecture is false if and only if P != NP and one-way functions exist.

canonical.title: On Isomorphisms and Density of NP and Other Complete Sets

status_evidence.title: Pseudo-random Generators and the Frequency of Simplicity

partial_results.summary: Joseph and Young (1985) proved the conjecture holds for all known 'natural' NP-complete sets.
Agrawal (2002) showed the conjecture fails iff P != NP and one-way functions exist, linking it to cryptography.

machine_generated.summary: The Berman-Hartmanis conjecture posits that all NP-complete languages are polynomial-time isomorphic, and its truth depends on the existence of one-way functions.
decomposition: Resolve the connection between isomorphism conjecture and one-way functions
Determine whether natural NP-complete problems exhibit additional structural properties

title: Black-Box Separations of Cryptographic Primitives

statement.canonical: Can the known black-box separations between cryptographic primitives be strengthened or circumvented? Specifically, which implications between primitives (e.g., OWF -> PKE, OWF -> OT, CRH -> OWF) can be established via non-black-box techniques, and which represent genuine impossibilities?
statement.informal: We know many cryptographic tools cannot be built from simpler ones using standard "black-box" techniques. But are these limitations fundamental, or can clever non-standard techniques overcome them?

why_it_matters: Black-box separations map out the landscape of cryptographic assumptions. Understanding which separations are genuine impossibilities versus artifacts of proof techniques would fundamentally reshape our understanding of the minimal assumptions needed for various cryptographic tasks.

canonical.title: Notions of Reducibility between Cryptographic Primitives

status_evidence.title: How to Go Beyond the Black-Box Simulation Barrier

partial_results.summary: Impagliazzo and Rudich (1989) initiated the study of black-box separations, showing OWF does not imply key exchange in a black-box way.
Barak (2001) gave the first non-black-box zero-knowledge proof, showing non-black-box techniques can overcome some barriers.
Simon (1998) proved that CRH cannot be black-box constructed from OWF.

machine_generated.summary: Determining which black-box separations between cryptographic primitives represent genuine impossibilities and which can be overcome by non-black-box techniques is a central question in theoretical cryptography.
decomposition: Develop non-black-box constructions that bypass known separations
Prove stronger separation results that apply to non-black-box techniques

title: BPP vs P Problem
aliases: Does randomness help in polynomial-time computation?

statement.canonical: Let BPP (Bounded-error Probabilistic Polynomial time) denote the class of languages decidable by a probabilistic Turing machine in polynomial time with error probability at most 1/3 on every input. Let P denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time. It is known that P is contained in BPP. Does P = BPP? That is, can every efficient randomized algorithm be derandomized into an efficient deterministic algorithm?
statement.informal: Does access to random coin flips give computers any extra power for solving problems efficiently? Or can every problem that can be solved quickly with randomness also be solved quickly without it? Most experts believe randomness does not help — that P = BPP — but proving this remains open.

why_it_matters: The BPP vs P question is central to understanding the power of randomness in computation. Many practical algorithms (primality testing, polynomial identity testing, randomized rounding) use randomness for efficiency. If P = BPP, then randomness is merely a convenience, not a fundamental computational resource. The question connects to pseudorandom generators, circuit complexity lower bounds, and the hardness-randomness tradeoff paradigm of Nisan-Wigderson and Impagliazzo-Wigderson.

canonical.title: P = BPP if E Requires Exponential Circuits: Derandomizing the XOR Lemma
Hardness vs Randomness
canonical.locator: Journal of Computer and System Sciences, 49(2), pp. 149-167

status_evidence.title: Derandomization: A Brief Overview
Recent Developments in Explicit Constructions of Extractors

partial_results.summary: Impagliazzo and Wigderson showed that if any problem in E = DTIME(2^{O(n)}) requires exponential-size circuits, then P = BPP.
Sipser showed BPP is contained in the polynomial hierarchy (specifically in Sigma_2^P intersect Pi_2^P).
AKS primality test derandomized the Miller-Rabin primality test, showing PRIMES is in P, providing a concrete example of successful derandomization.
It is known that BPP is contained in P/poly (BPP has polynomial-size circuit families), due to Adleman's theorem.

machine_generated.summary: The BPP vs P problem asks whether randomness provides any additional power for polynomial-time computation, with most experts conjecturing that P = BPP through derandomization.
decomposition: Prove strong enough circuit lower bounds to enable the Nisan-Wigderson derandomization framework
Construct explicit pseudorandom generators with seed length O(log n) for general polynomial-time computations

title: BQP vs NP Problem
aliases: Can quantum computers solve NP-complete problems?

statement.canonical: Let BQP (Bounded-error Quantum Polynomial time) denote the class of decision problems solvable by a quantum computer in polynomial time with error probability at most 1/3. Let NP denote the class of decision problems for which a "yes" answer can be verified in polynomial time given a suitable certificate. What is the relationship between BQP and NP? Specifically, is NP contained in BQP, is BQP contained in NP, or are they incomparable?
statement.informal: Quantum computers can solve some problems faster than classical computers (like factoring integers). But can quantum computers efficiently solve all problems whose solutions can be quickly verified? In other words, can quantum computers crack NP-complete problems like the traveling salesman problem? Most experts believe quantum computers cannot solve all NP problems efficiently, but this has not been proven.

why_it_matters: Understanding the relationship between BQP and NP is central to determining the power and limitations of quantum computation. If NP were contained in BQP, quantum computers would be able to efficiently solve NP-complete problems, with revolutionary consequences for optimization, cryptography, and artificial intelligence. If BQP is not contained in NP, it would mean quantum computers can solve problems that have no efficiently verifiable proofs, which would be equally surprising. The question also impacts the security of post-quantum cryptography and the practical motivation for building quantum computers.

canonical.title: Quantum Complexity Theory
NP-complete Problems and Physical Reality

status_evidence.title: BQP and the Polynomial Hierarchy

partial_results.summary: Bennett, Bernstein, Brassard, and Vazirani showed that relative to a random oracle, BQP does not contain NP, providing evidence that quantum computers cannot solve NP-complete problems.
Grover's algorithm gives a quadratic speedup for unstructured search, but this does not place NP inside BQP.
Aaronson showed that relative to an oracle, BQP is not contained in the polynomial hierarchy, demonstrating that BQP has problems potentially not in PH (and hence not in NP).
Raz and Tal proved an oracle separation between BQP and PH, showing that relative to an oracle, BQP is not contained in PH.
It is known that BQP is contained in AWPP (which is contained in PP), and BQP contains BPP, but the relationship to NP remains unresolved.

machine_generated.summary: The BQP vs NP problem asks whether quantum computers can efficiently solve NP-complete problems, with most evidence suggesting they cannot but no proof exists.
decomposition: Strengthen oracle separation results between BQP and NP to obtain unconditional separations
Develop quantum query complexity and communication complexity techniques to clarify structural relationships

title: BQP vs the Polynomial Hierarchy
aliases: Quantum computing and the polynomial hierarchy

statement.canonical: Is BQP contained in the polynomial hierarchy (PH)? It is known that BQP is contained in PP (which is in PSPACE), and there is an oracle relative to which BQP is not in PH. But in the unrelativized setting, is BQP inside or outside PH?
statement.informal: Does quantum computing fit within the classical complexity hierarchy, or does it transcend it? If BQP is outside PH, then quantum computers solve problems that are fundamentally beyond what classical computation can approximate even with alternating quantifiers.

why_it_matters: Placing BQP relative to PH would clarify the computational power of quantum mechanics. If BQP is outside PH, quantum computing represents a genuinely new kind of computational power not captured by the classical hierarchy.

canonical.title: BQP and the Polynomial Hierarchy

status_evidence.title: Oracle Separation of BQP and PH

partial_results.summary: Aaronson (2010) showed BQP is contained in PP and proved a 'quantum PH' analog.
Raz and Tal (2019) proved an oracle separation of BQP from PH, a major breakthrough.
The unrelativized question remains fully open.

machine_generated.summary: Whether BQP is contained in the polynomial hierarchy is a fundamental question about where quantum computation fits in the classical complexity landscape.
decomposition: Develop non-relativizing techniques to place BQP relative to PH
Exploit the Raz-Tal oracle separation toward unrelativized results

title: Optimal Cache-Oblivious Algorithms

statement.canonical: Cache-oblivious algorithms operate efficiently in the memory hierarchy without knowing cache parameters (block size B, cache size M). For which problems can cache-oblivious algorithms match the performance of cache-aware algorithms? Is there a separation: a problem where cache-aware algorithms are provably more efficient than any cache-oblivious algorithm?
statement.informal: Algorithms that automatically adapt to the memory hierarchy without knowing its parameters can often match algorithms that are specifically designed for the cache. Is this always possible, or are there problems where knowledge of cache parameters provably helps?

why_it_matters: Cache-oblivious algorithms are practically important because they automatically perform well across diverse hardware. Understanding whether they can always match cache-aware algorithms would determine the limits of hardware-independent algorithm design.

canonical.title: Cache-Oblivious Algorithms

status_evidence.title: On the Limits of Cache-Obliviousness

partial_results.summary: Frigo et al. (1999) showed that cache-oblivious algorithms match cache-aware for sorting, matrix multiplication, and FFT.
Brodal and Fagerberg (2003) showed funnel sort is an optimal cache-oblivious sorting algorithm.
For some problems like priority queues, small gaps between cache-aware and cache-oblivious are known.

machine_generated.summary: Whether cache-oblivious algorithms can always match cache-aware performance, or whether there are provable separations, is a fundamental question in algorithm design for modern hardware.
