Develop optimal cache-oblivious algorithms for remaining open problems

title: Capacity-Achieving Code with Linear-Time Encoding and Decoding

statement.canonical: Shannon's channel coding theorem guarantees the existence of codes that achieve the capacity of any discrete memoryless channel with vanishing error probability as the block length tends to infinity. However, Shannon's proof is non-constructive. Does there exist an explicit family of codes that simultaneously: (1) achieves the capacity of the binary symmetric channel (or more generally, any discrete memoryless channel), (2) has a linear-time encoding algorithm (O(n) time for block length n), and (3) has a linear-time decoding algorithm (O(n) time for block length n)?
statement.informal: Shannon proved in 1948 that reliable communication over a noisy channel is possible up to a fundamental limit called the channel capacity. But can we build practical codes that reach this limit while being extremely fast — encoding and decoding each message in time proportional to its length?

why_it_matters: Finding capacity-achieving codes with linear-time complexity would be a major breakthrough at the intersection of information theory, coding theory, and algorithm design. While LDPC codes and polar codes approach capacity and have near-linear complexity, achieving exact capacity with strictly linear-time encoding and decoding for general channels remains open. Such codes would have enormous practical impact in communications, storage, and any system where data must be transmitted reliably over noisy channels at the highest possible rate with minimal computational overhead.

canonical.title: A Mathematical Theory of Communication
Channel Polarization: A Method for Constructing Capacity-Achieving Codes for Symmetric Binary-Input Memoryless Channels
canonical.locator: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory, 55(7), pp. 3051-3073

status_evidence.title: Modern Coding Theory

partial_results.summary: Gallager's LDPC codes (1962) empirically approach capacity and have near-linear decoding complexity under iterative message-passing decoding, but provable capacity-achievement with linear-time decoding is not established for general channels.
Arikan's polar codes (2009) provably achieve the capacity of any binary-input symmetric memoryless channel with encoding and decoding complexity O(n log n).
Spielman showed how to construct linear-time encodable and decodable codes that correct a constant fraction of errors (for adversarial channels), but these do not achieve Shannon capacity.
Pfister and Urbanke showed that spatially coupled LDPC codes universally achieve capacity on the BEC with near-linear complexity under belief propagation decoding.
Polar codes with successive cancellation list decoding achieve competitive performance in 5G NR standards, with O(n log n) complexity.

machine_generated.summary: This problem asks whether there exist explicit error-correcting codes that achieve Shannon channel capacity with both encoding and decoding running in linear time O(n).
decomposition: Improve polar code decoding from O(n log n) to O(n) while maintaining capacity-achieving performance
Design new code families combining LDPC-like structure with provable capacity-achieving guarantees and linear-time algorithms

title: Circuit Complexity of Explicit Functions

statement.canonical: Prove a superlinear lower bound on the circuit complexity of an explicit Boolean function. Specifically, exhibit a function f in NP (or even in P) and prove that any Boolean circuit computing f requires more than C*n gates for every constant C, where n is the input length.
statement.informal: Can we prove that some specific, natural Boolean function requires large circuits to compute? Despite knowing that most functions require exponential-size circuits (by counting arguments), we cannot prove more than a roughly 3n lower bound for any explicit function.

why_it_matters: The inability to prove strong circuit lower bounds for explicit functions is one of the most embarrassing gaps in complexity theory. The best known lower bound for an explicit function is approximately 3n - o(n) by Blum (1984) and Lachish-Raz (2001). Any significant improvement would constitute a major breakthrough.

canonical.title: A Boolean Function Requiring 3n Network Size

status_evidence.title: A Better-than-3n Lower Bound for the Circuit Complexity of an Explicit Function

partial_results.summary: Blum (1984) proved a 3n - o(n) lower bound for an explicit function, which remained the record for over 30 years.
Find, Golovnev, Hirsch, and Kulikov (2016) proved a (3 + 1/86)n - o(n) lower bound, the first improvement beyond 3n.
Shannon (1949) showed by counting that most Boolean functions require circuits of size Omega(2^n/n), but this is non-constructive.

machine_generated.summary: The problem asks for superlinear circuit lower bounds for explicit Boolean functions. The best known bound is barely above 3n, far from the exponential bounds known to exist non-constructively.
decomposition: Develop new proof techniques beyond gate elimination
Bridge the gap between counting-based existential bounds and explicit constructions

title: Log-Rank Conjecture in Communication Complexity

statement.canonical: The log-rank conjecture (Lovász and Saks, 1988) states that the deterministic communication complexity of a Boolean function f(x,y) is at most polylogarithmic in the rank of its communication matrix M_f (where M_f[x,y] = f(x,y) over the reals). Is this conjecture true?
statement.informal: When two parties need to compute a joint function of their inputs by exchanging messages, is the amount of communication needed always bounded by a polynomial in the logarithm of the rank of the function's truth table viewed as a matrix?

why_it_matters: The log-rank conjecture is one of the most important open problems in communication complexity. It connects algebraic properties (matrix rank) to combinatorial ones (communication cost) and has implications for circuit complexity, data structures, and learning theory.

canonical.title: Lattices, Möbius Functions and Communication Complexity

status_evidence.title: The Log-Approximate-Rank Conjecture is False

partial_results.summary: Lovász and Saks (1988) proved the communication complexity is at most O(rank(M_f)), and conjectured polylog(rank) suffices.
Chattopadhyay, Mande, and Sherif (2019) disproved the approximate version (log-approximate-rank conjecture), but the exact version remains open.
Sudakov and Tomon (2023) proved a nearly tight bound of O(sqrt(rank)), improving previous results.

machine_generated.summary: The log-rank conjecture asks whether deterministic communication complexity is polylogarithmic in the rank of the communication matrix, connecting algebra and combinatorics.
decomposition: Improve the upper bound from O(sqrt(rank)) toward polylog(rank)
Develop new combinatorial or algebraic techniques for analyzing communication protocols

title: Computational-Statistical Gap

statement.canonical: For many statistical estimation problems, the information-theoretically optimal sample complexity is much smaller than what any known polynomial-time algorithm achieves. Is there a fundamental computational-statistical gap, where polynomial-time algorithms provably require more samples than information-theoretic bounds? Can we prove such gaps unconditionally, or at least under standard complexity assumptions?
statement.informal: Some learning problems can be solved with very few examples if we have unlimited computation time, but all known efficient algorithms need many more examples. Is this gap inherent, or are we missing better algorithms?

why_it_matters: The computational-statistical gap is central to understanding the limits of efficient machine learning. It determines whether statistical optimality and computational efficiency can be simultaneously achieved, with implications for sparse recovery, community detection, and planted problems.

canonical.title: Computational Lower Bounds for Sparse PCA

status_evidence.title: Reducibility and Statistical-Computational Gaps from Secret Leakage

partial_results.summary: Berthet and Rigollet (2013) showed computational-statistical gaps for sparse PCA assuming the planted clique conjecture.
Brennan and Bresler (2020) developed a framework for proving computational-statistical gaps via reductions between average-case problems.
Unconditional computational-statistical gaps (not relying on conjectures) remain open except in restricted models like SQ.

machine_generated.summary: The computational-statistical gap problem asks whether some learning tasks inherently require more data for efficient algorithms than for unbounded computation, a fundamental question in modern learning theory.
decomposition: Prove unconditional computational-statistical gaps beyond the SQ model
Develop a comprehensive theory of average-case reductions for statistical problems

title: Dense Model Theorem Extensions

statement.canonical: The dense model theorem (used by Green, Tao, and Ziegler) states that if a set S is dense in a pseudorandom set R, then S has a dense model in the full universe that is indistinguishable from S by bounded-complexity tests. Can this theorem be extended to stronger computational models, tighter parameters, or broader settings while maintaining efficiency?
statement.informal: Can we find efficient dense approximations to sparse structured sets in more general computational settings? This has connections to both additive combinatorics and complexity theory.

why_it_matters: The dense model theorem bridges additive combinatorics and computational complexity. Extensions could yield new derandomization results, improve our understanding of pseudorandomness, and lead to breakthroughs in both areas.

canonical.title: Dense Subsets of Pseudorandom Sets

status_evidence.title: Decompositions, Approximate Structure, Transference, and the Hahn-Banach Theorem

partial_results.summary: Reingold, Trevisan, Tulsiani, and Vadhan (2008) gave a computational proof of the dense model theorem with applications to derandomization.
Gowers (2010) gave a simplified proof using the Hahn-Banach theorem, opening new connections to functional analysis.
Zhang (2011) used dense model ideas to extend Green-Tao-type results; optimal computational parameters remain unknown.

machine_generated.summary: The dense model theorem extensions problem asks for broader and more efficient versions of the dense model theorem with applications to derandomization and additive combinatorics.
decomposition: Optimize parameters in the computational dense model theorem
Extend to stronger computational models and richer combinatorial settings

title: Derandomization: Does P = BPP?

statement.canonical: BPP is the class of languages decidable in polynomial time by a probabilistic Turing machine with bounded error probability. Is P = BPP? That is, can every randomized polynomial-time algorithm be derandomized to a deterministic polynomial-time algorithm?
statement.informal: Is randomness ever truly useful for efficient computation? Most experts believe P = BPP, meaning that randomized algorithms can always be made deterministic without significant slowdown, but a proof is lacking.

why_it_matters: Proving P = BPP would show that randomness is not a fundamental computational resource for efficient decision-making. This has profound implications for algorithm design, cryptography, and our understanding of computational resources. The Impagliazzo-Wigderson framework shows P = BPP follows from circuit lower bounds.

canonical.title: P = BPP if E requires exponential circuits: Derandomizing the XOR lemma

status_evidence.title: In a world of P = BPP

partial_results.summary: Impagliazzo and Wigderson (1997) proved P = BPP if E = DTIME(2^O(n)) requires exponential-size circuits.
Nisan and Wigderson (1994) constructed pseudorandom generators from hard functions, establishing the hardness-randomness connection.
Specific problems like primality testing (AKS 2004) have been derandomized, supporting the P = BPP conjecture.

machine_generated.summary: The P = BPP conjecture asks whether randomness can always be eliminated from efficient algorithms, which is widely believed but unproven unconditionally.
decomposition: Prove sufficiently strong circuit lower bounds to apply the Impagliazzo-Wigderson framework
Develop unconditional derandomization techniques for restricted models

title: Dynamic Connectivity Optimal Bounds
aliases: Dynamic connectivity lower and upper bounds

statement.canonical: In the dynamic connectivity problem, we maintain an undirected graph under edge insertions and deletions while answering connectivity queries. Holm, de Lichtenberg, and Thorup (2001) achieved O(log^2 n) amortized time per operation. Kapron, King, and Mountjoy (2013) achieved O(log^4 n) worst-case. What are the optimal bounds for both amortized and worst-case dynamic connectivity?
statement.informal: When a graph changes over time, how fast can we determine whether two vertices are connected? The known algorithms take O(log^2 n) amortized time, but it is unknown if this is optimal.

why_it_matters: Dynamic connectivity is a fundamental data structure problem with applications in network monitoring, incremental algorithms, and computational geometry. The gap between known O(log^2 n) upper bounds and Omega(log n) lower bounds is open.

canonical.title: Poly-Logarithmic Deterministic Fully-Dynamic Algorithms for Connectivity

status_evidence.title: Logarithmic Lower Bounds in the Cell-Probe Model

partial_results.summary: Holm, de Lichtenberg, and Thorup (2001) achieved O(log^2 n) amortized update time for dynamic connectivity.
Pǎtraşcu and Demaine (2006) proved an Omega(log n) cell probe lower bound for dynamic connectivity.
Wulff-Nilsen (2013) improved the worst-case bound, and Huang, Huang, Kopelowitz, and Pettie (2017) achieved O(log^4 n) worst-case time.

machine_generated.summary: Closing the gap between O(log^2 n) upper bound and Omega(log n) lower bound for dynamic connectivity is a central open problem in dynamic graph algorithms.
decomposition: Prove Omega(log^2 n) lower bound or achieve O(log n) upper bound
Determine optimal worst-case bounds for dynamic connectivity

title: Dynamic Graph Algorithms Lower Bounds
aliases: Cell probe lower bounds for dynamic graphs

statement.canonical: Can we prove polynomial (omega(polylog n)) unconditional lower bounds for dynamic graph problems in the cell probe model? For problems like dynamic reachability, dynamic shortest paths, or dynamic maximum matching, the best known unconditional lower bounds are only logarithmic despite polynomial conditional lower bounds from SETH/OMv.
statement.informal: When a graph changes over time (edges are inserted and deleted), we want to efficiently answer queries about it. Can we prove that some natural dynamic graph problems truly require significant update or query time?

why_it_matters: Dynamic graph algorithms are central to network analysis, databases, and optimization. The gap between conditional lower bounds (polynomial, from SETH) and unconditional lower bounds (logarithmic) is enormous. Closing this gap is a major challenge in data structure lower bounds.

canonical.title: Logarithmic Lower Bounds in the Cell-Probe Model

status_evidence.title: Conditional Lower Bounds for Dynamic Problems

partial_results.summary: Pǎtraşcu and Demaine (2004) proved Omega(log n) cell probe lower bounds for several dynamic problems.
Henzinger, Krinninger, and Nanongkai (2015) proved polynomial conditional lower bounds from OMv conjecture.
Larsen (2012) proved the first data structure lower bound with polynomial gap between updates and queries.

machine_generated.summary: Proving unconditional polynomial lower bounds for dynamic graph problems is a central challenge in data structures, with a large gap between conditional and unconditional results.
decomposition: Develop new techniques for cell probe lower bounds beyond logarithmic
Bridge conditional lower bounds (from OMv/SETH) to unconditional results

title: Dynamic Optimality Conjecture for Binary Search Trees

statement.canonical: The dynamic optimality conjecture (Sleator and Tarjan, 1985) states that splay trees are O(1)-competitive with any offline binary search tree algorithm. That is, for any sequence of accesses, the total cost of splay tree operations is at most a constant factor times the cost of the optimal offline BST for that sequence. Is this conjecture true?
statement.informal: Is the splay tree always within a constant factor of the best possible binary search tree for any access sequence, even compared to one that knows the future?

why_it_matters: Dynamic optimality would establish splay trees as universally optimal BSTs, meaning no other BST algorithm can do significantly better on any access pattern. This is one of the most important open problems in data structures.

canonical.title: Self-Adjusting Binary Search Trees

status_evidence.title: Dynamic Optimality — Almost

partial_results.summary: Sleator and Tarjan (1985) proved splay trees satisfy the static optimality theorem and many other access lemmas.
Demaine et al. (2007) constructed Tango trees achieving O(log log n)-competitive ratio, nearly matching the conjecture.
The conjecture remains open despite decades of effort; even O(log log n)-competitiveness was only achieved in 2004.

machine_generated.summary: The dynamic optimality conjecture asks whether splay trees are O(1)-competitive with any offline BST, making them universally optimal among BSTs.
decomposition: Close the gap between O(log log n)-competitive and O(1)-competitive
Develop better lower bound techniques for BST model

title: ETH vs SETH Gap
aliases: Exponential Time Hypothesis vs Strong ETH

statement.canonical: The Exponential Time Hypothesis (ETH) states that 3-SAT cannot be solved in 2^o(n) time. The Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis (SETH) states that for every epsilon > 0, there exists k such that k-SAT cannot be solved in O(2^((1-epsilon)n)) time. Does ETH imply SETH, or are they independent? More broadly, what is the relationship between these hypotheses and their fine-grained consequences?
statement.informal: Are the two main hardness assumptions in fine-grained complexity theory (ETH and SETH) equivalent, or does the stronger one (SETH) really add power? What are the exact consequences of each assumption?

why_it_matters: ETH and SETH are the foundational hypotheses of fine-grained complexity theory. They imply tight conditional lower bounds for many natural problems. Understanding whether SETH is strictly stronger than ETH would clarify the landscape of fine-grained complexity.

canonical.title: Which Problems Have Strongly Exponential Complexity?

status_evidence.title: Hardness of Easy Problems: Basing Hardness on Popular Conjectures such as the Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis

partial_results.summary: Impagliazzo, Paturi, and Zane (2001) proved the sparsification lemma showing ETH for 3-SAT implies ETH for k-SAT for every k.
SETH implies numerous conditional lower bounds including for edit distance, LCS, and OV that ETH alone does not seem to imply.
It is unknown whether ETH implies SETH; they may be independent hypotheses.

machine_generated.summary: The ETH vs SETH gap problem asks whether the Strong Exponential Time Hypothesis is strictly stronger than the Exponential Time Hypothesis, with implications throughout fine-grained complexity theory.
decomposition: Determine whether ETH implies SETH or construct an oracle separation
Identify problems with tight conditional lower bounds from SETH but not ETH

title: Existence of One-Way Functions

statement.canonical: A function f: {0,1}* -> {0,1}* is called a one-way function if (1) f is computable in polynomial time, and (2) for every probabilistic polynomial-time algorithm A, the probability that A(f(x), 1^|x|) outputs some x' with f(x') = f(x) is negligible in |x|, where x is chosen uniformly at random. Do one-way functions exist? Equivalently, does there exist a polynomial-time computable function that cannot be inverted by any probabilistic polynomial-time algorithm with non-negligible probability?
statement.informal: A one-way function is easy to compute but hard to reverse. For example, multiplying two large prime numbers is easy, but finding the prime factors of the product is believed to be hard. Do functions with this property actually exist, or can every efficiently computable function be efficiently inverted? The entire field of cryptography rests on the assumption that such functions exist.

why_it_matters: The existence of one-way functions is the minimal assumption on which almost all of modern cryptography is built. Impagliazzo and Luby showed that one-way functions exist if and only if pseudorandom generators exist, which in turn are equivalent to the existence of pseudorandom functions, commitment schemes, and digital signatures. The existence of one-way functions implies P != NP, but P != NP alone is not known to imply the existence of one-way functions. Resolving this question would either validate or invalidate the theoretical foundations of cryptography.

canonical.title: New Directions in Cryptography
canonical.locator: IEEE Transactions on Information Theory, 22(6), pp. 644-654

canonical.title: One-Way Functions Are Essential for Complexity Based Cryptography

status_evidence.title: Foundations of Cryptography, Volume 1: Basic Tools
A Personal View of Average-Case Complexity

partial_results.summary: Impagliazzo and Luby proved that one-way functions exist if and only if pseudorandom generators exist.
Hastad, Impagliazzo, Levin, and Luby showed that any one-way function can be transformed into a pseudorandom generator.
No candidate one-way function has been proven to be one-way; the security of candidates like integer factorization and discrete logarithm remains conjectural.

Liu and Pass showed connections between one-way functions and time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity, proving that one-way functions exist iff the time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity problem is hard on average.

machine_generated.summary: This problem asks whether one-way functions exist -- functions that are easy to compute but hard to invert -- which is the minimal assumption underlying all of modern cryptography.
decomposition: Prove average-case hardness for candidate one-way functions such as factoring or lattice-based constructions
Establish connections between worst-case and average-case complexity to derive one-way functions from P != NP

title: Formula vs Circuit Complexity Gap

statement.canonical: A Boolean formula is a circuit where every gate has fan-out one. It is known that formulas can be at most quadratically less efficient than circuits. Is there an explicit function with polynomial-size circuits but requiring super-polynomial-size formulas? Equivalently, is NC^1 strictly contained in P/poly?
statement.informal: Formulas are a restricted form of circuits where intermediate results cannot be reused. Is there a natural function that can be computed by small circuits but requires very large formulas?

why_it_matters: Understanding the gap between formula and circuit complexity illuminates the power of reusing intermediate computations. An explicit separation would advance our understanding of circuit depth vs size tradeoffs and is related to the NC^1 vs NC^2 question.

canonical.title: Realizations of Linear Functions by Formulas Using ∧, ∨, ¬

status_evidence.title: The Shrinkage Exponent of de Morgan Formulas is 2

partial_results.summary: Nechiporuk (1966) proved an Omega(n^2/log n) formula size lower bound for explicit functions using a counting argument.
Håstad (1998) proved the shrinkage exponent of de Morgan formulas is exactly 2, confirming Subbotovskaya's lower bound is tight for random restrictions.
No super-quadratic formula lower bounds are known for explicit functions.

machine_generated.summary: The formula vs circuit complexity gap problem asks for explicit functions with polynomial circuit complexity but super-polynomial formula complexity.
decomposition: Prove super-quadratic formula size lower bounds for explicit functions
Develop new techniques beyond Nechiporuk's method

title: Graph Isomorphism Problem Complexity

statement.canonical: The graph isomorphism problem (GI) asks, given two finite graphs G and H, whether there exists a bijection between their vertex sets that preserves adjacency. GI is known to be in NP but is neither known to be in P nor known to be NP-complete. What is the precise computational complexity of graph isomorphism? Specifically, is GI in P, or is it NP-complete, or does it have intermediate complexity?
statement.informal: Given two networks (graphs), is there a way to relabel the nodes of one so that it becomes identical to the other? We know this can be checked quickly once a relabeling is proposed, but we do not know whether the relabeling can always be found quickly. Is this problem fundamentally hard (NP-complete), efficiently solvable (in P), or something in between?

why_it_matters: Graph isomorphism is one of the few natural problems in NP that is neither known to be in P nor known to be NP-complete, making it a prime candidate for an NP-intermediate problem (which must exist if P != NP by Ladner's theorem). Its complexity status has implications for group theory, combinatorics, database theory, and chemistry (molecule comparison). Babai's 2015 quasipolynomial-time algorithm was a major breakthrough, but the gap between quasipolynomial and polynomial time remains.

canonical.title: Graph Isomorphism in Quasipolynomial Time
The Graph Isomorphism Problem: Its Structural Complexity

status_evidence.title: Group, Graphs, Algorithms: The Graph Isomorphism Problem

partial_results.summary: Babai achieved a quasipolynomial-time algorithm running in exp(polylog(n)) time, dramatically improving the previous best bound of exp(sqrt(n log n)) by Luks.
GI is known to be solvable in polynomial time for many restricted graph classes: planar graphs, bounded-degree graphs, bounded-genus graphs, and graphs of bounded treewidth.
Schoning showed that if GI is NP-complete, then the polynomial hierarchy collapses to Sigma_2^P, providing evidence against GI being NP-complete.
GI is in the complexity class SPP (a subclass of PP), shown by Arvind and Kurur.

machine_generated.summary: The graph isomorphism complexity problem asks whether determining if two graphs are structurally identical can be done in polynomial time, or has intermediate complexity between P and NP-complete.
decomposition: Improve Babai's quasipolynomial-time algorithm to achieve polynomial time
Develop group-theoretic and combinatorial tools to close the gap between quasipolynomial and polynomial complexity

title: Hardness vs Randomness Connections

statement.canonical: The hardness vs randomness paradigm establishes that circuit lower bounds imply derandomization. Is the converse true? Specifically, does P = BPP imply circuit lower bounds against E (or a related class)? More generally, are hardness assumptions exactly equivalent to derandomization results?
statement.informal: If randomness can be eliminated from efficient algorithms, does that necessarily mean hard problems exist? Is there a tight bidirectional connection between computational hardness and the uselessness of randomness?

why_it_matters: Establishing a tight equivalence between hardness and derandomization would unify two major themes in complexity theory. It would mean that understanding randomness is exactly the same as understanding circuit lower bounds.

canonical.title: Derandomizing Polynomial Identity Tests Means Proving Circuit Lower Bounds

status_evidence.title: Hardness vs Randomness, Revised: Uniform, Non-Black-Box, and Instance-Wise

partial_results.summary: Impagliazzo and Kabanets (2004) showed that derandomizing polynomial identity testing implies circuit lower bounds, establishing a partial converse.
Chen and Tell (2021) made significant progress toward non-black-box hardness-randomness connections.
Kabanets and Impagliazzo showed the equivalence holds in the algebraic setting for PIT.

machine_generated.summary: The hardness vs randomness problem asks whether derandomization is exactly equivalent to circuit lower bounds, seeking a tight bidirectional connection.
decomposition: Prove that P = BPP implies circuit lower bounds against E
Develop non-black-box hardness vs randomness connections

title: Fully Homomorphic Encryption Efficiency

statement.canonical: Fully homomorphic encryption (FHE) allows computation on encrypted data without decryption. Current FHE schemes incur significant overhead: ciphertext expansion is at least polylogarithmic and computation is orders of magnitude slower than plaintext computation. What is the minimum possible overhead for FHE? Can we achieve FHE with O(1) ciphertext expansion and computation overhead polynomial in the security parameter but independent of the computation depth?
statement.informal: Can we perform computations on encrypted data with only a small (ideally constant) overhead compared to computing on unencrypted data?

why_it_matters: FHE enables secure computation outsourcing, private database queries, and secure machine learning. Reducing the overhead from current impractical levels to near-optimal would make these applications practical.

canonical.title: Fully Homomorphic Encryption Using Ideal Lattices

status_evidence.title: Efficient Fully Homomorphic Encryption from (Standard) LWE

partial_results.summary: Gentry (2009) gave the first FHE construction, solving a 30-year-old open problem.
Brakerski and Vaikuntanathan (2014) constructed FHE from standard LWE assumption.
Current practical FHE schemes have overhead of 10,000x-1,000,000x for general computations.

machine_generated.summary: Reducing the computational overhead of fully homomorphic encryption to make it practical is one of the most important open problems at the intersection of cryptography and systems.
decomposition: Prove lower bounds on FHE overhead
Develop FHE schemes with rate-1 ciphertext expansion and minimal computation overhead

title: Ideal Lattice Problems Hardness

statement.canonical: Ideal lattices (lattices with algebraic structure arising from polynomial rings) are used in efficient lattice-based cryptography. Are computational problems on ideal lattices (such as Ring-LWE, Ring-SIS, and the Shortest Vector Problem on ideal lattices) as hard as their counterparts on general lattices? Or does the algebraic structure enable faster algorithms?
statement.informal: Lattice-based cryptography uses structured lattices for efficiency, but does that structure make the underlying mathematical problems easier to solve? If so, the security of many proposed post-quantum cryptosystems could be compromised.

why_it_matters: Most practical lattice-based cryptographic schemes (including NIST post-quantum standards like CRYSTALS-Kyber) rely on structured lattices. If ideal lattice problems are significantly easier than general lattice problems, these schemes could be broken.

canonical.title: On Ideal Lattices and Learning with Errors over Rings

status_evidence.title: Short Stickelberger Class Relations and Application to Ideal-SVP

partial_results.summary: Lyubashevsky, Peikert, and Regev (2010) proved worst-case to average-case reductions for Ring-LWE from ideal lattice problems.
Cramer, Ducas, and Wesolowski (2017) showed that SVP on ideal lattices in certain number fields can be solved faster than on general lattices.
No polynomial-time algorithm is known for ideal lattice problems in standard cyclotomic rings.

machine_generated.summary: Whether algebraic structure in ideal lattices makes core computational problems easier than on general lattices is critical for the security of post-quantum cryptographic standards.
decomposition: Determine whether SVP on ideal lattices admits subexponential algorithms
Prove or disprove worst-case hardness equivalence between ideal and general lattice problems

title: Indistinguishability Obfuscation from Standard Assumptions

statement.canonical: Indistinguishability obfuscation (iO) transforms a program into an equivalent but "unintelligible" program such that obfuscations of any two functionally equivalent programs are computationally indistinguishable. Can iO be constructed from well-studied, standard cryptographic assumptions (e.g., LWE, DDH) without relying on new or poorly understood assumptions?
statement.informal: Can we make computer programs unreadable while preserving their functionality, based on well-understood mathematical assumptions? Current constructions rely on novel assumptions whose security is less well understood.

why_it_matters: iO is often called the "master tool" of cryptography because it implies almost all other cryptographic primitives. Jain, Lin, and Sahai (2021) constructed iO from sub-exponential hardness of well-studied assumptions, but whether standard (polynomial) hardness suffices remains open.

canonical.title: Candidate Indistinguishability Obfuscation and Functional Encryption for All Circuits

status_evidence.title: Indistinguishability Obfuscation from Well-Founded Assumptions

partial_results.summary: Garg et al. (2013) gave the first candidate iO construction based on multilinear maps.
Jain, Lin, and Sahai (2021) constructed iO from sub-exponential LWE + SXDH + assumptions on pseudorandom generators.
Whether polynomial-security assumptions suffice, and whether the reliance on new PRG assumptions can be removed, remains open.

machine_generated.summary: Constructing indistinguishability obfuscation from purely standard polynomial-hardness assumptions is one of the most important open problems in cryptography.
decomposition: Remove the need for sub-exponential hardness in iO constructions
Base iO on standard LWE without additional assumptions

title: Optimal k-Clique Detection Algorithm

statement.canonical: What is the optimal time complexity for detecting whether an n-vertex graph contains a clique of size k? For k=3 (triangle detection), the best known algorithm runs in O(n^omega) time where omega is the matrix multiplication exponent. For general k, can we do significantly better than n^(omega*k/3) time? Is there an algorithm running in n^(o(k)) time?
statement.informal: How fast can we find a clique of size k in a graph? The naive approach takes about n^k time. Can we do much better, especially for large k?

why_it_matters: k-clique detection is a fundamental problem in graph algorithms. Its complexity is tightly connected to matrix multiplication, fine-grained complexity, and database query evaluation. Improvements would have broad algorithmic implications.

canonical.title: On the Complexity of the Subgraph Problem

status_evidence.title: Popular Conjectures Imply Strong Lower Bounds for Dynamic Problems

partial_results.summary: Nešetřil and Poljak (1985) showed k-clique can be detected in O(n^(omega*ceil(k/3))) time using matrix multiplication.
Alon, Yuster, and Zwick (1995) developed color-coding giving randomized O(n^k) algorithms parameterized for small k.
Under SETH, k-clique requires n^(Omega(k)) time, but unconditional lower bounds remain weak.

machine_generated.summary: Determining the optimal time complexity for k-clique detection in graphs is a central problem connecting graph algorithms, matrix multiplication, and fine-grained complexity theory.
decomposition: Improve k-clique algorithms beyond the matrix multiplication barrier
Establish conditional or unconditional lower bounds for k-clique

title: Kolmogorov Complexity and Cryptography

statement.canonical: Time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity Kt(x) measures the minimum description length of x among programs running in time t. Liu and Pass (2020) showed that the average-case hardness of computing Kt implies one-way functions. Is the converse true? Does the existence of one-way functions imply that Kt is hard on average? More broadly, what is the precise relationship between Kolmogorov complexity and cryptographic hardness?
statement.informal: Is the difficulty of compressing strings (computing Kolmogorov complexity) exactly equivalent to the existence of cryptographic hardness? Can we fully characterize cryptographic primitives through the lens of Kolmogorov complexity?

why_it_matters: Establishing a tight equivalence between Kolmogorov complexity and one-way functions would provide a beautiful unification of algorithmic information theory and cryptography, giving a new characterization of the minimal assumption for cryptography.

canonical.title: On One-way Functions and Kolmogorov Complexity

status_evidence.title: One-Way Functions and the Hardness of (Probabilistic) Time-Bounded Kolmogorov Complexity

partial_results.summary: Liu and Pass (2020) proved that average-case hardness of time-bounded Kolmogorov complexity implies one-way functions.
Hirahara (2023) established connections between MCSP, Kolmogorov complexity, and derandomization.
The full equivalence between OWFs and average-case hardness of Kt remains open.

machine_generated.summary: The relationship between Kolmogorov complexity and cryptographic hardness is being actively explored, with recent breakthroughs connecting time-bounded compression to one-way functions.
decomposition: Prove the converse: OWFs imply average-case hardness of Kt
Extend the connection to other cryptographic primitives beyond OWFs

title: L vs NL Problem

statement.canonical: Let L denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine using O(log n) space, and NL denote the class decidable by a nondeterministic Turing machine using O(log n) space. Does L = NL?
statement.informal: Can every problem solvable with a small amount of nondeterministic memory also be solved with the same amount of deterministic memory? The canonical NL-complete problem is directed graph reachability (st-connectivity).

why_it_matters: The L vs NL question is the space-bounded analogue of P vs NP. Resolving it would clarify the power of nondeterminism in space-bounded computation. Savitch's theorem shows NL is contained in L^2 (deterministic O(log^2 n) space), but closing the gap to L remains open.

canonical.title: Relationships between nondeterministic and deterministic tape complexities

status_evidence.title: Undirected connectivity in log-space

partial_results.summary: Savitch (1970) proved NL is contained in DSPACE(log^2 n), giving a quadratic blowup from nondeterministic to deterministic space.
Immerman and Szelepcsényi independently proved NL = coNL (1988), showing nondeterministic logspace is closed under complement.
Reingold (2008) proved undirected st-connectivity is in L, solving a major special case.

machine_generated.summary: The L vs NL problem asks whether deterministic logspace equals nondeterministic logspace, the space-bounded analogue of the P vs NP question.
decomposition: Improve Savitch's theorem to show NL is in DSPACE(o(log^2 n))

title: L vs P Problem

statement.canonical: Let L denote the class of languages decidable in deterministic logarithmic space, and P denote the class decidable in deterministic polynomial time. Does L = P?
statement.informal: Can every problem solvable in polynomial time also be solved using only logarithmic memory? This asks whether time-efficient computation can always be made space-efficient.

why_it_matters: The L vs P question concerns the fundamental relationship between time and space in computation. It is known that L is contained in P (by simulation), but separation has not been proven. Proving L != P would be a breakthrough in establishing time-space tradeoffs.

canonical.title: Characterizations of Pushdown Machines in Terms of Time-Bounded Computers

status_evidence.title: Computational Complexity: A Modern Approach

partial_results.summary: It is known that L is contained in NL is contained in P. No unconditional separation is known between any of these classes.
P-complete problems under logspace reductions (like circuit value problem) are believed to be outside L but this is unproven.

machine_generated.summary: The L vs P problem asks whether every polynomial-time problem can be solved in logarithmic space, probing the fundamental relationship between time and space in computation.
decomposition: Prove time-space tradeoff lower bounds for P-complete problems
Develop new techniques for separating space classes from time classes

title: LWE Hardness Exact Parameters

statement.canonical: The Learning with Errors (LWE) problem is to distinguish (A, As+e) from (A, u) where A is random, s is a secret, e is a noise vector, and u is uniform. What are the exact hardness parameters: for given dimension n and noise rate alpha, what is the precise computational complexity of LWE? Do the best known algorithms (BKZ, dual attacks) achieve optimal complexity?
statement.informal: How hard is the LWE problem as a function of its parameters? The answer determines the security levels of most lattice-based cryptographic schemes.

why_it_matters: LWE is the foundation of most lattice-based cryptography, including NIST post-quantum standards. Accurate hardness estimation is crucial for setting security parameters. Overestimation wastes performance; underestimation compromises security.

canonical.title: On Lattices, Learning with Errors, Random Linear Codes, and Cryptography

status_evidence.title: On the Concrete Hardness of Learning with Errors

partial_results.summary: Regev (2005) proved worst-case to average-case reduction, showing LWE is as hard as standard lattice problems.
The best known attacks run in roughly 2^(0.292n) time (using BKZ with sieving), but their exact complexity is debated.
The 'gate count' vs 'classical operations' debate affects practical parameter choices.

machine_generated.summary: Determining the exact computational hardness of LWE as a function of its parameters is critical for setting secure parameters in lattice-based cryptographic standards.
decomposition: Determine the exact asymptotic complexity of the best LWE attacks
Resolve the practical gap between theoretical and implemented attack complexities

title: Matrix Multiplication Exponent

statement.canonical: Define the matrix multiplication exponent omega as the infimum over all real numbers c such that two n x n matrices over a field can be multiplied using O(n^c) arithmetic operations. It is known that 2 <= omega < 2.372. What is the exact value of omega? In particular, does omega = 2?
statement.informal: Multiplying two n-by-n matrices the standard way takes about n^3 operations. Strassen showed in 1969 that it can be done faster. The fundamental question is: what is the best possible exponent? Can we multiply matrices in essentially n^2 operations (which is optimal since just reading the input takes n^2 time), or is the true exponent strictly greater than 2?

why_it_matters: Matrix multiplication is one of the most fundamental operations in computer science and mathematics, underlying algorithms for linear algebra, graph problems (transitive closure, shortest paths, matching), parsing, and scientific computing. The value of omega directly determines the best possible running time for all these applications. Determining whether omega = 2 is one of the most important open problems in algebraic complexity theory. Each improvement in upper bounds has had widespread algorithmic consequences.

canonical.title: Gaussian Elimination Is Not Optimal
Matrix Multiplication via Arithmetic Progressions

status_evidence.title: New Bounds for Matrix Multiplication: from Alpha to Omega

partial_results.summary: Strassen showed omega < 2.808 by giving an algorithm for 2x2 matrix multiplication using 7 multiplications instead of 8.
Coppersmith and Winograd achieved omega < 2.376 using the laser method on the Coppersmith-Winograd tensor.
Le Gall improved the bound to omega < 2.3729 using the powers of the Coppersmith-Winograd tensor.
Alman and Vassilevska Williams improved the bound to omega < 2.3728596.
Duan, Wu, and Zhou further improved the bound to omega < 2.371866.

machine_generated.summary: This problem seeks to determine the optimal exponent omega for matrix multiplication, specifically whether two n-by-n matrices can be multiplied in essentially O(n^2) operations.
decomposition: Discover new tensor decomposition techniques or algebraic identities to improve upper bounds toward omega = 2
Prove lower bounds on the value of omega using barriers to current laser-method approaches

title: Maximum Flow in Linear Time

statement.canonical: Can the maximum flow in a network with m edges and n vertices be computed in O(m) time (linear in the number of edges)? Chen et al. (2022) achieved an almost-linear time algorithm running in m^(1+o(1)) time. Can the o(1) term in the exponent be eliminated entirely?
statement.informal: Can we compute the maximum flow through a network in time proportional to just reading the input? Recent breakthroughs got very close, but truly linear time remains elusive.

why_it_matters: Maximum flow is a fundamental optimization problem with applications in transportation, network design, bipartite matching, and image segmentation. Achieving truly linear time would be optimal and would represent the culmination of 70 years of research.

canonical.title: Maximum Flow and Minimum-Cost Flow in Almost-Linear Time

status_evidence.title: Beyond the Flow Decomposition Barrier

partial_results.summary: Ford and Fulkerson (1956) introduced the max-flow min-cut theorem and augmenting path algorithms.
Chen et al. (2022) achieved m^(1+o(1)) time for max flow and min-cost flow, a major breakthrough.
Truly linear O(m) time remains open even for unit-capacity graphs.

machine_generated.summary: Whether maximum flow can be computed in truly linear O(m) time is open, with the best known algorithm achieving m^(1+o(1)) time.
decomposition: Eliminate the o(1) term from the almost-linear time max flow algorithm

title: Minimum Circuit Size Problem Complexity

statement.canonical: The Minimum Circuit Size Problem (MCSP) asks: given the truth table of a Boolean function f and a parameter s, does there exist a Boolean circuit of size at most s that computes f? Determine the exact computational complexity of MCSP. In particular, is MCSP NP-complete?
statement.informal: Given a complete description of a Boolean function and a size bound, can we efficiently determine whether there exists a small circuit computing that function? This problem is known to be in NP but its exact hardness is a mystery.

why_it_matters: MCSP occupies a unique position in complexity theory. It is a natural NP problem whose NP-completeness status is unknown, and it has deep connections to one-way functions, natural proofs, learning theory, and Kolmogorov complexity. Resolving its complexity could have far-reaching consequences for cryptography and circuit complexity.

canonical.title: Circuit Minimization Problem

status_evidence.title: The Complexity of Complexity

partial_results.summary: Kabanets and Cai (2000) showed that MCSP is not NP-complete under standard (p-many-one) reductions unless EXP != ZPP.
Allender and Das (2014) connected MCSP to Kolmogorov complexity and established hardness under randomized reductions.
Hirahara (2018) showed connections between MCSP and the existence of one-way functions.

machine_generated.summary: MCSP asks whether computing the minimum circuit size of a Boolean function given its truth table is NP-complete. It has deep connections to cryptography, learning theory, and fundamental complexity questions.
decomposition: Determine whether MCSP is NP-complete under some notion of reduction
Establish connections between MCSP hardness and the existence of one-way functions

title: Minimum Enclosing Ball Algorithms in High Dimensions

statement.canonical: Given n points in d-dimensional space, find the smallest ball enclosing all points. In fixed dimension, linear-time algorithms exist (Welzl 1991). For high dimensions (d growing with n), what is the optimal time complexity? Can we compute or (1+epsilon)-approximate the minimum enclosing ball in time polynomial in both n and d with optimal dependence on epsilon?
statement.informal: How fast can we find the smallest sphere containing a set of points, especially when the number of dimensions is large? This is easy in low dimensions but becomes challenging in high dimensions.

why_it_matters: The minimum enclosing ball is fundamental to clustering, classification, and computational geometry. In machine learning applications, high-dimensional data is common, making efficient high-dimensional algorithms essential.

canonical.title: Smallest Enclosing Disks (Balls and Ellipsoids)

status_evidence.title: Optimal Core-Sets for Balls

partial_results.summary: Welzl (1991) gave a randomized linear-time algorithm in fixed dimension d.
Bădoiu and Clarkson (2003) showed that (1+epsilon)-approximation can be achieved using O(1/epsilon) points (a core-set).
For exact computation in high dimensions, the complexity grows exponentially in d using known methods.

machine_generated.summary: The optimal complexity of minimum enclosing ball computation in high dimensions is open, with a gap between low-dimensional linear-time results and high-dimensional exponential-time methods.
decomposition: Determine optimal dependence on epsilon for (1+epsilon)-approximation in high dimensions
Develop algorithms with polynomial dependence on d for exact computation

title: Monotone Circuit Lower Bounds

statement.canonical: A monotone Boolean circuit uses only AND and OR gates (no NOT gates). Razborov (1985) proved exponential lower bounds for the monotone circuit complexity of the clique function. Can similar exponential lower bounds be proved for monotone functions that have polynomial-size general (non-monotone) circuits? Can the gap between monotone and general circuit complexity be fully characterized?
statement.informal: How much does negation help in circuit computation? For which problems does removing NOT gates make circuits exponentially larger?

why_it_matters: Monotone circuits are a natural restriction where strong lower bounds are known. Understanding the gap between monotone and general circuits could yield insights into the role of negation in computation and potentially transfer to general circuit lower bounds.

canonical.title: Lower Bounds on the Monotone Complexity of Some Boolean Functions

status_evidence.title: Deterministic Communication vs. Partition Number

partial_results.summary: Razborov (1985) proved exponential monotone circuit lower bounds for the clique function using the approximation method.
Tardos (1988) exhibited a monotone function in P (has poly-size general circuits) that requires exponential-size monotone circuits.
Göös, Pitassi, and Watson used monotone circuit techniques to resolve long-standing communication complexity conjectures.

machine_generated.summary: The monotone circuit lower bounds problem asks for a complete characterization of the gap between monotone and general circuit complexity, and whether monotone techniques can be leveraged for general lower bounds.
decomposition: Characterize which functions exhibit exponential monotone-nonmonotone gaps
Develop techniques that transfer monotone lower bounds to general circuit lower bounds

title: Multi-Party Computation Optimal Round Complexity

statement.canonical: What is the minimum number of rounds required for secure multi-party computation (MPC) of general functions in various settings (honest majority, dishonest majority, computational vs information-theoretic security)? Can all functions be securely computed in a constant number of rounds with optimal communication complexity?
statement.informal: When multiple parties want to jointly compute a function of their private inputs without revealing anything else, how many rounds of communication are needed? What is the minimum possible?

why_it_matters: MPC is fundamental to privacy-preserving computation, secure voting, auctions, and machine learning on private data. Round complexity directly impacts latency in practical protocols.

canonical.title: How to Play Any Mental Game

status_evidence.title: Two-Round Multiparty Secure Computation from Minimal Assumptions

partial_results.summary: Yao (1986) showed two-party computation is possible in constant rounds using garbled circuits.
BMR (1990) extended constant-round MPC to multiple parties in the computational setting.
Two-round MPC has been constructed from LWE but optimal communication complexity in constant rounds remains open.

decomposition: Achieve two-round MPC from standard assumptions with optimal communication
Prove round lower bounds for MPC under specific assumptions

title: Minimum Spanning Tree Optimal Comparison Complexity

statement.canonical: What is the optimal number of comparisons needed to find the minimum spanning tree of a weighted graph? Chazelle (2000) gave a nearly-linear time algorithm running in O(m * alpha(m,n)) time. Pettie and Ramachandran (2002) gave an optimal algorithm in the comparison model, but its complexity is unknown -- it runs in O(T*(m,n)) time where T* is the optimal decision tree complexity for MST, which is not known to be linear. Is the optimal MST comparison complexity O(m)?
statement.informal: What is the minimum number of comparisons needed to find a minimum spanning tree? We know the answer is close to linear, but whether it is exactly linear is unknown.

why_it_matters: MST is one of the oldest problems in combinatorial optimization. Despite decades of research, whether a linear-time comparison-based MST algorithm exists remains one of the most tantalizing open questions in algorithm design.

canonical.title: An Optimal Minimum Spanning Tree Algorithm

status_evidence.title: A Minimum Spanning Tree Algorithm with Inverse-Ackermann Type Complexity

partial_results.summary: Chazelle (2000) achieved O(m*alpha(m,n)) time, where alpha is the inverse Ackermann function.
Pettie and Ramachandran (2002) gave an algorithm that is optimal among comparison-based algorithms but its complexity is unknown.
For integer weights, Fredman and Willard (1994) achieved linear expected time using non-comparison operations.

machine_generated.summary: Whether minimum spanning tree can be computed in linear time using comparisons is a classic open problem, with the best known algorithms nearly but not quite linear.
decomposition: Determine the decision tree complexity T*(m,n) for MST
Either achieve linear-time MST or prove a super-linear comparison lower bound

title: Multi-Armed Bandit Optimal Regret

statement.canonical: In the multi-armed bandit problem, a learner selects one of K arms at each round and observes a reward. For structured bandits (linear, contextual, combinatorial), what are the exact minimax regret bounds? For contextual bandits with large or infinite policy classes, what is the optimal regret as a function of the complexity of the policy class?
statement.informal: When making sequential decisions under uncertainty with limited feedback, what is the best possible performance for complex, structured settings? Many important variants of the bandit problem still lack tight bounds.

why_it_matters: Multi-armed bandits are fundamental to sequential decision-making with applications in clinical trials, recommendation systems, and online advertising. Tight regret bounds guide practical algorithm design.

canonical.title: Finite-time Analysis of the Multiarmed Bandit Problem

status_evidence.title: Bandit Algorithms

partial_results.summary: UCB achieves O(sqrt(KT log T)) regret for K-armed bandits, nearly matching the Omega(sqrt(KT)) lower bound.
For linear bandits in d dimensions, the optimal regret is Theta(d*sqrt(T)) but structural assumptions affect constants.
For contextual bandits with general policy classes, tight bounds in terms of VC/Natarajan dimension remain open.

Determine instance-optimal regret for structured bandits

title: Natural Proofs Barrier Circumvention

statement.canonical: Razborov and Rudich (1997) showed that any proof of circuit lower bounds that is both "constructive" (can recognize hard functions efficiently) and "large" (applies to a large fraction of functions) cannot prove P != NP if one-way functions exist. Can we develop proof techniques that circumvent the natural proofs barrier to prove strong circuit lower bounds?
statement.informal: Most known techniques for proving circuit lower bounds have a special structure (they are "natural proofs") that provably cannot work to separate P from NP if cryptographic assumptions hold. Can we find fundamentally different proof strategies?

why_it_matters: The natural proofs barrier, together with relativization and algebrization, represents a fundamental obstacle to resolving P vs NP. Understanding how to circumvent this barrier is essential for making progress on the most important questions in complexity theory.

canonical.title: Natural Proofs

status_evidence.title: Natural Proofs versus Derandomization

partial_results.summary: Williams (2013) showed that certain algorithmic approaches to circuit lower bounds can avoid the natural proofs barrier.
Williams' NEXP not in ACC^0 proof (2011) circumvents the natural proofs barrier by using a non-natural proof technique based on satisfiability algorithms.
Carmosino, Impagliazzo, Kabanets, and Kolokolova showed connections between learning algorithms and natural proofs.

decomposition: Develop non-natural proof techniques for circuit lower bounds

title: NC vs P Problem

statement.canonical: Let NC denote the class of languages decidable by Boolean circuits of polylogarithmic depth and polynomial size (equivalently, problems solvable in polylogarithmic parallel time with polynomially many processors). Let P denote the class of polynomial-time decidable languages. Does NC = P?
statement.informal: Can every polynomial-time problem be efficiently parallelized? That is, can we always achieve a massive speedup by using many processors working in parallel?

why_it_matters: The NC vs P question captures whether inherently sequential computation exists. If NC != P, there are problems that can be solved efficiently but cannot be significantly sped up through parallelism. P-complete problems like the circuit value problem are candidates for such inherently sequential problems.

canonical.title: On Simultaneous Resource Bounds

status_evidence.title: Limits to Parallel Computation: P-Completeness Theory

partial_results.summary: Many natural problems have been shown P-complete under NC reductions, including the circuit value problem, linear programming, and maximum flow.
NC^1 is known to be contained in L is contained in NL is contained in NC^2, but no separation between any adjacent pair is known.

machine_generated.summary: The NC vs P problem asks whether all polynomial-time problems can be efficiently parallelized, or whether inherently sequential computation exists.
decomposition: Prove circuit depth lower bounds for P-complete problems
Develop new techniques for showing problems require sequential computation

title: Nearest Neighbor Search in High Dimensions
aliases: Curse of dimensionality for NN search

statement.canonical: Given n points in d-dimensional space (with d potentially large), preprocess them into a data structure that answers exact nearest neighbor queries efficiently. Is there a data structure with polynomial space and query time polynomial in d and polylogarithmic in n for exact nearest neighbor search? Or does the curse of dimensionality make this fundamentally impossible?
statement.informal: Can we build efficient data structures for finding the closest point in a dataset when the data lives in a high-dimensional space? Current methods either use exponential space or have query time exponential in the dimension.

why_it_matters: Nearest neighbor search is fundamental to machine learning, databases, computer vision, and information retrieval. The curse of dimensionality is a major practical barrier, and understanding its theoretical limits would guide algorithm design.

canonical.title: Approximate Nearest Neighbors: Towards Removing the Curse of Dimensionality

status_evidence.title: Near-Optimal Hashing Algorithms for Approximate Nearest Neighbor in High Dimensions

For exact NN, all known methods suffer from exponential dependence on dimension in either space or time.
Andoni and Indyk (2008) achieved optimal LSH for approximate NN under l2 distance with c-approximation in n^(1/c^2 + o(1)) time.

title: NEXP vs BPP Problem
aliases: Nondeterministic exponential time vs bounded-error probabilistic polynomial time

statement.canonical: Let NEXP denote the class of languages decidable by a nondeterministic Turing machine in exponential time, and BPP denote the class of languages decidable by a probabilistic Turing machine in polynomial time with bounded error. Is NEXP different from BPP?
statement.informal: Can randomized polynomial-time algorithms solve all problems solvable by nondeterministic exponential-time machines? It is widely believed that NEXP is much more powerful than BPP, but a proof remains elusive.

why_it_matters: Separating NEXP from BPP would be a major advance in computational complexity, establishing that nondeterministic exponential time is strictly more powerful than randomized polynomial time. This is one of the frontier separation problems in complexity theory and is connected to circuit lower bounds and derandomization.

canonical.title: Non-Deterministic Exponential Time has Two-Prover Interactive Protocols

status_evidence.title: Non-Uniform ACC Circuit Lower Bounds

partial_results.summary: Williams (2011) proved NEXP is not in ACC^0, giving the strongest known circuit lower bound against NEXP.
Impagliazzo and Wigderson (1997) showed that if EXP != BPP, then BPP can be derandomized, but the converse separation for NEXP remains open.

title: Nisan-Wigderson Hypothesis

statement.canonical: The Nisan-Wigderson hypothesis posits the existence of a function in E = DTIME(2^O(n)) that requires circuits of size 2^Omega(n). This is a sufficient condition for constructing pseudorandom generators that derandomize BPP. Can this hypothesis be proven unconditionally?
statement.informal: Is there a problem solvable in exponential time that cannot be solved by any sub-exponential-size circuit? Proving this would show that randomness can be eliminated from efficient computation.

why_it_matters: The Nisan-Wigderson hypothesis is a standard hardness assumption in complexity theory. If true, it implies P = BPP and provides explicit pseudorandom generators. Proving it unconditionally would be a major breakthrough, essentially resolving the derandomization question.

canonical.title: Hardness vs Randomness

status_evidence.title: Randomness vs Time: Derandomization under a Uniform Assumption

partial_results.summary: Nisan and Wigderson (1994) proved that the hypothesis implies explicit PRGs that derandomize BPP.
The hypothesis is consistent with current knowledge but proving it requires proving circuit lower bounds beyond current techniques.

machine_generated.summary: The Nisan-Wigderson hypothesis conjectures the existence of hard functions in E that would enable complete derandomization of BPP via pseudorandom generators.
decomposition: Prove unconditional circuit lower bounds for functions in E
Develop alternative hardness assumptions sufficient for derandomization

title: NP vs co-NP Problem

statement.canonical: Let NP denote the class of languages for which membership can be verified in polynomial time given a suitable certificate, and let co-NP denote the class of languages whose complements are in NP (equivalently, languages for which non-membership can be verified in polynomial time). Does NP = co-NP?
statement.informal: For problems where "yes" answers have short proofs that can be checked quickly (NP problems), do "no" answers also always have short proofs that can be checked quickly? For example, we can quickly verify that a number is composite by exhibiting a factor, but can we always quickly verify that a number is prime by providing a short proof of primality? (This particular example was resolved — primality is in P — but the general question remains open.)

why_it_matters: The NP vs co-NP question is fundamental to our understanding of the structure of computational complexity. If NP != co-NP, it would immediately imply P != NP, since P is closed under complement. The question has deep implications for proof complexity: NP = co-NP would mean that every tautology has a polynomially bounded proof in some proof system. It also relates to the existence of optimal propositional proof systems and the foundations of cryptography.

canonical.title: The Relative Efficiency of Propositional Proof Systems
Introduction to the Theory of Computation

status_evidence.title: A Short History of Computational Complexity

partial_results.summary: If NP != co-NP, then P != NP. The converse is not known; P != NP does not obviously imply NP != co-NP.
Pratt showed that primality is in NP (via Pratt certificates), and later AKS showed primality is in P, but these results do not resolve the general NP vs co-NP question.
In proof complexity, superpolynomial lower bounds have been shown for restricted proof systems (resolution, bounded-depth Frege), but not for general proof systems.
Razborov proved exponential lower bounds on the size of resolution proofs for the pigeonhole principle, contributing to proof complexity understanding.

machine_generated.summary: The NP vs co-NP problem asks whether every problem with efficiently verifiable "yes" answers also has efficiently verifiable "no" answers, with deep implications for proof complexity and cryptography.
decomposition: Prove superpolynomial lower bounds for general propositional proof systems
Explore connections between proof complexity and circuit complexity to establish separations

title: Online Bipartite Matching Optimal Competitive Ratio

statement.canonical: In the online bipartite matching problem, vertices on one side arrive online and must be irrevocably matched to previously arrived vertices. Karp, Vazirani, and Vazirani (1990) achieved a competitive ratio of 1 - 1/e for the unweighted case. For the general weighted (edge-weighted) online bipartite matching problem, what is the optimal competitive ratio? Can 1 - 1/e be achieved in the weighted setting?
statement.informal: When items arrive one at a time and must be immediately and permanently assigned to available slots, how close to optimal can we get without knowing the future? The unweighted case is solved but the weighted version remains open.

why_it_matters: Online matching is central to the theory of online algorithms and has direct applications to ad auctions, resource allocation, and kidney exchange. Determining the optimal competitive ratio for weighted variants would advance both theory and practice.

canonical.title: An Optimal Algorithm for On-Line Bipartite Matching

status_evidence.title: Online Primal Dual Meets Online Matching with Stochastic Rewards

For the fully online model where both sides arrive, the optimal ratio is still open with current best around 0.5541.
Various stochastic and semi-online models have been studied with improved ratios.

title: Online Learning with Expert Advice Optimal Bounds

statement.canonical: In the prediction with expert advice setting, a learner must choose among n experts over T rounds. The minimax optimal regret is known to be Theta(sqrt(T log n)) for the basic setting. For structured variants (e.g., combinatorial experts, sleeping experts, experts with limited feedback), what are the exact minimax optimal regret bounds? Can we achieve computationally efficient algorithms matching the information-theoretic optimum?
statement.informal: When learning from experts over time, we want to perform nearly as well as the best expert in hindsight. For many natural variants of this problem, the exact optimal performance and efficient algorithms are still unknown.

why_it_matters: Online learning with expert advice is foundational to online optimization, machine learning, and game theory. Understanding the exact minimax rates for structured variants is essential for practical algorithm design.

canonical.title: Prediction, Learning, and Games

status_evidence.title: Introduction to Online Convex Optimization

The sleeping experts problem and bandit variants have gaps between known upper and lower bounds.

title: P vs NP Problem

statement.canonical: Let P denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time, and NP denote the class of languages decidable by a nondeterministic Turing machine in polynomial time (equivalently, languages for which a proposed solution can be verified in polynomial time). Does P = NP?
statement.informal: If a solution to a problem can be quickly verified (in polynomial time), can the solution itself always be quickly found (in polynomial time)? In other words, is efficient verification of answers just as powerful as efficient computation of answers?

why_it_matters: The P vs NP problem is widely regarded as the most important open problem in theoretical computer science and one of the seven Millennium Prize Problems. A proof that P = NP would revolutionize cryptography (rendering most current encryption insecure), optimization, artificial intelligence, and mathematics. A proof that P != NP would formally justify the belief that many important computational problems are inherently intractable and would place fundamental limits on what can be efficiently computed.

canonical.title: The Complexity of Theorem-Proving Procedures
canonical.locator: Proceedings of the 3rd Annual ACM Symposium on Theory of Computing, pp. 151-158

canonical.title: Reducibility Among Combinatorial Problems
P vs NP Problem — Clay Mathematics Institute

status_evidence.title: The Status of the P Versus NP Problem
P =? NP

partial_results.summary: Baker, Gill, and Solovay proved that there exist oracles A and B such that P^A = NP^A and P^B != NP^B, showing that any proof resolving P vs NP must be non-relativizing.
Razborov and Rudich showed that 'natural proofs' cannot prove P != NP if one-way functions exist, ruling out a large class of proof strategies.
Aaronson and Wigderson showed that the algebrization barrier further restricts the class of techniques that can resolve P vs NP.
Ryan Williams proved that NEXP is not in ACC^0, the strongest circuit lower bound known, using a connection between algorithms and circuit lower bounds.

machine_generated.summary: The P vs NP problem asks whether every problem whose solution can be verified in polynomial time can also be solved in polynomial time, and is the most important open question in computational complexity theory.

Explore connections between proof complexity, algebraic geometry, and combinatorial optimization

title: P vs PSPACE Problem

statement.canonical: Let P denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine in polynomial time, and let PSPACE denote the class of languages decidable by a deterministic Turing machine using polynomial space. It is known that P is contained in PSPACE. Does P = PSPACE? Equivalently, can every problem solvable with polynomial space also be solved in polynomial time?
statement.informal: We know that problems solvable quickly (polynomial time) can also be solved with limited memory (polynomial space). But is the reverse true? Can every problem that requires only a reasonable amount of memory also be solved quickly? Most researchers believe the answer is no, but this remains unproven.

why_it_matters: Resolving whether P = PSPACE would clarify the relationship between time and space as computational resources. PSPACE includes problems from game theory (determining optimal strategies in two-player games like generalized chess), formal verification, and planning. A separation would prove that there are problems solvable with limited memory that inherently require exponential time, establishing a fundamental gap between these two resources. Since NP is contained in PSPACE, proving P != PSPACE would immediately imply P != NP.

canonical.title: Relationships Between Nondeterministic and Deterministic Tape Complexities
canonical.locator: Journal of Computer and System Sciences, 4(2), pp. 177-192

canonical.title: Introduction to the Theory of Computation

status_evidence.title: Computational Complexity: A Modern Approach

partial_results.summary: By the time hierarchy theorem, P != EXPTIME, and by the space hierarchy theorem, PSPACE != EXPSPACE. However, no direct separation between P and PSPACE is known.

title: PAC Learning DNF Formulas

statement.canonical: Can polynomial-size DNF formulas (disjunctive normal form) be PAC learned in polynomial time under the uniform distribution? DNF formulas are a fundamental Boolean function class, and their learnability has been open since Valiant introduced the PAC model in 1984.
statement.informal: Can we efficiently learn any Boolean function expressible as a short OR-of-ANDs formula from random examples? This is one of the most basic open problems in computational learning theory.

why_it_matters: DNF learnability is the most prominent open problem in PAC learning theory. DNF formulas represent a natural and important concept class, and their learnability connects to fundamental questions about the power of learning algorithms.

canonical.title: A Theory of the Learnable

PAC learning polynomial-size DNF from random examples in polynomial time remains open.

title: Planarity Testing in Optimal Parallel Time

statement.canonical: Planarity testing (determining if a graph can be drawn in the plane without edge crossings) can be done in linear time sequentially (Hopcroft-Tarjan, 1974). Can planarity testing be done in O(log n) time with a linear number of processors (i.e., is it in NC^1 or even AC^1)? What is the optimal complexity for dynamic planarity testing under edge insertions and deletions?

why_it_matters: Planarity is one of the most natural graph properties, and understanding its complexity in parallel and dynamic settings connects to fundamental questions in circuit complexity and dynamic data structures.

Ramachandran and Reif (1994) showed planarity testing is in NC (can be parallelized), but the parallel depth is O(log^2 n).

title: Optimal Polygon Triangulation

statement.canonical: Chazelle (1991) gave a linear-time algorithm for triangulating a simple polygon, but the algorithm is extremely complex and has never been implemented. Is there a simple, practical linear-time algorithm for simple polygon triangulation? Can the linear-time result be extended to polygons with holes efficiently?

why_it_matters: Polygon triangulation is a fundamental primitive in computational geometry, computer graphics, and mesh generation. A practical linear-time algorithm would be both theoretically satisfying and practically useful.

title: Is the Polynomial Hierarchy Infinite?

statement.canonical: The polynomial hierarchy (PH) is defined as the union of the classes Sigma_k^P for k >= 0, where Sigma_0^P = P, and Sigma_{k+1}^P = NP^{Sigma_k^P}. The hierarchy is said to collapse to level k if Sigma_k^P = Sigma_{k+1}^P (equivalently, if PH = Sigma_k^P). Is the polynomial hierarchy infinite, i.e., is Sigma_k^P strictly contained in Sigma_{k+1}^P for every k >= 0?
statement.informal: The polynomial hierarchy is a tower of complexity classes built on top of P and NP, where each level adds another layer of alternating "guessing." Does this tower go on forever with each level being strictly more powerful than the one below it, or does it eventually flatten out (collapse) so that additional levels of guessing add no more power?

why_it_matters: The polynomial hierarchy is one of the central structures in computational complexity theory. Many natural computational problems are complete for various levels of PH. If PH collapses, it would have dramatic consequences for the landscape of complexity theory — for instance, if PH collapses to P, then P = NP. Conversely, proving PH is infinite would separate P from NP (since P = NP implies PH = P). The question connects to circuit complexity, counting complexity, and the relativization barrier.

canonical.title: The Polynomial-Time Hierarchy
The Equivalence Problem for Regular Expressions with Squaring Requires Exponential Space

status_evidence.title: Computational Complexity: A Modern Approach

partial_results.summary: Relative to a random oracle, the polynomial hierarchy is infinite with probability 1 (Babai, 1985; Cai, 1986).
Toda's theorem shows that PH is contained in P^{#P}, connecting the polynomial hierarchy to counting complexity.
Karp and Lipton showed that if NP is contained in P/poly, then PH collapses to Sigma_2^P.
If any Sigma_k^P-complete problem is also in Pi_k^P, then PH collapses to Sigma_k^P.

machine_generated.summary: This problem asks whether the polynomial hierarchy is strictly infinite, meaning each level is strictly more powerful than the previous one, which would imply P != NP.
decomposition: Develop techniques for separating levels of the polynomial hierarchy beyond oracle results
Connect circuit complexity lower bounds to structural separations within PH

title: Post-Quantum Secure Hash Functions
aliases: Hash function security against quantum attacks

statement.canonical: Can we construct collision-resistant hash functions that are provably secure against quantum adversaries, based on well-studied assumptions? Specifically, what is the exact quantum query complexity of collision-finding, and which lattice-based or code-based constructions provably resist quantum attacks with optimal parameters?
statement.informal: Are current hash functions secure against quantum computers? Can we build provably quantum-resistant hash functions from well-understood mathematical problems?

why_it_matters: Hash functions are ubiquitous in cryptography and security. Ensuring they remain secure in a post-quantum world is critical. While Grover's algorithm provides only a quadratic speedup for generic preimage search, the security of specific constructions and collision-resistance against quantum adversaries requires careful analysis.

canonical.title: Post-Quantum Cryptography

status_evidence.title: A Note on the Quantum Collision and k-Distinctness Problems

Zhandry (2015) showed that random oracles are collision-resistant against quantum adversaries making superposition queries.
NIST post-quantum standards include hash-based signatures but the exact security parameters for hash functions against quantum attacks remain debated.

decomposition: Determine tight quantum security bounds for standard hash function constructions
