title: PPAD-Completeness of Nash Equilibrium Variants

statement.canonical: While computing a Nash equilibrium in general games is PPAD-complete, many natural variants remain unresolved. What is the complexity of computing Nash equilibria in succinctly represented games, polymatrix games, or games with special structure? Is finding an approximate Nash equilibrium with inverse polynomial precision in PPAD-hard for all game representations?
statement.informal: We know computing exact Nash equilibria is hard (PPAD-complete), but for many structured or approximate versions the complexity is still unknown. What is the precise boundary of hardness for equilibrium computation?

why_it_matters: Nash equilibrium is the central solution concept in game theory. Understanding the precise complexity of computing equilibria in various settings has implications for economics, mechanism design, and multi-agent systems.

canonical.title: The Complexity of Computing a Nash Equilibrium

Chen, Deng, and Teng (2009) extended PPAD-completeness to 2-player games.
Rubinstein (2017) proved that computing an epsilon-approximate Nash equilibrium is PPAD-hard for epsilon = 1/poly(n) under ETH for PPAD.

title: Optimal Pseudorandom Generators from One-Way Functions
aliases: PRG from OWF with optimal seed length

statement.canonical: Håstad, Impagliazzo, Levin, and Luby (1999) proved that pseudorandom generators (PRGs) can be constructed from any one-way function. However, the construction incurs a significant overhead in seed length. Can we construct PRGs from one-way functions with optimal (linear or near-linear) seed length and polynomial security loss?
statement.informal: We know how to build pseudorandom generators from any one-way function, but the known construction is very inefficient. Can we make it practical?

why_it_matters: The HILL construction is one of the most fundamental results in cryptography but the polynomial blowup in seed length limits its practical applicability. An optimal construction would tighten the foundations of pseudorandomness.

canonical.title: A Pseudorandom Generator from Any One-Way Function

status_evidence.title: Characterizing Pseudoentropy and Simplifying Pseudorandom Generator Constructions

Vadhan and Zheng (2012) simplified the construction but did not significantly improve the seed length.
Haitner, Reingold, and Vadhan (2013) improved the construction but a large polynomial gap remains.

decomposition: Reduce the seed length overhead in the HILL construction
Prove lower bounds on the necessary seed length for black-box constructions

title: Proper PAC Learning Hardness

statement.canonical: In proper PAC learning, the hypothesis must come from the same concept class as the target. For which concept classes is proper learning strictly harder than improper learning? Specifically, are there concept classes that are improperly learnable in polynomial time but for which proper learning requires superpolynomial time (under standard complexity assumptions)?
statement.informal: When learning, does it matter if the hypothesis we output has the same form as the target function? Is it sometimes fundamentally easier to learn using a different representation?

why_it_matters: Understanding the gap between proper and improper learning illuminates the role of representation in learning. It connects to practical questions about model selection and the power of different hypothesis classes.

canonical.title: Computational Limitations on Learning from Examples

status_evidence.title: A Complete Characterization of Statistical Query Learning with Applications to Evolvability

partial_results.summary: Pitt and Valiant (1988) showed proper learning of some classes is NP-hard while improper learning is easy.
For intersection of halfspaces, proper learning is NP-hard but improper learning is open.
The exact characterization of the proper-improper gap remains incomplete.

decomposition: Characterize concept classes with strict proper-improper learning gaps

title: Public-Key Cryptography from One-Way Functions

statement.canonical: Can public-key encryption be constructed from one-way functions alone? One-way functions are known to imply private-key cryptography (pseudorandom generators, symmetric encryption, digital signatures), but it is unknown whether they suffice for public-key encryption or key exchange.
statement.informal: If hard-to-invert functions exist, does that automatically mean we can do public-key cryptography? We know hard-to-invert functions give us symmetric crypto, but it is open whether they give us asymmetric/public-key crypto.

why_it_matters: This is one of the most fundamental questions in cryptography. A positive answer would unify the foundations of cryptography under one-way functions. A negative answer (a black-box separation exists) would mean public-key crypto requires fundamentally different assumptions.

canonical.title: Limits on the Provable Consequences of One-Way Permutations

status_evidence.title: The Relationship between Public Key Encryption and Oblivious Transfer

partial_results.summary: Impagliazzo and Rudich (1989) proved that public-key encryption cannot be constructed from one-way functions via black-box reductions.
Non-black-box techniques (like those of Barak) leave open the possibility of non-black-box constructions.
The question remains open for both standard and non-black-box constructions.

title: QMA vs QCMA
aliases: Quantum proofs vs classical proofs for quantum verifiers

statement.canonical: QMA is the class of problems verifiable by a quantum computer given a quantum proof (witness), while QCMA is the same but restricted to classical proofs. Does QMA equal QCMA? That is, does allowing quantum proofs give strictly more verification power than classical proofs, when the verifier is quantum?
statement.informal: Do quantum proofs (witnesses) provide extra power compared to classical proofs when verified by a quantum computer? Can every quantum proof be replaced by a classical one?

why_it_matters: The QMA vs QCMA question probes whether quantum information provides inherent advantages for proof verification. It is analogous to fundamental questions about the nature of quantum information and has implications for quantum computation and quantum verification protocols.

Natarajan and Nirkhe (2023) showed oracle separation using ideas from quantum error correction.

title: Quantum Communication Complexity Advantages

statement.canonical: What is the largest possible separation between quantum and classical communication complexity for total Boolean functions? For partial functions, exponential separations are known (Raz 1999). For total functions, can quantum communication be exponentially smaller than classical communication, or is the advantage at most polynomial?
statement.informal: How much can quantum communication reduce the amount of information two parties need to exchange to compute a joint function? For all functions (not just specially constructed ones), is the quantum advantage limited?

why_it_matters: Understanding the power of quantum communication has implications for quantum networks, distributed quantum computing, and the foundations of quantum information. It also connects to important questions in circuit complexity and data structures.

status_evidence.title: Exponential Separation of Quantum Communication and Classical Information

title: Quantum Error Correction Threshold (Exact)

statement.canonical: The threshold theorem for quantum computation states that if the error rate per gate is below a threshold p_th, arbitrary long quantum computations can be performed with polynomial overhead. What is the exact value of p_th for the most general noise model? Current estimates range from 10^-4 to 10^-2 depending on the model and error correction code.
statement.informal: How noisy can quantum gates be while still allowing reliable quantum computation through error correction? We know a threshold exists, but its exact value depends on details that are not fully understood.

why_it_matters: The error correction threshold determines the engineering requirements for building a scalable quantum computer. A higher threshold would make quantum computing more practical; a lower one would demand more stringent hardware.

canonical.title: Fault-Tolerant Quantum Computation with Constant Error

status_evidence.title: Quantum Computing with Realistically Noisy Devices

partial_results.summary: Aharonov and Ben-Or (1997) first proved the threshold theorem, with threshold around 10^-6.
Knill (2005) showed thresholds as high as 3-5% are achievable with postselection and magic state distillation.
Surface codes achieve thresholds around 1% for local noise models.

title: Quantum Money from Standard Assumptions

statement.canonical: Can we construct a public-key quantum money scheme -- quantum states that can be publicly verified but not copied -- from standard cryptographic assumptions (e.g., LWE)? Wiesner's original private-key scheme is information-theoretically secure, but public-key quantum money from standard assumptions remains elusive.

why_it_matters: Quantum money would be the ultimate uncounterfeitable currency, leveraging the no-cloning theorem. A public-key scheme from standard assumptions would be a breakthrough at the intersection of quantum computing and cryptography.

title: Quantum PCP Conjecture

statement.canonical: The classical PCP theorem states that every language in NP has a probabilistically checkable proof (PCP) where the verifier reads only a constant number of bits of the proof. The quantum PCP conjecture asks for an analogous result in the quantum setting: is it QMA-hard to approximate the ground state energy of a local Hamiltonian to within a constant fraction of the total number of terms? Formally, does there exist a constant epsilon > 0 such that the following promise problem is QMA-hard: given a k-local Hamiltonian H = sum_{i=1}^m H_i on n qubits (where each H_i acts on at most k qubits and 0 <= H_i <= I), distinguish between the cases where the ground state energy is at most epsilon * m and at least (1 - epsilon) * m?
statement.informal: The classical PCP theorem says you can verify any mathematical proof by reading only a few random bits of it. The quantum PCP conjecture asks whether a similar phenomenon holds for quantum proofs: is it computationally hard even to approximately determine the lowest energy state of a quantum system, even when "approximately" means just getting the answer roughly right?

why_it_matters: The quantum PCP conjecture is considered one of the most important open problems in quantum complexity theory and quantum information. If true, it would have profound implications for condensed matter physics (implying that ground states of local Hamiltonians are generically hard to approximate), quantum error correction, quantum algorithms, and the hardness of approximation for quantum problems. It would be the quantum analogue of the PCP theorem, which revolutionized classical complexity theory and hardness of approximation. The conjecture also connects to the study of entanglement, area laws, and the structure of quantum ground states.

canonical.title: Guest Column: The Quantum PCP Conjecture
Quantum NP - A Survey

status_evidence.title: Quantum Hamiltonian Complexity

partial_results.summary: Kitaev showed that the local Hamiltonian problem is QMA-complete for 5-local Hamiltonians, later improved to 2-local (Kempe, Kitaev, Regev, 2006), establishing the quantum analogue of Cook's theorem.
Brandao and Harrow showed that the quantum PCP conjecture is false for the special case of dense local Hamiltonians, using a quantum de Finetti theorem.
Aharonov, Arad, Landau, and Vazirani proved a quantum analogue of gap amplification (the detectability lemma), a key ingredient in classical PCP proofs.
Eldar and Harrow showed that no-low-energy trivial states (NLTS) exist, proving a prerequisite conjecture for the quantum PCP conjecture.
Anshu, Breuckmann, and Nirkhe proved the NLTS conjecture using quantum LDPC codes, removing a major barrier to the quantum PCP conjecture.

machine_generated.summary: The quantum PCP conjecture asks whether approximating the ground state energy of a local Hamiltonian to constant precision is QMA-hard, which would be the quantum analog of the classical PCP theorem.
decomposition: Build on the NLTS theorem to construct quantum locally testable codes with constant soundness

title: Quantum Speedup for NP-Complete Problems

statement.canonical: Can quantum computers achieve more than a quadratic speedup (beyond Grover's algorithm) for NP-complete problems? statement.canonical: Can we formally prove that quantum computers can solve some problem asymptotically faster than any classical computer (i.e., prove an unconditional separation between BQP and BPP)? statement.canonical: Quantum walks provide a framework for quantum algorithms that has yielded speedups for search, element distinctness, and graph problems. title: Rectilinear Steiner Tree Problem

statement.canonical: The rectilinear Steiner tree problem asks for the shortest tree connecting a given set of points in the plane using only horizontal and vertical line segments, where additional Steiner points may be introduced. What is the best polynomial-time approximation ratio achievable? The current best is a PTAS by Arora (1998), but can the exact problem be solved faster in practice, and what are tight bounds for specific approximation schemes?

why_it_matters: The rectilinear Steiner tree problem is NP-hard and has direct applications in VLSI circuit design and network routing. Understanding its approximability and practical solvability is important for chip design optimization.

title: Secretary Problem Variants

statement.canonical: The matroid secretary conjecture (Babaioff, Immorlica, Kleinberg, 2007) states that for any matroid, there exists an O(1)-competitive online algorithm for the secretary problem defined over that matroid. That is, elements arrive in random order with adversarial weights, and the algorithm must select an independent set of maximum weight, achieving a constant fraction of the offline optimum. Is this true?
statement.informal: In the classical secretary problem, the best candidate can be found with probability 1/e. For generalizations involving complex combinatorial constraints (matroids), can we still achieve a constant-factor approximation?

why_it_matters: The matroid secretary conjecture connects online algorithm design with matroid theory and has applications in mechanism design, auction theory, and combinatorial optimization. Despite much progress, a constant-competitive algorithm for general matroids remains elusive.

canonical.title: Matroids, Secretary Problems, and Online Mechanisms

status_evidence.title: O(log log rank)-Competitive Ratio for the Matroid Secretary Problem

partial_results.summary: Babaioff, Immorlica, and Kleinberg (2007) proved O(1)-competitive algorithms for specific matroid classes (uniform, graphic, transversal).
Lachish (2014) achieved an O(log log rank)-competitive algorithm for general matroids.
The conjecture has been verified for many special cases but the general case remains open.

machine_generated.summary: The matroid secretary conjecture asks whether O(1)-competitive online algorithms exist for secretary problems over arbitrary matroids.
decomposition: Prove the conjecture for general matroids or find a counterexample
Improve the O(log log rank) competitive ratio toward O(1)

title: Sensitivity Conjecture Extensions

statement.canonical: Huang (2019) proved that the sensitivity s(f) of a Boolean function f satisfies s(f) >= sqrt(deg(f)), resolving the sensitivity conjecture. What are the tight relationships between sensitivity and other Boolean function complexity measures (block sensitivity, certificate complexity, decision tree depth, degree)? Specifically, is s(f) = Theta(bs(f))? What is the exact polynomial relationship?
statement.informal: The sensitivity conjecture was resolved, but the exact polynomial relationships between sensitivity and other measures of Boolean function complexity remain unknown. What are the tight bounds?

why_it_matters: Boolean function complexity measures (sensitivity, block sensitivity, degree, certificate complexity) are fundamental tools in theoretical computer science. Determining their exact relationships would complete our understanding of Boolean function structure.

canonical.title: Induced Subgraphs of Hypercubes and a Proof of the Sensitivity Conjecture

status_evidence.title: Open problems related to the sensitivity conjecture

partial_results.summary: Huang (2019) proved s(f) >= sqrt(deg(f)), confirming the sensitivity conjecture with a short spectral proof.
The best known upper bound is bs(f) <= 2^(s(f)-1), and the gap between sensitivity and block sensitivity can be at most exponential.
Tight quadratic separation between sensitivity and block sensitivity is known for specific functions.

machine_generated.summary: After the resolution of the sensitivity conjecture, open problems remain about the exact polynomial relationships between sensitivity and other Boolean function complexity measures.
decomposition: Determine the exact polynomial relationship between sensitivity and block sensitivity
Improve the known gaps between various complexity measures

title: Optimal Depth of Sorting Networks

statement.canonical: A sorting network is an oblivious comparison-based sorting algorithm with a fixed comparison pattern. The AKS network (Ajtai, Komlós, Szemerédi, 1983) achieves O(log n) depth, which is asymptotically optimal. However, the constant factor is enormous. What is the minimum depth sorting network for practical values of n? Can the constant in O(log n) be made practical?
statement.informal: We know sorting networks of depth O(log n) exist, but the known constructions have huge hidden constants. Can we build sorting networks with both optimal depth and practical constants?

why_it_matters: Sorting networks are important for parallel sorting and hardware implementations. The gap between the theoretical O(log n) optimum and practical constructions (like bitonic sort with O(log^2 n) depth but small constants) remains significant.

canonical.title: An O(n log n) Sorting Network

status_evidence.title: Zig-Zag Sort: A Simple Deterministic Data-Oblivious Sorting Algorithm Running in O(n log n) Time

partial_results.summary: AKS (1983) proved the existence of O(log n) depth sorting networks, but the constant factor exceeds 6000.
Paterson (1990) reduced the constant significantly but it remains impractical.
For specific small values of n, optimal depth networks have been found by exhaustive search.

machine_generated.summary: Finding sorting networks with optimal O(log n) depth and practical constant factors remains open, with a large gap between theory and practice.
decomposition: Reduce the constant factor in O(log n) depth sorting networks
Determine exact minimum depth for small practical values of n

title: Streaming Lower Bounds for Frequency Moments

statement.canonical: In the streaming model, a sequence of items from a universe of size m arrives one at a time. The k-th frequency moment F_k = sum_i (f_i)^k where f_i is the frequency of item i. For k > 2, what is the exact space complexity of (1+epsilon)-approximating F_k in a single pass? Are the known upper and lower bounds tight?
statement.informal: How much memory is needed to approximately compute statistical properties (frequency moments) of a data stream in one pass? For higher moments (k>2), the exact answer is still not fully understood.

why_it_matters: Streaming algorithms are fundamental for processing massive data sets. Frequency moment estimation is the canonical problem in streaming, and determining its exact space complexity would complete our understanding of the streaming model's power and limitations.

canonical.title: The Space Complexity of Approximating the Frequency Moments

status_evidence.title: Zero-One Frequency Laws

partial_results.summary: Alon, Matias, and Szegedy (1999) initiated the study and gave polynomial space algorithms for all frequency moments.
Indyk and Woodruff (2005) proved Omega(m^(1-2/k)) space lower bounds for F_k estimation for k > 2.
The gap between upper and lower bounds for general k and epsilon has been narrowed but not closed.

machine_generated.summary: Determining the exact space complexity of streaming frequency moment estimation for k > 2 remains open despite decades of progress.
decomposition: Close the gap between upper and lower bounds for F_k estimation space complexity
Develop tighter communication complexity lower bounds for streaming problems

title: Strongly Polynomial Algorithm for Linear Programming
aliases: Is LP in strongly polynomial time?

statement.canonical: A linear program (LP) is the problem of optimizing a linear objective function subject to linear inequality and equality constraints: minimize c^T x subject to Ax <= b, x >= 0, where A is an m x n matrix. A strongly polynomial algorithm is one whose running time is bounded by a polynomial in n and m alone, independent of the bit-lengths of the coefficients in A, b, and c. Known polynomial-time algorithms for LP (ellipsoid method, interior-point methods) are weakly polynomial — their running time depends on the bit-length of the input. Does there exist a strongly polynomial-time algorithm for linear programming?
statement.informal: Linear programming is the problem of finding the best outcome in a mathematical model with linear constraints. We know how to solve it efficiently, but all known methods have running times that depend on the sizes of the numbers in the input, not just on the number of variables and constraints. Can we find a method whose speed depends only on the number of variables and constraints?

why_it_matters: Linear programming is arguably the most important optimization problem, with applications throughout operations research, economics, engineering, and computer science. While weakly polynomial algorithms exist (Khachiyan's ellipsoid method, Karmarkar's interior point method), the existence of a strongly polynomial algorithm remains one of Smale's 18 problems for the 21st century. It is fundamental to understanding the complexity of combinatorial optimization and the boundary between discrete and continuous computation.

canonical.title: A Polynomial Algorithm in Linear Programming
Mathematical Problems for the Next Century

status_evidence.title: Towards a Genuinely Polynomial Algorithm for Linear Programming
A Bound for the Number of Different Basic Feasible Solutions Generated by the Simplex Method

partial_results.summary: Khachiyan's ellipsoid method (1979) and Karmarkar's interior point method (1984) gave weakly polynomial algorithms for LP, resolving the question of polynomial-time solvability but not strong polynomiality.
Tardos showed that the number of arithmetic operations can be bounded by a polynomial in n and m for LP with integer data, but each operation may involve large numbers.
Strongly polynomial algorithms exist for special cases: network flow, minimum cost flow (via combinatorial algorithms).
Dadush and Huiberts gave a condition-number-free algorithm using O(n^{2.5} log(n/delta)) pivots with high probability, but it is not strongly polynomial.

machine_generated.summary: This problem asks whether linear programming admits a strongly polynomial algorithm whose runtime depends only on the number of variables and constraints, not on the magnitudes of the input numbers.
decomposition: Design pivot rules or interior point variants that achieve iteration counts independent of input bit-length
Exploit combinatorial structure of LP feasible regions to bypass dependence on numerical precision

title: Sublinear Time Algorithms for Edit Distance

statement.canonical: What is the best approximation ratio achievable for edit distance (Levenshtein distance) between two strings of length n in truly subquadratic time? Can edit distance be approximated within a constant factor in near-linear time? Can it be estimated in sublinear time with reasonable approximation?
statement.informal: How fast can we approximately compute the edit distance between two strings? The exact problem takes quadratic time (and this is conditionally optimal), but can we get good approximations much faster, even without reading the entire input?

why_it_matters: Edit distance is fundamental to string processing, bioinformatics, and natural language processing. The quadratic barrier for exact computation (conditional on SETH) motivates the study of fast approximation algorithms. Achieving constant-factor approximation in near-linear time would be a major breakthrough.

canonical.title: Polylogarithmic Approximation for Edit Distance and the Asymmetric Query Complexity

status_evidence.title: Edit Distance in Near-Linear Time: it's a Constant Factor

partial_results.summary: Andoni, Krauthgamer, and Onak (2010) achieved polylogarithmic approximation in near-linear time.
Chakraborty, Das, Goldenberg, Koucky, and Saks (2018) obtained constant-factor approximation in truly subquadratic time.
Andoni and Nosatzki (2020) achieved constant-factor approximation in near-linear time for the asymmetric case.

machine_generated.summary: The sublinear edit distance problem asks for the best approximation ratio achievable in truly subquadratic or near-linear time for edit distance computation.
decomposition: Achieve constant-factor approximation in near-linear time for general edit distance
Determine whether (1+epsilon)-approximation is possible in subquadratic time

title: Structure of Total NP Search Problems (TFNP)

statement.canonical: TFNP is the class of total NP search problems, where a solution is guaranteed to exist. Important subclasses include PLS, PPAD, PPA, PPP, and CLS. Determine the complete structure of TFNP: are these subclasses all distinct? Is TFNP itself characterized by a syntactic complexity class?
statement.informal: For computational problems where a solution always exists (like finding a Nash equilibrium), how do the different complexity subclasses relate to each other? What is the full landscape of hardness for guaranteed-to-exist solutions?

why_it_matters: TFNP and its subclasses capture the complexity of fundamental problems in game theory (Nash equilibrium via PPAD), economics, and combinatorics. Understanding this landscape has practical implications for algorithmic mechanism design and equilibrium computation.

canonical.title: On Total Functions, Existence Theorems and Computational Complexity

status_evidence.title: The Hairy Ball Problem is PPAD-Complete

partial_results.summary: Daskalakis, Goldberg, and Papadimitriou (2009) proved that computing a Nash equilibrium is PPAD-complete, establishing PPAD as a fundamental class.
Fearnley, Gordon, Mehta, and Savani (2021) established the CLS-completeness of many continuous optimization problems.
Separations between TFNP subclasses have been proven relative to oracles, but unconditional separations remain open.

machine_generated.summary: Understanding the structure of TFNP, the class of total NP search problems, is a central open question connecting complexity theory, game theory, and economics.
decomposition: Prove unconditional separations between TFNP subclasses
Determine whether TFNP has a complete problem

title: Topological Quantum Computation Universality

statement.canonical: Can non-abelian anyons (specifically Fibonacci anyons or Ising anyons supplemented with additional operations) be physically realized to implement a universal set of quantum gates with topological protection? What is the exact computational power of topological quantum computation with specific anyon models?
statement.informal: Can we build quantum computers using exotic particles (anyons) whose quantum information is protected by topology? Which types of anyons give full quantum computing power?

why_it_matters: Topological quantum computation promises inherently fault-tolerant quantum computing by encoding information in topological properties that are immune to local noise. Determining which anyon models are computationally universal and physically realizable would guide experimental efforts.

canonical.title: Topological Quantum Computation

status_evidence.title: Non-Abelian Anyons and Topological Quantum Computation

partial_results.summary: Freedman, Kitaev, Larsen, and Wang (2003) proved that Fibonacci anyons yield universal quantum computation.
Ising anyons alone are not universal but can be supplemented to achieve universality.
Physical realization of non-abelian anyons remains experimentally challenging with recent progress in Majorana platforms.

machine_generated.summary: Determining which anyon models support universal topological quantum computation and whether they can be physically realized is a key open question bridging physics and computer science.
decomposition: Classify computational universality of physically realizable anyon models
Develop efficient compilation of quantum circuits into braiding operations

title: Union-Find Optimal Bounds

statement.canonical: The Union-Find (disjoint set) data structure supports union and find operations. Tarjan (1975) showed that union by rank with path compression achieves O(alpha(n)) amortized time per operation, where alpha is the inverse Ackermann function. Fredman and Saks (1989) proved an Omega(alpha(n)) lower bound in the cell probe model. Is this tight in more restrictive but natural models? What are the optimal bounds for concurrent and cache-oblivious variants?
statement.informal: The union-find data structure is essentially optimal for the pointer machine model, but what about for modern computing models that account for parallelism, caching, and concurrency?

why_it_matters: Union-Find is one of the most important data structures in computer science, used in algorithms for minimum spanning trees, connected components, and equivalence classes. Understanding its optimal performance in modern computational models has practical significance.

canonical.title: Efficiency of a Good but not Linear Set Union Algorithm

status_evidence.title: The Cell Probe Complexity of Dynamic Data Structures

partial_results.summary: Tarjan (1975) showed O(alpha(n)) amortized time per operation using union by rank and path compression.
Fredman and Saks (1989) proved matching Omega(alpha(n)) lower bound in the cell probe model.
Optimal bounds for concurrent and distributed union-find remain open.

machine_generated.summary: While the classical Union-Find problem is essentially solved, optimal bounds for concurrent, cache-oblivious, and distributed variants remain open.
decomposition: Determine optimal bounds for concurrent union-find
Analyze union-find in cache-oblivious and external memory models

title: Unique Games Conjecture

statement.canonical: For every constant epsilon > 0, the following promise problem is NP-hard: given a unique game instance (a constraint satisfaction problem where each constraint is a bijection between label sets of size k), distinguish between the case where at least (1 - epsilon) fraction of constraints can be satisfied and the case where at most epsilon fraction of constraints can be satisfied. Formally, for all epsilon > 0, there exists a label set size k = k(epsilon) such that it is NP-hard to (1 - epsilon, epsilon)-decide the Unique Label Cover problem with label set size k.
statement.informal: Consider a system of "unique" constraints where each constraint relates two variables via a one-to-one mapping over a set of labels. The conjecture says that even approximately deciding whether nearly all constraints can be satisfied (versus almost none can be) is computationally intractable (NP-hard).

why_it_matters: The Unique Games Conjecture (UGC), proposed by Subhash Khot in 2002, has become one of the central conjectures in computational complexity and approximation algorithms. If true, it would provide tight inapproximability results for a wide range of optimization problems, including Max-Cut, Vertex Cover, and constraint satisfaction problems. It would unify and strengthen many known hardness-of-approximation results. The conjecture led Khot to receive the Nevanlinna Prize in 2014 for its transformative impact on the field.

canonical.title: On the Power of Unique 2-Prover 1-Round Games
On the Unique Games Conjecture
canonical.locator: Proceedings of the ICM 2010, Vol. I, pp. 99-121

status_evidence.title: Hardness of Approximation

partial_results.summary: Khot, Kindler, Mossel, and O'Donnell showed that UGC implies the Goemans-Williamson SDP-based algorithm for Max-Cut is optimal.
Raghavendra showed that assuming UGC, the SDP relaxation gives the optimal approximation for every constraint satisfaction problem.
Arora, Barak, and Steurer gave a subexponential-time algorithm for unique games, showing that if the conjecture is true, NP-hard problems admit subexponential algorithms.
Khot and Moshkovitz proved a candidate reduction for the 2-to-2 conjecture (a weaker variant of UGC).
Barak, Kothari, and Steurer showed limitations of SDP approaches for unique games.

machine_generated.summary: The Unique Games Conjecture asserts that approximately solving unique constraint satisfaction problems is NP-hard, and if true, would yield tight hardness-of-approximation results for many optimization problems.
decomposition: Resolve the 2-to-2 Games Conjecture and extend to the full UGC
Determine whether subexponential-time algorithms for unique games can be improved to refute or confirm the conjecture

title: VP vs VNP Problem

statement.canonical: Let VP denote the class of polynomial families computable by arithmetic circuits of polynomial size, and VNP denote Valiant's class of polynomial families that are definable as exponential sums over VP families (analogous to NP). Does VP = VNP?
statement.informal: Is the permanent polynomial as easy to compute as the determinant? This is the algebraic analogue of P vs NP, asking whether every polynomial with a short description also has a short computation.

why_it_matters: VP vs VNP is the central problem of algebraic complexity theory, introduced by Valiant in 1979. The permanent vs determinant question has deep connections to combinatorics, representation theory, and algebraic geometry. Resolving VP != VNP is considered a necessary step toward P != NP.

canonical.title: Completeness Classes in Algebra

status_evidence.title: Completeness and Reduction in Algebraic Complexity Theory

partial_results.summary: Valiant (1979) proved the permanent is VNP-complete, so VP != VNP iff the permanent requires superpolynomial arithmetic circuits.
Mignon and Ressayre (2004) proved that any determinantal representation of the permanent requires exponential size over the reals.
Landsberg, Manivel, and Ressayre established lower bounds on determinantal complexity of the permanent using representation theory.

machine_generated.summary: VP vs VNP is Valiant's algebraic analogue of P vs NP, asking whether the permanent polynomial can be computed by polynomial-size arithmetic circuits.
decomposition: Prove superpolynomial arithmetic circuit lower bounds for the permanent
Develop connections between algebraic and Boolean complexity
